To ask the Minister of Education whether children diagnosed with dyslexia privately are offered the same support as those who have been diagnosed by an educational psychologist, or other statutory provision.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) provides support for children and young people with literacy difficulties, including dyslexia, through a needs led model of service delivery rather than on the basis of the source of diagnosis.Under the Local Impact Team (LIT) model, access to support is informed by the child’s presentation and level of educational need within the school context; not by whether a diagnosis has been obtained privately or through the Educational Psychology Service. A private diagnosis of dyslexia may be considered by schools and EA professionals as part of the wider evidence base to help inform understanding of a child or young person’s needs. Schools are supported to identify and respond to literacy needs through the Graduated Response Framework, with EA professionals working alongside schools to build capacity, provide advice and guidance, and deliver targeted interventions where appropriate.Within the LIT model, support for literacy related needs may include:advice and guidance to schools to inform classroom practice and planning.targeted, time limited interventions delivered or supported by EA LIT staff; andsignposting to appropriate resources or training.Support is provided on a consistent, equitable, and needs led basis, in accordance with the LIT model and the Graduated Response Framework.

To ask the Minister of Health whether the routine offering of postpartum physiotherapy will be included in the Women’s Health Action Plan.
answer_text: Postnatal recovery, including access to physiotherapy where clinically appropriate, is recognised as an important component of women’s health following childbirth. NICE guidelines NG194 (Postnatal Care) and NG210 (Pelvic Floor Dysfunction: prevention and non-surgical management), both of which are endorsed by my Department, recommend that women’s physical recovery should be assessed after birth and that additional support, including referral to physiotherapy, should be offered where needs are identified.In Northern Ireland, postpartum physiotherapy is currently available on a targeted, needs based basis, with referral commonly provided for women who experience severe perineal trauma, pelvic floor dysfunction, or ongoing physical symptoms following childbirth. Access to physiotherapy is therefore aligned with NICE guidance, which emphasises assessment, early identification of problems, and referral to appropriate support rather than routine provision.The Women’s Health Action Plan is focused on priority areas for raising awareness and improving women’s health outcomes across the life course, as well as supporting women to access appropriate care and advice within existing services.

To ask the Minister of Health whether the delegated nursing task review will also include a review of the delegated model within special schools.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the delegated nursing task under direct payment review.
answer_text: My Department commissioned the Northern Ireland Practice and Education Council for Nursing and Midwifery (NIPEC) to lead a review focusing on the delegation of healthcare interventions to social care practitioners employed by Trusts and third-party care providers and PAs employed through direct payments. The purpose of this work is to develop recommendations that enable people to live as independently as possible in their own homes. This work is guided by the principle of delivering the right care, in the right place, at the right time, by the right person - supported by timely, safe, and effective delegation of healthcare interventions. While such arrangements may in some cases be linked to direct payment packages, the NIPEC led work is concerned with ensuring safe delegation, appropriate governance and timely clinical support, rather than any review of direct payments.Given the complexity of the issues involved, additional time has been necessary to ensure the review is robust and fully informed. A draft of the final report has been received by the Department and will be subject to sign off by the Delegation of Healthcare Interventions Oversight Group in the coming weeks before being submitted for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the number of children awaiting an autism assessment, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Active Travel Delivery Plan.
answer_text: The Active Travel Delivery Plan is a significant piece of work setting out how my Department will prioritise and deliver active travel improvements in our towns and cities for the next 10 years and beyond.The public consultation on the draft Plan showed strong support for prioritising connections to local schools, town centres, and public transport interchanges. It also endorsed my Department’s high level design principles, that design will be people-centred, creating places that are welcoming and accessible to all, and that design will focus on the provision of high-quality infrastructure, appropriate to the situation, with safety at its heart. During the consultation, my Department also received valuable local input on the priority routes identified within the draft Plan which will influence the routes included in the final Plan.Officials provided written evidence to the Committee on 5 November 2025, followed by an oral evidence session on 18 February 2026. The Committee’s feedback, received on 3 March 2026, is currently being considered as officials finalise the Plan for my consideration.Given the importance of the Plan in terms of future investment and delivery of active travel, I will need to carefully consider the final Plan, however I hope to be in a position to publish it in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any waiting well initiatives, modelled on the Arthritis UK Waiting Well programme, are available for patients awaiting orthopaedic surgery.
answer_text: The Support While Waiting Grant Scheme will be funding 20 community and voluntary sector organisations, to put in place programmes to support patients on Health and Social Care (HSC) waiting lists across a range of specialties, including orthopaedics. These programmes are intended to provide support such as self-management, education and wellbeing interventions to help patients manage their condition while waiting for treatment. The names of the 20 organisations will be published when funding has been released by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when he will be publish the community rehabilitation framework.
answer_text: The development of a Community Rehabilitation Framework is currently at an early stage, with work focused on establishing key principles to underpin future service design and delivery.As rehabilitation spans many needs and settings, it requires a coordinated, multidisciplinary approach involving clinicians, community teams, and social care to ensure individuals receive timely and appropriate support throughout their recovery.My Department is engaging with key stakeholders including Health and Social Care Trusts and professional leads, to ensure the framework reflects best practice and meets the needs of patients across Northern Ireland.It is not possible to be definitive about the timescale at this stage, in recognition of the multi-faceted nature of this approach and the need to align with other key Departmental initiatives, such as the neighbourhood model that is currently under development. I remain committed to taking all actions to strengthen hospital services including rehabilitation services to improving outcomes for patients across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when he will publish the findings of the Consultation on the Health and Social Care Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any contingency measures in place following the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s decision to stand down new and review appointments within the Psychiatry of Old Age Service due to staffing concerns.
answer_text: I am aware of the Belfast Trust’s decision to temporarily stand down some of the routine appointments within the Psychiatry of Old Age (POA) Service as a result of significant and unforeseen staffing pressures. The Trust Care of Elderly Team continues to accommodate all new and review appointments which includes some memory assessment work.The Belfast Trust has put in place a number of contingency measures. The service remains operational for all urgent cases including for patients requiring immediate assessment or intervention. This includes prioritising medical cover arrangements for patients who are detained under the Mental Health Order within the hospital setting.POA nurses, social workers, psychologists, and community mental health staff continue to provide ongoing management, monitoring, and followup for existing service users.Targeted recruitment, retention measures, and the use of locum and agency staff are being progressed at pace to restore full service capacity as quickly as possible. Support from within existing teams is being used to stabilise the service with the of utilisation of additional programmed activities  and a retired nurse has been secured to support the hospital liaison service.The Trust is maintaining direct communication with primary care to ensure any patient presenting with deterioration, safeguarding concerns, or increased risk can be escalated rapidly.The Trust is working with other HSC Trusts to explore shortterm crossboundary support and to ensure that regional service resilience is maintained.My Department continues to engage closely with the Belfast Trust regarding the restoration of the full POA service and to ensure that appropriate clinical safeguards remain in place throughout this period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the workforce sustainability of the psychiatry workforce.
To ask the Minister of Health whether there is capacity across the Health and Social Care Trusts to deliver the Psychiatry of Old Age services in its entirety for the immediate future.
answer_text: The continued growth of the ageing population across Northern Ireland has led to increased demand for Psychiatry of Old Age (POA) services in recent years. This increasing demand presents us with the challenge of ensuring we continue to have the required expertise within our POA workforce to provide the required level of service provision.BHSCT I am aware of the recent challenges being experienced by BHSCT in delivering its POA service in its entirety. This has been due to the high level of vacancies in its consultant workforce. The Trust is operating with a number of locum consultant posts and to manage within a reduced capacity it has been necessary for the service to introduce a prioritisation of the current workload.  Within POA Community services, the team are prioritising the urgent and emergency referrals. Whilst the team continue to respond to the routine referrals, there may be some delay in response times. GPs and other referring colleagues have been advised to continue to refer patients as per normal procedure and all referrals will be triaged. My Department acknowledges the current vulnerabilities within the BHSCT POA service and is working with the Trust to bring further stability to the service. NHSCTIn the immediate term, NHSCT capacity to deliver the full range of POA related functions is stable but this stability is dependent upon workforce. Across Dementia diagnostic services most of the Psychiatry of Old Age Consultant input is currently provided by locum staff. SEHSCTSEHSCT Psychiatry of Old Age service is stable and workforce capacity is in line with the funded establishment. SHSCTThe Southern Trust have experienced workforce pressures particularly in regard to recruitment to Consultant Psychiatry posts and have offered a recruitment and retention premium in order to fill vacancies. The Trust has reconfigured their Older Person’s Mental Health Service in recent years, with the aim of maximising efficiency and managing service demand.  WHSCTWHSCT Psychiatry of Old Age service is stable and workforce capacity is in line with the funded establishment. However, similar to SHSCT, the Trust has had to offer the recruitment/retention premium in order to deliver the full Consultant Psychiatry service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale behind the proposed closure of services at Holy Cross Play and Learn Group.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for managing the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) and for ensuring that sufficient funded pre-school provision is available to meet demand in all areas. To remain in the PSEP settings must achieve a minimum threshold of eight applications in the admissions process.  In Stage One of the process Play and Learn Playgroup received a total of six applications and therefore in line with policy was considered for withdrawal from the PSEP by the Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG).The decision was subsequently taken by the EA PEG to withdraw Play and Learn Playgroup from the PSEP for the 2026/27 academic year due to receiving insufficient applications and there being sufficient alternative pre-school provision in the area.The withdrawal of the playgroup is for one year only and the setting will be invited to participate in the pre-school admissions process for the 2027/28 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 39193/22-27, whether his Department has reviewed the impact of the in-school provision available through the Graduated Response Framework and the Local Impact Teams.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will be implementing a 20mph speed limit in residential areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to assess options for restricting non-surgical aesthetic procedures for under 18s.
answer_text: There is currently no set minimum age a person can avail of these treatments in Northern Ireland. To effectively introduce and enforce such a minimum age threshold would require legislative change. In the short time remaining of this mandate, my Department has no current plans to introduce such legislation.  This issue could be considered as part of any potential development of future legislation in respect of the provision of Non-Surgical Cosmetic Products and Procedures, which would include an assessment of options for setting minimum age requirements in respect of accessing treatments.The risk to public safety posed by the unlawful distribution and misuse of unauthorised medicinal products within the non-surgical cosmetic sector in Northern Ireland remains an ongoing issue and my Department remains committed in its efforts to combat unlawful promotion, supply or misuse of medicines, including those identified for use in the non-surgical cosmetic sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there will be regional commissioning of post-diagnosis support for children who receive an autism diagnosis.
answer_text: Children’s Autism services are currently commissioned to offer post diagnostic intervention. Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) already offer post diagnostic support following an autism assessment. It is recognised that there is a deficit in provision both for assessment and post-diagnostic support and that investment is required across a range of services that would help meet the needs of the autistic population. The Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. Any decision to commission services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any obligations Health and Social Care Trusts have to ensure their unused or abandoned sites do not have a negative impact on the local community.
answer_text: The Department of Health has several estate policies designed to prevent negative impacts associated with vacant estate. This would include, by extension, negative impacts on the local community. The Department recognises that vacant estate can become a magnet for antisocial behaviour, as well as being an inefficient use of public sector resources. HSC Trusts are required to identify and release surplus assets without any delay. In addition, the Department requires HSC Trusts to maintain the external envelope of vacant buildings; maintain security on all premises; identify and report on any significant deficiency in health and safety, fire safety, or physical condition; and carry out inspections of land and property.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will reduce the cost of a renewal of a driver licence, for those who need a three-yearly renewal of a licence.
answer_text: The fees for driving licences in the north are prescribed in Schedule 2 to the Motor Vehicles (Driving Licences) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1996 and are set to reflect the administrative costs of processing applications. The duration of driving licences is determined by Article 15 of the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981. Under these provisions, applicants aged under 60 are ordinarily issued with a ten-year licence. Those aged 60 or over receive a licence valid either until their 70th birthday or for three years, whichever is longer. From age 70, all drivers move onto a free three-year renewal cycle.Medically restricted licences valid for one, two, or three years are also renewed free of charge. Given the legislative basis for both licence durations and associated fees, there are no plans to reduce the cost of renewing licences for applicants who receive a three-year licence before the age of 70.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the future use of the building and site at Alternative Angles Day Opportunities in Abbots Road, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: The former ‘New Way’ building (formerly Hill Croft School) forms part of a larger site owned by the Department of Health. This site is surplus to health requirements. The site and the existing building are managed by the Northern HSC Trust on behalf of the Department. Neither the Department nor the Trust have a future use for the existing building. The Department intends to dispose of the surplus site (including the existing building). Given the existence of a scheduled monument on the wider site, Departmental officials are currently working in partnership and collaboration with statutory partners to see if this land can be brought forward for social housing development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the cost of a driver licence renewal is in line with the cost of renewal in all UK regions.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) are responsible for the administration of driver licensing for those drivers who are resident in the north and the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Britain is responsible for the administration of driver licensing for drivers who are resident in England, Scotland and Wales. The fee for an ordinary driving licence renewal in the north is higher than in Britain. This difference in fees is largely attributable to the economies of scale associated with the volumes of licences issued, with DVA’s licensing costs spread across a much smaller number of licensing transactions.  Medical renewals and renewals for those aged 70 and over remain free of charge in both jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any obligations that Health and Social Care Trusts have to secure their unused sites that are in a state of disrepair to ensure public safety.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Waiting List Initiative for Breast Assessment Clinics, including any action taken specifically to reduce waiting times for urgent referrals.

To ask the Minister of Health whether (i) his Department; or (ii) the Health and Social Care Trusts, have engaged with Royal Mail in relation to patient appointments being missed due to delays in letter deliveries.
answer_text: My Department has not engaged with Royal Mail in relation to patients’ appointments being missed due to delays in postal deliveries. In respect of the Health and Social Care Trusts, Belfast Trust has advised that it holds regular contract meetings with Royal Mail as part of the overarching postal services contract. The issue of delayed post to patients and clients has been raised on several occasions at these meetings, and the Trust will continue to monitor the position.Northern and South Eastern Trusts have advised that no engagement has taken place with Royal Mail in relation patients’ appointments being missed due to delays in letter deliveries. Southern and Western Trusts have not engaged directly with Royal Mail. However, both Trusts use a contracted supplier, Postal Sort, which has recently approached Royal Mail regarding delivery issues on the Trusts’ behalf.To minimise any patient inconvenience, Health and Social Care Trusts operate a dual notification system for appointment reminders. In addition to issuing standard appointment letters, most services also provide text message reminders to notify patients of their upcoming appointments. In addition, the Encompass system is now fully operational across Northern Ireland. Through the My Care application, all patients and service users can securely access a subset of their health and social care record, including appointment information, clinic letters, medication details, and some test results.  Users can also view future appointments and track the progress of their treatment.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions he has taken to ensure that all schools are aware of the 2010 publication Guidelines for Management of Anaphylaxis in Educational Establishments.
answer_text: The 2010 Guidelines for Management of Anaphylaxis in Educational Establishments was superseded by Department of Health Guidance on the use of adrenaline auto-injectors (AAIs) in schools in Northern Ireland, updated October 2018.  This addendum is designed to be read in conjunction with Supporting Pupils with Medication Needs (2008) guidance and every school’s protocol or policy on use of the AAI should have regard to it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the roll-out of encompass.
answer_text: Encompass is now live in all Health and Social Care Trusts after a phased rollout completed in May 2025.  This introduces a single digital care record for every patient and service user and aims to improve patient care, safety, and efficiency by providing staff with access to vital information and allowing patients to view and manage their own health data via the My Care patient portal.  Stabilisation and optimisation activities are ongoing with additional elements delivered including Northern Ireland Specialist Transport and Retrieval (NISTAR) in July 2025 and Healthcare in Prisons in November 2025. In March 2026 the encompass upgrade will add new features, chosen by the five Health and Social Care Trusts. These new features will further optimise how the system works and continue to tailor it to Health and Social Care staff.Further areas including Mortality & Morbidity Pathways, Child Health System Replacement and Children’s Social Care are under development and will be implemented over the coming year along with further enhancements to the My Care portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: The Dilapidation Bill is currently in Committee Stage, which has been extended to 15 May 2026 to allow the AERA Committee adequate time to scrutinise the Bill. 		Oral evidence sessions concluded on 22 January 2026 and the Committee has now begun its deliberations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when figures from encompass will be considered official statistics, rather than official statistics in development.
answer_text: Published figures sourced from new sources such as encompass are considered to be ‘official statistics in development’, which are a subset of Official Statistics in line with the Code of Practice for Statistics while their accuracy and trustworthiness is being refined. Caution must be exercised when using these figures, as they may not be directly comparable with legacy (pre-encompass) data. They are however judged to give a meaningful representation of what they measure and are of sufficient quality for publication and use.Figures sourced from encompass will continue to be published as ‘official statistics in development’ until appropriate validations have been completed and the department is satisfied, they meet the robust criteria for official statistics standards. The quality assurance of encompass data for official statistics purposes are ongoing. Furthermore, a review of the Official Statistics status will be considered for each statistical output separately, depending on the progress of their specific data validations.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work undertaken alongside the Minister of Education to ensure that schools have access to life-saving allergy medication.
answer_text: From 1 October 2017 an amendment to the Human Medicines (Amendment) Regulations (HMR) 2017 has allowed schools to buy adrenaline auto-injector (AAI) devices without a prescription, for emergency use in children who are at risk of anaphylaxis but whose own device is not available or not working.To reflect this change guidance was issued by the Chief Medical Officer, ‘Guidance on the Use of Adrenaline Auto-Injectors in Schools (HSS(MD)21/2017))’. This is turn acted as an addendum to ‘Supporting Pupils with Medication Needs’ (2008). This guidance remains extant.As an enabler to this the DoH Medicines Regulatory Group and the then Health and Social Care Board (HSCB) jointly issued a summary of the changes to community pharmacies (Adrenaline Auto-Injectors in Schools, 13 October 2018), as community pharmacies are the primary supply routeMy officials have contributed to the development of the ‘Supporting Pupils with Medication Needs’ guidance, and to a review of the guidance led by the Department of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work undertaken to increase weekly allowances for foster carers.
answer_text: The allowances paid to foster carers in Northern Ireland (NI) are for costs associated with day to day care; and are outlined within the annual published Fostering Model Scheme. The Department reviews the allowances payable to foster carers on an annual basis in respect of the decision for an annual uplift to apply to these allowances. The annual review considers rates of inflation, the rates of allowances payable in other UK jurisdictions, and what is affordable within the financial envelope available to the Department. The current published allowance rates are 2024-25 rates, and these include the annual uplift increase of 3% which was applied to foster care allowance rates for the 2024-25 financial year. The current allowances rates are published on the Department of Health website and are available to view at Foster care allowances | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk). Consideration of an annual uplift to apply to allowance rates payable for 2025-26 is currently under review, and foster carers will be notified of the outcome as soon as possible. Payment of any uplift to rates for 2025-26 will be backdated to April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any of the Health and Social Care Trusts have received accreditation as a British Society of Gynaecological Endoscopy endometriosis centre.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts provide care for endometriosis, with women referred under the care of a consultant gynaecologist. Gynaecology services are delivered through multidisciplinary teams to ensure holistic, responsive, and appropriate care throughout the patient journey. Each team member—whether a consultant, nurse, or other professional—functions as a specialist within their discipline. Plans and investment for endometriosis services will commence in 2026. Business Cases relating to both the Regional Centres and local service models are expected to be submitted to the Department by March 2026 for consideration.More complex endometriosis care will be provided in the Belfast and Western Trusts, supported by multidisciplinary teams of surgeons, including colorectal and urological consultants.As part of these plans Belfast Trust will be applying for accreditation as a specialist centre and Western Trust are seeking reaccreditation, with Business Cases expected late March 26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the pilot programme to transfer eligible endometriosis patients from secondary to primary care, due to be launched in the Belfast and South Eastern Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The pilot programme has focused on patients with the longest routine waiting times within Trusts who are clinically suitable for transfer to the General Practice Elective Care Service. These patients are reviewed by GPs with enhanced skills in gynaecology.As part of this pilot, the General Practice Elective Care Service has accepted just over 300 patients from the South Eastern Trust, transferring appropriate long-wait patients from secondary care into a primary care setting.The primary care service is currently working through this cohort of patients and continues to operate in close collaboration with Trust teams to ensure safe and effective management.At present, no patient has been transferred as yet from the Belfast Trust. However, approximately 750 patients have been transferred from the Northern, Western, and Southern Trusts as part of this regional initiative.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has undertaken any scoping exercise looking at the potential for foster carers to provide respite or short breaks for children with complex needs, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: I can confirm that there are two HSC Trusts who already have well established Short Break Fostering Services for children with disability that have been delivering valuable short break opportunities, and which are being further expanded via the investment to the children with disability service. For those HSC Trusts where this type of specific Short Break Fostering Service has not previously been established – the CWD Funding is being used to develop this service. At present, all five HSC Trusts have commenced developing or are already further expanding specific Fostering Short Breaks Services for children with disability. However, these services take time to establish - with both the recruitment of workforce and foster carers required.The benefits for families and children arising through short breaks fostering include the experience of receiving consistent, safe care in a family setting, developing strong trusting relationships, being open to new experiences and independence, development of social opportunities, and support with emotional wellbeing and regulation. The individual needs of the young person are recognized to be a priority within the context of matching young people to potential carers.Short Break Fostering can support parents and carers by reducing carer burnout, strengthening family resilience and building a dynamic and responsive support network around families. This form of short break provision will be an integral feature in ongoing work to enhance the Children with Disability service moving forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to undertake any work focused on expanding fostering services, similar to the plan published in England on 3 February 2026.
answer_text: The ‘Renewing Fostering: homes for 10,000 more children’ report was published by the Department of Education (DfE) in England on 3 February 2026 along with a consultation on proposed changes to the assessment and approval of foster carers and improvements to the handling of allegations of abuse.  DfE has also published a call for evidence for further improving the foster care system.Many of the themes identified in the report are also relevant to Northern Ireland, including the need for improvements in; recruitment and retention of foster carers, regional collaboration, data collection and support for foster carers.  Many of these themes were reflected in the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services, which made several recommendations in relation to foster care.  As part of the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme, established in response to the issues which led to the Independent Review, there is a wider programme of reform underway to address a range of challenges across children’s social care which includes provision of foster care.  This has resulted in a number of specific actions which it is anticipated will contribute to the expansion of foster care services in Northern Ireland and address the recommendations of the Independent Review.A Foster Care Strategic Plan is currently being drafted, which aims to outline how HSC Trusts will recruit and retain foster carers.  This will inform the future direction of services, by setting out plans to expand and strengthen both mainstream and specialist foster care provision.  It is currently aimed to finalise the plan in mid 2026. In advance of the plan and to align with it, work is already progressing on increasing the regionalisation of recruitment of foster carers across Northern Ireland and developing more support for foster carers. My Department is nearing completion on a new regional procedure for managing concerns, complaints and allegations made in respect of foster carers in Northern Ireland.A new scheme – Foster Care Ambassador - has been developed following research of similar models in the UK and is in the early stages of implementation across HSC Trusts.    A foster care ambassador is an approved, experienced foster carer, and partnership working between the ambassador and the fostering social worker is considered crucial in supporting potential new foster carers through the assessment and approval process. The aim of the scheme is to provide end to end support to new fostering applicants, from point of enquiry to approval at Foster Panel, and ultimately to help improve the number of initial enquiries which result in approvals of new foster carers.I am also bringing forward new legislative and regulatory arrangements, to streamline the foster carer assessment process and remove profit from foster care services.  I have already approved the making of the new foster regulations, to be brought into operation during 2026/27, which also introduce a requireme

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any current guidance or policy around the labelling of food products that contain alcohol.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, the Food Standards Agency has policy responsibility for food information to consumers rules. Alcoholic beverages containing more than 1.2 % alcohol, must indicate alcoholic strength. Alcoholic beverages are exempt from certain mandatory labelling requirements, such as nutrition declarations and ingredients.		All prepacked food must have a list of ingredients. If an ingredient with an alcoholic content is included in a food, then it must be included in the ingredients list.  	If an ingredient containing alcohol appears in the name of the food or is emphasised on the labelling, this must be declared in the ingredients list, along with a quantitative declaration.  		For non-prepacked food, including foods sold loose in catering establishments, there is no mandatory requirement to provide a list of ingredients. However, if provided, it must not mislead.		DAERA has responsibility for policy relating to the definition, description, presentation and labelling of spirit drinks. EU Regulations applicable in Northern Ireland contain provisions which apply when spirit drinks are used in food. If a food label alludes, either directly or indirectly, to the legal name of one or more spirit drinks, such as whiskey or rum, or to a Geographical Indication such as Irish Whiskey, all alcohol present in that food should come exclusively from those spirit drinks. The product can also include alcohol that is in flavourings, colours or other authorised ingredients.		Detailed guidance on the labelling of alcoholic drinks can be found online. For spirit drinks, please refer to: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/labelling-spirit-drinks. Guidance on wine labelling is available at: https://www.food.gov.uk/business-guidance/wine-labelling.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why Lindsay House, in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, has not reopened for short breaks, despite the registration and opening of Redwood Children with Disability Residential Facility from January 2026.
answer_text: Redwood Children’s Home, was issued with registration from RQIA during December 2025.The current registration allows for one placement to be used as a shared care arrangement. This will be revisited by RQIA when the home is fully staffed and internal works to the stair case are completed. The South Eastern Trust plans to have these works completed by the 22nd of February 2026 and to be fully staffed by the first week in March 2026. Currently Redwood accommodates one young person on a shared care basis for three nights per week. The family and the young person report positively upon this shared care arrangement. The second planned admission to Redwood will be in respect of one of the young people currently residing in Lindsay house. The final planned admission to Redwood is for one young person who will have a shared care arrangement. The South Eastern Trust continues to work with RQIA and officials within my Department to facilitate the re-opening of Lindsay House for short breaks. This is a complex undertaking, requiring a focus upon the individual needs of the young people currently placed in Lindsay House. These discussions are taking place against a background where options for transitions to alternative placements are also limited.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 38111/22-27 and when enforcement is not successful, to detail any steps her Department can take for ensuring the road is (i) brought up to the standard required; and (ii) adopted by her Department.
answer_text: All private streets sites are the responsibility of the developer until they are formally adopted into the public road network. However, my officials continue to engage with developers and encourage them to complete the sites to the required specification and proceed to adoption.Last year I gave a commitment to the Assembly to provide updated Private Streets Enforcement Guidance to staff in an effort to reduce the risk of developments entering the Department’s backlog of unadopted private streets sites.On the 25th of September I announced the introduction of the new Private Streets Enforcement Guidance. The guidance establishes a threshold for the initiation of enforcement action of 2 years following 80% occupation of houses in a development.My Department has also published new developer information which amends the bond release thresholds. This change will enable my department to retain a larger proportion of the bond which provide the funds required to bring new developments roads infrastructure to an adoptable standard should enforcement action be necessary.The new guidance, implemented alongside the changes to the bond release thresholds, will protect homeowners by greatly reducing the risk of new developments remaining unadopted.My Department may consider enforcement when a developer has defaulted through the issuing of a Notice under Article 11 of the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980, outlining the outstanding work, and allowing the developer to take action towards completion of the site within 28 days The developer has a right to appeal under Article 12.In instances where there is non-compliance with the Article 11 Notice, my Department may take forward the necessary works and recover the costs under the provisions of Article 15 and adopt the road on completion of the work.If the Developer does not pay costs as detailed in Department's request or the developer is no longer trading, the Article 15 demand is served to the appropriate Bank or Insurance Company acting as surety for payment of costs incurred in bringing the development to an adoptable standard.If a site is not brought to an adoptable standard by the developer, and the funds in the bond are insufficient to complete the infrastructure to an adoptable standard, my Department are not funded to carry out such works. My Department will continue to communicate with and encourage developers to have sites brought to an adoptable standard at the earliest date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the adoption of Ben Madigan Hill road, Belfast, and (ii) whether the enhanced enforcement powers, announced in September 2025, have aided the adoption process in this instance.
answer_text: The private development at Ben Madigan Hill Road, Belfast is being undertaken by the developer Kilmoon Trading Ltd. For the development to be adopted by my Department, the Developer is required to complete the works to meet the required standards. The development is substantially complete and was placed on a 1-year maintenance period in March 2025. Since the current maintenance period was issued, a defect to the Vehicle Restraint System has arisen. The development was inspected on 28 January 2026 and a list of defects that included remedial works to the Vehicle Restraint System were forwarded to the developer later that day. As this is a Developer led scheme, the responsibility for addressing the outstanding matters remains with the Developer. Final adoption can be considered after the 1-year maintenance period and the completion of any remedial works necessary to bring the road to the appropriate standards. The timescales for adoption are therefore dictated by the Developer.The enhanced enforcement powers, announced in September 2025, have not aided the adoption process in this instance due to the very recent nature of the defects. Officials are in contact with the developer and will ensure the recent defects are addressed prior to adoption.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any evidence that his Department has received that contradicts current advice on minimum unit pricing of alcohol.
answer_text: It is important to note from the outset that alcohol consumption causes real harm to individuals, families and communities in Northern Ireland. Minimum Unit Pricing is not a ‘silver bullet’, but is a proven, effective tool to reduce alcohol harms.All evidence that the Department has received points to the fact that drinking above the safe limits can have a negative impact on people’s physical and mental health, on family relationships, on our local communities, on crime and community safety, and on our economy. Research commissioned by my Department estimates that alcohol misuse costs Northern Ireland up to £900 million every year.There is a range of research, evidence, guidance, modelling, and evaluations that policies which affect alcohol pricing, and Minimum Unit Pricing, is likely to be effective including:WHO reports;Meta analysis;Modelling undertaken in England, Scotland, Ireland and Northern Ireland based on a sales, consumption, and harm data; andEvaluations in Scotland and Wales.Overall, the evidence is that MUP: reduces consumption (particularly among those who drink the most); that the main impact is felt by hazardous or harmful drinkers; reduces harm as measured by hospital admissions and alcohol related mortality and can be easily implemented.MUP is not a panacea for all the harms relating to alcohol use, and there continues to be a need for investment in services, particularly for those with alcohol dependence. However, in my view MUP is a highly targeted policy which can make a meaningful difference for the health service, but more importantly will reduce the harm for individuals, families and across the region.However, such a policy requires the support of the NI Executive, and I continue to await formal confirmation of the position of all my Executive colleagues. Should Executive support not be forthcoming, I will be required to make a statement to that effect to the Assembly under the Licensing & Registrations of Clubs (Amendment) Act (NI), legislation to the Assembly by 6 April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's (i) enforcement powers; and (ii) policy, for tackling parking on (a) cycle lanes; (b) bus lanes; and (iii) bus stops.
answer_text: I refer the Member to AQW 38972 / 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of Departments that have responded in favour to the proposed Minimum Unit Pricing of Alcohol bill.
answer_text: As I have made clear, I believe that Minimum Unit Pricing for Alcohol could be a key mechanism to reduce alcohol-related harm in Northern Ireland and to address the health inequalities that exist in this area.However, such a policy requires the support of the NI Executive, and I continue to await formal confirmation of the position of my Executive colleagues. Executive business is confidential.  Prior to the 6 April 2026, I am required to either bring forward legislation to set a minimum unit price for alcohol or make a statement to the Assembly on why it has not been reasonably practicable to do so. This requirement is provided in the Licensing & Registrations of Clubs (Amendment) Act (NI) 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department could facilitate automatic updates on fault reports, on its fault reporting software on the website.
answer_text: My Department receives approximately 90,000 road faults and 60,000 lighting faults annually.  The system for reporting such faults was first introduced by the Department in 2015 and is now available for members of the public to report instances directly using this online system or via the NI Direct contact centre.When a fault is reported, the customer receives an automated acknowledgement email which provides an indication of the priority of the fault. The customer is also provided with a webpage link and reference number to facilitate the tracking of the fault.Customers can also use this link to identify the status of the fault, any results of investigation and where possible an indication of the timescale for repair.Whilst officials will keep any potential updates to the system that could improve the process under review, there are currently no plans to change the tracking system at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the refurbishment and use of Chestnut Grove in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, in the last four years; and (ii) whether this site has been considered for use for respite services.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has advised that:Chestnut Grove is a residential facility used to deliver short term bed-based rehabilitation. It is currently undergoing a programme of major refurbishment works which commenced in December 2024 and will complete in December 2026.This facility has not been considered for provision of respite services. The facility has been used as an intermediate care facility since 2015 where patients discharging from acute hospital sites receive a period of rehabilitation before discharging to their home.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether those children who were no longer seen as on the edge of care, in the last 12 months, were no longer assessed as such because they are getting their assessed needs met.
answer_text: The strategy for Looked After Children ‘A Life Deserved: ‘Caring for Children & Young People in Northern Ireland’ (2021) outlines that ‘edge of care’ means one of a number of things:Living in Families with intense needs and requiring intensive supports [on the edge of coming into care];Returning home from a period of care [on the edge of a [short] period in care];Leaving care to make the journey into adult life [on the edge of ageing out of care].It also applies to children and young people who have left care and are still in need of some support; including those who have been adopted; those who are living in a family under a Private Law Order; and those who are supported in independent living in early adulthood. There is no single, formal, regional assessment process specifically called an “edgeofcare assessment” in Northern Ireland.Trust social work staff enact a range of functions within the context of the core legislation and policy for all children who may require support, protection, or entry into care. The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, sets out statutory duties for assessing children’s needs and safeguarding welfare. Health and Social Care Trusts conduct assessments of need using the Understanding the Needs of Children in Northern Ireland, (UNOCINI) assessment framework, when a child or family requires support or where a  safeguarding concern is identified. This is the regional mechanism for determining risk and required interventions. If concerns escalate, Trusts follow the established child in need and child protection procedures which guide decisions about whether a child should remain at home with support or move into care.The individual circumstances of any child, including children with disability, complex needs and co-morbid diagnoses will be considered by the assessing social worker within the same multi-disciplinary assessment process as outlined above.Social Workers are required to adopt a trauma informed approach to families experiencing elevated levels of need and to reflect in their decision making upon the central importance of establishing authentic partnerships with parents at times of increased stress and challenge. When a parent is expressing the need for their child to be admitted to residential care as a direct result of the intensity of presentation at home, the child is considered to be on the edge of care. SPPG collates information from each of the Health and Social Care Trusts on a monthly basis in relation to the number of children and young people with a disability deemed to be on the edge of care. However, specific data is not collected in relation to the number of children assessed as being on the edge of care in the last 12 months who have moved to adult services.As detailed above SPPG collates information in respect of children and young people with a disability deemed to be on the edge of care and these numbers are showing a decrease in recent months. This is reflective of the impact of the

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW37719/22-27, whether the Emotional Health and Wellbeing framework will address the issue of lack of obligated support, following an autism diagnosis.
answer_text: Each child diagnosed with autism will have individual needs and will require a person-centred support plan to meet those needs. Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) offer post diagnostic support following an autism assessment. The Autism Act does not define or identify obligated support other than post diagnostic support. The Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework has been developed to support fully inclusive needs-based care pathways for children and young people. The Framework aims to identify the roles and responsibilities of everyone who has input to supporting children and young people pre and post assessment and regardless of diagnostic outcome. This will help identify gaps in service and co-produce priority areas for service development and future funding.  Autistic people should have equal access to all HSC services and to support this the Department has recently developed Neuro Diversity Awareness training that is available to all HSC staff. This should help staff to make all healthcare environments more accessible and welcoming for autistic people. It is recognised that there is a deficit in provision both for assessment and post-diagnostic support and that investment is required across a range of services that would help meet the needs of the population including the autistic population. The Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. Any decision to commission services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 38523/22-27, whether his Department, and the Education Authority, plan to make the training programme for teachers mandatory.
answer_text: I have no plans, at this stage, to introduce mandatory autism training for teachers.
