answer_text: I have not met with Parents for Inclusive Education Northern since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any organisations he has met with (a) online; and (b) in another setting, to discuss the Supreme Court judgment in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant), or mandatory Christian-based education in schools, since 2023; and (ii) whether they met (a) before the court's ruling; and (b) following the court's ruling.
answer_text: I met with the Transferor Representatives’ Council (TRC) on 1 April 2025.  The meeting covered a number of issues, including the matter of JR87.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the (i) active travel; and (ii) connectivity, plans for the Sailortown area, North Belfast.
answer_text: My Department is currently progressing a number of Active Travel projects that will improve connectivity for the Sailortown area of North Belfast. As part of the Belfast Cycling Network, the Sailortown Pedestrian and Cycle Improvement Scheme aims to provide new high quality active travel infrastructure connecting York Street Train Station, Ulster University and the Cathedral Quarter via Dock Street, Corporation Street and Great Patrick Street. Detailed design is ongoing following positive initial public engagement in October 2025, and my Department plans to commence the statutory process required to legislate the new cycle route later this year, once the design has been suitably developed.In 2024 my Department also allocated £0.3m to Belfast City Council to progress the preliminary scheme development for a potential Titanic/Sailortown bridge. Provision of a new cross harbour pedestrian and cycle bridge at this location would provide a continuous active travel corridor along both sides of the Lagan, improving connections between the Sailortown Community and Queen’s Island/Titanic Quarter. The Council is planning to commence stakeholder engagement in 2026, including engagement on the preferred bridge option.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they have had with the Diocese of Down and Connor regarding the safe keeping of records in line with legislation, particularly in regards to future access for any inquiry or research into Mother and Baby Homes, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Executive Office appointed PRONI to survey, catalogue, digitise and preserve institutional records. To date, over 5,500 items amounting to more than 50,000 digitised pages, have been processed. This includes material provided by the Diocese of Down and Connor, includes records relating to Milltown Cemetery registers.PRONI has also worked closely with the non-statutory Truth Recovery Independent Panel, appointed by The Executive Office in April 2023. The Panel is expected to report in the coming months.All the records and research will support the statutory Public Inquiry scheduled to begin in 2026. More broadly, the Preservation of Documents (Historical Institutions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 sets out the responsibilities of record holders.These steps represent significant progress in preserving vital records and enabling individuals, and society more widely, to better understand our recent past.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any contact they have had with the department for Children, Disability and Equality, to learn from the current excavation at the Tuam site, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The need for the excavation of the Tuam site was driven by oral testimony but also the examination of differences between death and burial records.  The Truth Recovery Independent Panel have taken testimony of victims, survivors and others and will consider any evidence or records in relation to unmarked graves. The Panel’s report is due to be completed in the coming months and will help inform the statutory Public Inquiry.Our officials had previously met with their counterparts in the Department for Children, Disability and Equality to discuss the context in which it was necessary for the Government of Ireland to bring forward the Institutional Burials Act 2022. This is a complex and detailed piece of legislation which is being used to enable the excavation work at Tuam.Ministers via North South Ministerial Council (NSMC) meetings have covered key issues in relation to Mother and Baby Institutions. This engagement will continue in relation to records, benefit disregards and other issues as needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department's role in the Children & Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework, led by the Department of Health, in regards to in-school services for young people awaiting an autism diagnosis.
answer_text: My department has engaged with the Department of Health regarding the Framework and will continue to do so.In-school services for children and young people awaiting an autism diagnosis are already in place via the Graduated Response Framework and the Local Impact Teams, as part of the SEN Reform Delivery Agenda.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether a Business Growth Accelerator will be available for businesses, for the incoming financial year.
answer_text: I announced this proposal in November on foot of intensive engagement with businesses and have committed to bringing a consultation on the measure later this year. That process will seek views on the policy options for using the rating system to aid business expansion while unlocking future increased rates income for local and central government.That unlocking of future tax-based growth would operate alongside existing initiatives brought in since the restoration of the Executive, such as the Back in Business scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of whether individual inspections ought to take place at new businesses, particularly in the hospitality sector, as part of the revised Net Annual Value assessment.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) determines Net Annual Values in accordance with the statutory framework set out in the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order 1977. As part of this process, LPS may carry out an inspection of a property where this is necessary to establish the physical characteristics and use of the hereditament. In general, new properties will be inspected as part of the standard process to ensure that the Net Annual Value accurately reflects the property as it exists on the ground.For businesses, including those in the hospitality sector, an inspection may also take place where there have been physical alterations, changes in layout or use, or where additional information is required to complete the valuation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any artificial intelligence improvements in relation to stroke services, the last five years.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence to assist in the interpretation of scans and the targeting of treatment including thrombectomy and thrombolysis was a Reshaping Stroke Care, Stroke Action Plan (2022) priority. I can confirm that Artificial Intelligence software has also been rolled out in all stroke receiving hospitals to support clinical decision-making. This has been funded recurrently from savings made elsewhere in stroke services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether (i) underinvestment in; or (ii) delay of, major public capital projects is taken into account with regards to environmental impact factors, for Net Annual Value assessment.
answer_text: I can confirm that neither underinvestment in, nor delays to, major public capital projects are taken into account when assessing Net Annual Value (NAV).Non-domestic rating valuations are carried out under the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order 1977. Under Schedule 12 of that Order, the basis of assessment is a rental value at a single, fixed statutory valuation date. Valuers are required to estimate the rent a property could reasonably be expected to command on the open market at that valuation date, and only evidence relevant to that statutory test may be considered.On 29 January, having listened to concerns raised following publication of the draft list, I announced my decision to stop the Reval 2026 process. I would refer the Member to my oral statement and assembly debate of 2 February.Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of Finance Official Reports

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the PITSTOP pilot at Altnagelvin Area Hospital in relation to stroke services and ambulance response.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any interim measures can take place with regards to the Clifton Gateway scheme and her Department’s role, before the full implementation of the Belfast Rapid Transit.
answer_text: My announcement on the Belfast Rapid Transit project represents a commitment to an initial investment of £48m in this innovative service across the North and South of the city, bringing the benefits already established by the Glider serviced to communities living along this route.Funding of £35m was allocated within the Belfast Region City Deal, and my Departmental budget committed an additional £13m to deliver this initial phase.  Officials are focussed on delivering the best value possible through interventions along the route which create the most benefit, and the delivery of a Park and Ride at O’Neill Road.Due to the significant traffic management implications of the work associated with the Carlisle Circus junction, including Clifton Gate, it is important to ensure that the delivery of this signalisation is planned to reduce the impact on the travelling public and to minimise congestion levels in and around the city centre.Unfortunately, for the reasons outlined above, and the need to deliver best value with the finite resources available for the BRT2 scheme, this work will not form part of this phase of the project, but will be timed for completion in advance of full operation of the North/South Glider route.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why there is not an accessible mechanism to report or log chronic problem parking, akin to those for road or lighting repairs, in order to ensure footways are not blocked.
answer_text: My Department currently has the authority to issue Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) where a vehicle is observed parked on a legislated restriction, such as double yellow lines. However, my Department cannot take enforcement action where a vehicle is parked on a pavement causing an obstruction where no parking restrictions are marked on the carriageway. In such cases, responsibility lies with the PSNI.Enforcement schedules remain flexible and, where resources permit, can be adjusted. If additional enforcement is required, my parking enforcement team can be contacted at this email address Parking.Request@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk which is also accessible on my Department’s website at this link Contacts | Department for InfrastructureMy Department has had exploratory discussions on the possibility of adding a web link to nidirect, similar to those for reporting faults, however, due to other work commitments this has not progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Women’s Health Action Plan.
answer_text: As I advised in my response to AQW 38113/22-27, I plan to publish the Women's Health Action Plan following completion of the public listening exercise. My Department has helped to fund this important project by Derry Well Women and Queen’s University Belfast who have now completed a women’s health survey and focus groups with harder to reach women. There will be a further round of engagement in partnership with stakeholders and representative organisations during February to share the findings and to identify the priorities and recommendations which will inform the final report to my Department. The Plan when published will describe the wide range of policy and service developments supporting women's health, what has been achieved to date and what is achievable in the years ahead within current resources. It will also set out how delivery will be overseen and coordinated on an ongoing basis across the range of relevant services and specialties, and in continued partnership with the representative groups who have been instrumental in raising the profile of women's health in Northern Ireland and ensuring that women’s voices and experiences are heard and reflected in the Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any prioritisation criteria that will be used for school enhancement projects, given the gap in funding required for completion of all schools within the School Enhancement Programme.
answer_text: Funding continues to be available to progress SEP projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release projects to tender for a contractor will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.I will continue to make the case for additional funding for education through the Executive to allow projects to proceed to construction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the capital development stage of Our Lady of Lourdes Primary School, North Belfast.
answer_text: Our Lady of Lourdes Primary School was announced in May 2018 to advance in design under the second call to the School Enhancement Programme (SEP).The project is now very well advanced.  The RIBA Stage 4 (Technical Design) report has been received from the Design Team and is currently being reviewed by my Department.Funding is available to progress SEP projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release projects to tender for a contractor will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.  I will continue to make the case for additional funding for education through the Executive to allow projects, such as Our Lady of Lourdes Primary School, to proceed to construction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures her Department is taking to ensure restrictions against pavement parking across Belfast, are being effectively enforced.
answer_text: My Department currently has the authority to issue Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) where a vehicle is observed parked on a legislated restriction, such as double yellow lines. These restrictions are enforced through traffic attendants who are deployed across Belfast and the rest of the North.Enforcement schedules remain flexible and, where resources permit, can be adjusted. If additional enforcement is required, my parking enforcement team can be contacted at this email address: Parking.Request@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, which is also accessible on my Department’s website. However, my Department cannot take enforcement action where a vehicle is parked on a pavement causing an obstruction where no parking restrictions are marked on the carriageway. In such cases, responsibility lies with the PSNI.My Department continues to work collaboratively with others to address particular problem areas that are brought to our attention. For example, my Department has worked with Belfast City Council and the PSNI on the ‘Park with us in mind’ initiative which targeted hotspots where council waste collection vehicles were often unable to gain access to areas due to poor, inconsiderate or illegal parking.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his department plays any role in decisions to remove the daily living component of Personal Independent Payments, from residents in care homes.
answer_text: When a person in receipt of the daily living component of Personal Independence Payment (PIP) has been resident in a care home for more than 28 days, and their stay in the care home is funded by a Health & Social Care Trust, a decision maker in the Department will make a decision to suspend payability of the daily living component of PIP. Although payment is suspended until they leave the care home, their underlying entitlement to the daily living component is retained and payment reinstated following their discharge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on efforts being taken by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to address workforce issues within Community Dental Services (CDS), in particular providing clear commissioning guidance on the CDS scope of service as an important step in ensuring this service is not overrun, given that access to NHS dentistry in General Dental Services is declining.
answer_text: Community Dental Services (CDS) workforce planning responsibilities are shared across the Department of Health (DoH), the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), and the Health and Social Care Trusts.Trusts are responsible for developing business cases to address local workforce needs, including staffing shortages and changes in skill mix. Structural or regionwide changes to CDS fall under the remit of DoH. Training and workforce development are matters for the Workforce Policy Directorate, working closely with the Chief Dental Officer (CDO) and the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA).My Department has progressed several workstreams to support reform of the CDS including a detailed analysis of CDS activity and patient treatment groups across all five Trusts and a UKwide scoping exercise on CDS referral criteria and service remits. These outputs have been shared with all CDS Clinical Directors.Workforce pressures and service alignment have been regularly discussed at the quarterly CDS Clinical Director meetings with SPPG and the CDO. A BDAfacilitated CDS Roundtable in September 2025 prompted further collaborative work.A dedicated regional meeting is scheduled for early February with the aim of reducing unwanted clinical variation between Trusts, producing an updated CDS scope of service and standardising data collection.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how Translink’s rail planning process takes account of adopted Local Development Plans; and (ii) the rationale as to why a railway halt at Merville Garden Village, at the Abbey Centre, identified in the Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council Local Development Plan 2030, is not currently reflected within Translink’s Shaping the Future rail strategy.
answer_text: The reference to a Merville halt in the Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council Plan Strategy document and its omission from the Shaping the Future rail review reflects the differences in purpose and remit between these documents. The Plan Strategy is the statutory first part of the Local Development Plan prepared by the Council under the Planning Act (NI) 2011. Its purpose is to set the longterm spatial planning framework for the whole council area through establishing a vision, strategic objectives, spatial growth strategy, strategic policies and detailed management policies that guide development decisions. The Council has identified the Merville halt in its Plan Strategy because it remains a valid local policy aspiration for the Council. While the Plan Strategy may identify infrastructure such as new railway halts, nothing in it should be read as a commitment that public resources will be provided and all proposals requiring public expenditure remain subject to economic appraisal and resource availability. The Shaping the Future is based on a series of feasibility studies and is intended to support strategic rail investment planning and therefore prioritises major systemwide enhancements such as electrification, intercity journeytime improvements, reinstated lines, and airport connectivity. The proposal for a halt at Merville did not fall within any of the areas which were the subject of the feasibility studies.  However, it is recognised that wider network capacity constraints would require that any such proposals to be considered after route electrification and capacity enhancement on the Antrim / Larne line.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will task officials to undertake a needs analysis of Community Dental Services (CDS) to ensure demand is aligned with capacity, as recommended by a recent British Dental Association roundtable event focusing on CDS Workforce issues.
answer_text: A needs analysis of the Community Dental Service (CDS) is currently under consideration as part of a wider assessment of the future remit of the Service. High quality data on population health is a key element of needs assessment and, while SPPG and Trusts have made significant progress in gathering population oral health data, this work is not yet complete across the main CDS patient groups.The British Dental Association convened a CDS Workforce Roundtable event in September 2025 and this was attended by representatives from my Department, SPPG as well as the CDS. A central recommendation emerging from this event was that a Trust-led service analysis should be the first step in exploring the gap between local demand and capacity. However, the main challenge in establishing a clear demand-capacity position for CDS remains the absence of reliable and comparable activity data from Trusts. Addressing this is the immediate focus of SPPG’s CDS-related work, and it is expected that standardised information will be available from Trusts in the near future.A regional meeting between SPPG, the CDO and Trust representatives is scheduled for February 2026 to discuss the future scope of CDS services. This meeting will help establish a consistent regional understanding of the CDS remit and will support the standardisation of data collection and data interpretation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail when a workforce plan was last put in place to determine existing and projected service pressures in (a) Community Dental Services; and (b) Hospital Dental Services; and (ii) for an update on progress made.
answer_text: Establishing a dental workforce with the capacity and skills to meet the future demands of the Northern Ireland population is a complex task involving contributions from multiple stakeholders including my Department, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group, Health and Social Care Trusts and staff representatives. The Skills for Health’s Workforce Review for Dental Services in Northern Ireland final report, which was published in 2023, had input from all of these groups. The stakeholder engagement event which followed the publication of the report provided an opportunity for voices to be heard from other important groups such as dental nurses, dental hygienists and dental laboratories.The Community Dental Service (CDS) plays a central role in prevention, special care dentistry, care of vulnerable groups, children and older people, and has been instrumental in improving children’s oral health outcomes through community programmes. The Workforce Review highlights that CDS will, amongst other things, need to shift towards new models of care, integrated with wider health and social care services and make better use of skill mix. Progress has been made in this regard with the development of Extended Duty Dental Nurses who play an increasingly important role in the CDS by supporting the delivery of preventive oral health interventions, including the application of fluoride varnish.Also, in 2023 the Department produced the Northern Ireland Children’s Oral Health Improvement Plan (NIOHIP). Among the CDS workforce relevant recommendations contained in this report were that the dental epidemiology programme needs to be further supported and embedded within the CDS. SPPG has worked closely with Trusts to ensure the stability of this programme through investment and staff training. There is now a rolling Northern Ireland annual survey programme staffed by enthusiastic and dedicated CDS personnel.A further CDS relevant recommendation in the NIOHIP was to enhance CDS’s role in preventive programmes. Earlier this year, I agreed three-year funding of £400,000 per year to expand the Northern Ireland evidence-based children’s supervised toothbrushing programme (Happy Smiles). This involves additional funding for CDS staffing across all Trusts.Hospital Dental Services (HDS) are delivered by a small but highly specialised workforce that includes consultants, specialty registrars, staff grades, and foundation dentists. This team provides complex, specialist dental care and forms an essential support system for both the CDS and the General Dental Services.Although projections indicate that only a modest increase in hospital dentist numbers will be needed to meet population growth the Workforce Review for Dental Services notes significant gaps in workforce intelligence. Key data such as age profiles, retirement intentions, and likely attrition are either incomplete or unavailable, making longterm planning challenging. These information gaps limit confiden

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether future rail connectivity options for North Belfast have been considered as part of Translink’s long-term network planning; and (ii) whether locations such as Giant’s Park and Loughside have been assessed, given their planned regeneration and proximity to the existing rail network.
answer_text: The All Island Strategic Rail Review included a recommendation that consideration be given to new stations in the Belfast and Derry city regions. As set out in the Project Prioritisation Strategy, new stations will be progressed as “business as usual”, subject to the necessary approvals and funding. This means that Translink will consider the need for additional stations through their usual network planning process.Translink currently has no plans to develop a new station at Giant Park in the short to medium term. Its modelling of the wider network has indicated that provision of any further railway halts on the Larne /Antrim line between Whiteabbey and York Street could only be considered following capacity increases, either through the introduction of battery-electric trains or infrastructure enhancements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail where responsibility lies within his Department for workforce planning for (i) Community Dental Services; and (ii) Hospital Dental Services.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for strategic workforce planning, i.e. aiming to secure workforce supply over a 5 – 10-year horizon for health and social care, including dental services. It commissioned Skills for Health’s Workforce Review for Dental Services in Northern Ireland (August 2018) for that purpose. Community Dental Services and Hospital Dental Services were included within the remit of that review, which was published in July 2023.Individual Health and Social Care Trusts are responsible for operational workforce planning, i.e. service delivery and safe staffing levels, operational vacancy management and recruitment, skill mix/band mix changes, career progression pathways and workforce design to support new models of care. They aim to ensure that their respective Community Dental Services and Hospital Dental Services are sufficiently staffed to provide those services commissioned from them by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any current policy in relation to the sharing of secondary health data to enable research and innovation in healthcare.
answer_text: The Department’s approach to the sharing of secondary health data for research and innovation is governed by a robust legislative framework. Primarily the processing and sharing of such data is regulated under existing legislation, notably the Health And Social Care (Control of Data Processing) Act 2016, which legislates for statutory regulations in the use of patient identifiable data, without explicit consent in limited circumstances. Recognising the need to address specific issues in the original legislation, the Department is bringing forward a Bill to amend the 2016 Act; this amendment seeks to correct identified legislative shortcomings rather than introduce fundamental changes to the principles governing secondary data use.Supporting policy measures have been developed in parallel with the legislation. The policy underpinning the 2016 act has been updated to align with the proposed bill, ensuring continued compliance and clarity. It should be noted, however, that there is no single, standalone departmental policy authorising the secondary use of health data. Instead, the department relies on the statutory provisions of the act 2016, relevant common law duties, and the requirements set out in the UK General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and Data Protection Act 2018 to provide the legal basis and necessary safeguards for the processing and sharing of health data for secondary purposes.Further, the statutory framework and accompanying regulations, which are yet to be established, will detail the practical mechanisms by which organisations can comply with the legislative requirements. These future regulations will outline the operational aspects of data sharing and processing, ensuring consistency and transparency across the sector. The Department is required to publish a code of practice, which will provide requirements to HSC bodies/ organisations on best practice in the handling and sharing of secondary health data for research and innovation, reinforcing the commitment to safe, lawful and effective use of health and care data in Northern Ireland.The Department of Health has, currently, published a 'Code of Practice on protecting confidentiality of patient records'.   This will be replaced by a statutory Code of Practice, which will be put in place as part of the enabling regulations to the Act 2016. 	- https://privacyadvisorycommittee.hscni.net/PACrefdocs.html

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any training programmes available to Principals and teachers in regards to supporting pupils who are neurodiverse; and (ii) whether this training is mandatory.
answer_text: (i) The Education Authority (EA) and Middletown Centre for Autism (MCA) currently offer a range of training to all teachers and the educational workforce in relation to Autism and neurodiverse pupils. The EA through its Programme of Professional Development Opportunities offers professional development opportunities to school staff which includes courses to support neurodiverse pupils.  MCA provides a range of accessible full-day, part-day and online training sessions for all school teams where the focus of the training is the support of autistic pupils in school settings and the development of neuroaffirming school culture.Initial Teacher Education (ITE) programmes are aligned with the Northern Ireland Curriculum and designed to prepare future teachers for its delivery. All ITE programmes include mandatory and optional Special Educational Needs modules ensuring new teachers achieve the professional competences set out by the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI). (ii) training programmes offered by the EA and MCA to support neurodiverse pupils is not mandatory.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether patients will soon have access to Abiratone for treatment in relation to prostate cancer.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions. In practice this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also routinely available in Northern Ireland.Where NICE has recommended the use of abiraterone (Zytiga®) in the treatment of prostate cancer, this medicine is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, in line with the current NICE guidelines. Further information on this treatment is available at the following links:https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta259https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta387https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta951https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta1110In circumstances where NICE is yet to complete a full appraisal, or does not recommend a particular drug, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) does not routinely commission that therapy for use in Northern Ireland. Northern Ireland does not have its own health technology assessment body or equivalent to NICE, Scottish Medicines Consortium or All Wales Medicine Strategy Group, that could make a bespoke determination for Northern Ireland patients in the absence of NICE guidance.My Department has recently published a review of HSC medicines access arrangements which recognises the need to take action to ensure continued access to clinically and cost-effective medicines for the Northern Ireland population on an equitable basis, including in cases where NICE have not made a determination, but use is supported by clinical evidence and best practice. Informed by the review’s recommendations, my Department plans to review the existing workforce infrastructure underpinning medicines access pathways to identify potential resource requirements needed to strengthen existing access arrangements, including for use of off-label and off-patent medicines where use is supported by clinical evidence and best practice. Any additional resource requirements for medicines access infrastructure, and additional costs associated with provision of access for off-label or off-patent medicines, will need to be considered within the context of my Department’s challenging financial position and competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why bespoke arrangements for community placement and care have not been completed for residents over the age of 18 residing at the Iveagh Centre.
answer_text: There have been significant efforts to plan bespoke arrangements for the individual who is over 18 and continues to be a patient in Iveagh children’s ward. These efforts will continue in partnership with the family. This young person’s case is on the Muckamore resettlement list, and is presented to the Department of Health Oversight Board fortnightly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of inpatients residing in the Iveagh Centre.
answer_text: There are currently 3 inpatients residing in the Iveagh Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of individuals currently assessed as requiring respite support, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) provided the following response outlining the number of individuals assessed as requiring respite/short break support, as noted below.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT):Children’s ServicesWithin the SEHSCT there are currently 99 children availing of respite/ short breaks through a combination of residential short breaks, fostering short breaks, Children’s Disability community in-reach short breaks and Children’s Disability community in-reach short breaks contracted through the voluntary sector. These 99 children receive short breaks on a regular basis, however the Fostering Service also delivered episodic short breaks to 93 children within the Delegated Statutory reporting period between April and September 2025 to support placements both within care and within the community. In relation to the combination of the above services there are currently 77 children on the various waiting lists to receive respite/short breaks.ADULT SERVICESMental Health ServicesThere are no commissioned respite/short break beds in the Trust for adults known to Mental Health Services. Respite/short break beds are spot purchased when essential and there are currently no people identified as requiring this support.Adults Learning Disability ServicesThere are 454 individuals currently assessed as requiring respite/short break support in Adult Learning Disability Services within the Trust. In Adult Learning Disability Services there are 32 service users on a waiting list for respite/short breaks.Physical and Sensory ServicesThere are 39 individuals currently assessed as requiring respite/short breaks support in Adult Physical Disability and Sensory Support Services. This includes access to bed-based care, domiciliary support and direct payments.Older People’s ServicesIn Older People’s Services approximately 8-10% of the Older People’s Community Teams’ caseloads have been assessed as requiring and have availed of respite/short breaks, this equates to between 573-458 individuals. There is no waiting list for respite care/short breaks in Older People’s Services.  The Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT):Children’s ServicesThe SHSCT Children with Disabilities Service currently provide overnight short breaks provision via short breaks residential placements and short breaks fostering placements. As of the 21st January 2026, the total number of children assessed as requiring and receiving overnight short breaks are 36 children via short breaks residential placements, and 18 children via active short break fostering homes. As of the 21st January 2026, the total number of children awaiting access to overnight short breaks are 20 children awaiting short break residential provision, and 24 children awaiting short breaks fostering provision. In addition, short breaks are provided to children and families through self-directed support packages, summer schemes, day clubs, voluntary sector contracted individual development sc

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the resettlement scheme for patients at Muckamore Abbey Hospital.
answer_text: There are currently 7 individuals remaining in Muckamore Abbey Hospital. Two of these have resettlement plans in place with expected discharge dates in January 2026, and one patient is expected to transfer to an alternative hospital placement.  Planning is continuing to finalise suitable community placements for the other 4 individuals. Work to complete the resettlement of the remaining patients continues to be overseen by the Regional Resettlement Oversight Board, chaired by Dr Patricia Donnelly.  The Oversight Board meets on a fortnightly basis and their work is informed by ongoing engagement with key personnel from the relevant Trusts, providers of community resettlement accommodation and patients and their families and advocates.  As the hospital population continues to reduce, the staffing position at Muckamore is being kept under ongoing review and Dr Donnelly has asked the RQIA to work with the Belfast Trust to monitor on-site services.  My focus remains on ensuring that services at the hospital can continue to be provided safely, and that any contingency arrangements that may be necessary to facilitate this are in place.  The primary purpose of any such arrangements will be to ensure the safety and well-being of the remaining patients.  These contingency arrangements are considered at meetings of the Regional Resettlement Oversight Board, and will be discussed with patients and families should they be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of judicial reviews that are ongoing regarding patients currently residing at the Iveagh Centre.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT):The Belfast HSC Trust has one Judicial Review ongoing related to a patient currently residing at the Iveagh Centre.Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT):The Northern HSC Trust has no ongoing Judicial Reviews related to patients currently residing at the Iveagh Centre.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT):Within the South Eastern HSC Trust there is currently one active Judicial Review ongoing relating to a delayed discharge in Iveagh Inpatients Hospital.  Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT):The Southern HSC Trust have not been subject of any Judicial Review proceedings relating to Iveagh. Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT):The Western HSC Trust does not hold any recorded Judicial Review cases, that are ongoing regarding patients currently residing at the Iveagh Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of homes initially planned for North Belfast, through the Housing for All Shared Housing Programme.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Since the inception of the Housing for All (HfA) Shared Housing Programme, three developments within the North Belfast District Electoral Area have been included in the programme, delivering a total of 168 homes:Felden, Newtownabbey – 97 units delivered by Clanmil Housing AssociationAbbeyville, Newtownabbey – 35 units delivered by Connswater Housing Association (now Choice)Glenwhirry Court, Newtownabbey – 36 units delivered by Clanmil Housing AssociationIn addition, it is hoped to add a further scheme at Antrim Road, Glengormley to the 2025/26 programme, which is being delivered by Woven Housing Association and would provide a further 63 homes.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans to introduce legislation regarding mental health first aid training.
answer_text: There are no plans to introduce legislation regarding MHFA training. This training is currently available through the Public Health Agency and 1922 people were trained in MHFA in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any underspend in the Housing for All Shared Housing Programme, since its transfer to the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“There has been no underspend of Housing for All shared housing programme budget since the programme transferred to the Housing Executive.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are plans for more schemes in North Belfast under the Housing for All Shared Housing Programme.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing for All Team in the Housing Executive are currently assessing Woven Housing Association’s new 63-unit development at Antrim Road, Glengormley, for potential to be added to the 25/26 Housing for All programme.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many respite beds that were scheduled for patients, were cancelled in the last financial year, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Belfast Trust CAMHS do not have respite bed provision within the current service model.   From April 2025 to present, two overnight short breaks had to be cancelled.  These were in relation to two separate children open to Belfast Trust.   NHSCT 15 short breaks have been cancelled in total since short breaks reopened in August 2025. A further 12 children had their short breaks changed in September and were provided with day care instead. SEHSCT The South Eastern Trust can provide this information from June 2025 as prior to this date the Greenhill provision was unable to fully operate given workforce deficits.   From June 2025 there have been 4 respite bed nights cancelled, and all cancellations were based on assessed risk to other children.  Where it was possible, attempts were made to offer alternative nights to families who were affected. SHSCT From the 1st April 2025 until 28 January 2026, the Southern Trust Children with Disabilities service provided overnight short breaks provision via 2 Short Breaks Residential facilities and short break foster care.   During this period both facilities cancelled a very small number of overnights (as a consequence of unforeseen circumstances). During the period, from a total of approximately 1,600 overnight short breaks provided, 33 overnight episodes of cancellation occurred. This impacted on 21 individual children and the Trust has endeavoured to rescheduled any cancelled nights. WHSCT The Trust have interpreted ‘last financial year’ as the period 1st April 2025 to date.   The WHSCT are commissioned for 4 respite beds in Rosebud Cottage, Londonderry.  These beds were available 6 days per week from 1st April 2025 – 26th May 2025. However, this service was paused on 27th May 2025 due to an emergency placement of a child with complex needs.  Due to the high level of assessed risk associated with this placement, no other young people could be accommodated for short breaks.  Two additional emergency admissions have been facilitated in Rosebud Cottage since 20th January 2026.  The MDT are working intensively with the family to ensure supports are in place to allow these young people to return to the family home as soon as possible.    The Trust is also commissioned for the provision of 8 respite beds in Avalon House, Omagh. This service continues to remain paused due to the ongoing medium/long term placements of a number of young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of Personal Expenses Allowance for patients in a residential care home.
answer_text: The Personal Expenses Allowance (PEA) rate is specified in the annual Charging for Residential Accommodation Guide (CRAG) circular. This amount is reviewed each year and is generally uplifted in line with the increase in Northern Ireland Average Earnings. The current rate for the PEA is £34.10. My Department will notify Trusts of any change in the rate of personal expenses by way of an issued circular. The PEA amount is the same for each resident, whether in a Trust managed home or an independent sector home.The annual review of the PEA will conclude in March 2026 and any amendments will take effect from 6 April 2026.The CRAG circular for 2026/27 advising of the revised PEA will be circulated and made available on the Department's external website on completion of the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on how the additional £13 million in year funding for children's respite services has been spent.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any increases in the number of respite beds following the £13 million in year funding into children's respite care.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on plans to address the shared care arrangements around neurodiversity diagnosis in under-18s.

To ask the Minister of Health whether Health and Social Care Trust policy enables residents who have a Patients’ Private Property Account to access funds through care home staff, or otherwise to pay for living costs such as clothing.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Women's Health Action Plan, which was announced in February 2024.
answer_text: I plan to publish the Women's Health Action Plan following completion of the public listening exercise over the coming months. My Department has helped to fund this important project by Derry Well Women and Queen’s University Belfast who have now completed a women’s health survey and focus groups with harder to reach women. There will be a further round of engagement in partnership with stakeholders and  representative organisations during February to share the findings and to identify the priorities and recommendations which will inform the final report to my Department. The Plan when published will describe the wide range of policy and service developments supporting women's health, what has been achieved to date and what is achievable in the years ahead within current resources. It will also set out how delivery will be overseen and coordinated on an ongoing basis across the range of relevant services and specialties, and in continued partnership with the representative groups who have who have been instrumental in raising the profile of women's health in Northern Ireland and ensuring that women’s voices and experiences are heard and reflected in the Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the commissioning of robotic assisted surgery for gynaecology surgeries, in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Robotic Assisted Surgery has been identified through the General Surgery Network as part of the Review of General Surgery within Northern Ireland.  Worked has commenced to look at what the requirements would be, and to discuss how this could support the workforce and population. My officials within SPPG are leading these discussions with Trust colleagues at present. A sub group has been established and preparation work commenced to consider the development of a regional, phased, pan specialism plan for Robotic Assisted Surgery within Northern Ireland.There is agreement across the Trusts of the need for a regional pan specialism approach, which is co-produced and agreed with clinicians and other stakeholders.  The specialism of gynaecology is one service in a state of readiness to proceed.  There are challenges and barriers to implement Robotic Assisted Surgery into NI, given the current financial position and the significant investment required.  Nevertheless, I have asked my officials to continue to proceed with this work to ensure Northern Ireland maintains at pace with other nations and an agreed regional direction is set.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any enforcement action has taken place in North Belfast against developers, under the new Private Streets Enforcement Guidance.
answer_text: On 25 September 2025 I announced the introduction of new Private Streets Enforcement Guidance for staff.  This guidance will greatly reduce the risk of developments, constructed from January 2024, remaining unadopted.  The new policy indicates enforcement action will be taken by the Department after a period of 2 years following 80% occupancy. Therefore, the timescales have not yet been breached.My officials continue to monitor the progress of all housing developments and engage with developers to ensure timely adoption through the normal Private Streets process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans to upgrade diabetic users who have a constant glucose monitor to the new Libre 3 sensors.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the road adoption of Loughshore Manor, Newtownabbey; and (ii) whether there has been any movement from the developers on completing the scheme; and (iii) whether any enforcement action can take place, under the new Private Streets Enforcement Guidance, in order to complete construction and adopt the road.
answer_text: Loughshore Manor is a private development accessed via a bridge over the railway line which was constructed in consultation with Translink in 2008.  In 2015, planning consent was granted for a revised scheme which required the adoption of the road and footway over the already constructed bridge.As the time of construction the bridge was not being considered for adoption and therefore technical approval was not required.  Since the revised planning consent the developer has been working with the department to gain the necessary compliance requirements for the bridge to allow the adoption of the associated road and footways.The developer has recently advised the Department they cannot achieve Technical Approval for the bridge and therefore the Department has determined the developer to have defaulted in their responsibility and enforcement action under the Private Streets Order may now be considered.However, there would be significant challenges in progressing successful enforcement, particularly given the developer’s failure to demonstrate technical approval for the design and construction of the bridge and the financial shortfall within the bond funding which would be required to achieve adoption.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his Department's assessment of the psychiatric assessment and treatment hub pilot within the Mater Hospital; and (iI) whether there are any plans to extend this project to other areas, given the pressure it releases on Emergency Departments.
answer_text: The Psychiatric Assessment and Treatment Hub (PATH) has indicated a positive impact in its first year of operation, including reduction in patients waiting in Emergency Departments or community for admission; a reduction in services completing 1:1 observations within Emergency Departments, which helped reduce some pressure on Emergency Departments. Belfast Health and Care Trust have submitted a business case to support year two delivery, which my officials are currently considering. Any decision regarding further funding to support PATH will need to be taken within the context of significant budgetary constraints, and alongside wider pressures within the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department supports after-school daycare providers to offer the Childcare Subsidy Scheme, to allow parents to avail of the subsidised childcare.
answer_text: My Department provides an administrative payment to all childcare providers, including after-school daycare providers, that choose to participate in the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS). This payment is intended to help offset additional administrative costs associated with operating the NICSS, including applying the subsidy to invoices and submitting monthly claims. There are currently 19 daycare providers and 12 out of school providers operating in North Belfast. Of these, 17 daycare providers and 8 out of school providers are registered to the NICSS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any criteria used to determine funding priorities across competing pressures.
answer_text: As part of Budget Information Gathering exercises and in-year monitoring rounds, departments are required to submit returns to my Department setting out the justification for any requests for additional funding. This information is used to inform the recommendations I present to the Executive for approval.Decisions on all funding allocations are taken collectively by the Executive, taking account of a range of factors including the level of funding available, competing pressures and the alignment of individual bids with our Programme for Government priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the rational as to why there is no (a) service user; or (b) carer representatives, on the Regional Area Integrated Partnership Boards; and (ii) whether he will give consideration to appointing such representatives.
answer_text: We have put in place the building blocks for greater community and stakeholder engagement in shaping health services through our integrated care system. Currently there are five Area Integrated Partnership Boards (AIPB), one for each Trust area. The AIPBs are supported and guided by the Regional ICS Partnership Forum.Each AIPB has one user and one carer member and while it is intended the Regional ICS Partnership Forum will expand its membership to include users and carers, to date their views have been reflected in the Forum by the Chair of the ICS Service User and Carer Liaison Group.With developments underway to establish a Neighbourhood model of care, the role and function of the AIPBs and the future role for the Regional ICS Partnership Forum are under consideration which is the rationale for not expanding the Forum’s membership at this time.Regional ICS Partnership Forum membership will be kept under review and the Department will ensure service user and carer views and participation are embedded in future Forum arrangements as these become clear over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number (i) of day care and after-school providers registered in North Belfast; and (ii) of these who enable parents to avail of the Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: My Department provides an administrative payment to all childcare providers, including after-school daycare providers, that choose to participate in the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS). This payment is intended to help offset additional administrative costs associated with operating the NICSS, including applying the subsidy to invoices and submitting monthly claims. There are currently 19 daycare providers and 12 out of school providers operating in North Belfast. Of these, 17 daycare providers and 8 out of school providers are registered to the NICSS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current estimated number of places required for special school allocations, in North Belfast, for 2026-2027 admissions.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that;Early planning assumptions are collated at a local council district area. Current planning assumptions indicate a pressure of circa 100 special school places within the Belfast City Council area.Our strategic vision is that every child with SEN has equity to be placed, in their community with their peers, which supports them to reach their full potential.  EA are actively engaging with schools to make this vision a reality; however, this will require a fundamental shift towards a system in which all schools offer specialist provision classes, alongside sustainable financial investment in the school estate to alleviate placement pressures within our special schools and improve the outcomes for all our children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the (i) commissioning of; and (ii) business case proposal for, robotic-assisted surgery for gynaecological use.
answer_text: Robotic Assisted Surgery has been identified through the General Surgery Network as part of the Review of General Surgery within Northern Ireland.
