question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's policy for the adoption of new and any unadopted roads.
answer_text: Road adoption by my Department is governed by The Private Streets (NI) Order 1980 (The Order) and roads, including those within private developments, will only be considered for adoption if brought to standard through the Private Streets Determination process or by owners/frontages in accordance with Article 9 of the Order.On 25 September 2025 I announced the introduction of new Private Streets Enforcement Guidance for staff.  This guidance will greatly reduce the risk of developments, constructed from January 2024, remaining unadopted.  My officials continue to monitor the progress of all housing developments and engage with developers to ensure timely adoption through the normal Private Streets process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on a review of neighbourhood renewal areas and partnerships.
answer_text: The Review of the Executive’s ‘People and Place Strategy: A strategy for Neighbourhood Renewal’ (2003) was progressed through an inclusive approach and has concluded, with the development of a Strategic Framework draft.The Strategic Framework is currently being considered by myself and, subject to my Executive colleagues approval, a public consultation will take place.It is not anticipated that any changes to the existing Neighbourhood Renewal programme will take place during the 2026/27 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Women's Health Action Plan will contain actions specifically addressing endometriosis waiting lists within the gynaecology service.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the implications of the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30 for her Department.
answer_text: I welcome the launch of the Draft Budget 2026-2029/30 consultation which will help inform the Executive’s consideration and decisions on a Final Budget 2026-29/30.I fully recognise the constrained financial position and the challenges faced with increasing demand and costs to deliver vital public services and on our Programme for Government priorities. The proposed allocations for my Department would go some way towards addressing the level of investment required for our infrastructure and for maintaining service delivery.However, difficult decisions would be required in prioritising services and capital schemes for delivery, which I would aim to do alongside seeking to drive further efficiencies and innovation within my Department.The reality is that the funding package that the Executive receives from London continues to fall well short of what is required, to run and recover, our public services after over a decade of austerity from British Governments.I look forward to consideration of the responses to the Draft Budget consultation and working with Executive colleagues to agree a Final Budget 2026-29/30.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment of the capacity of suicide prevention and crisis support services have been carried out following the November 2025 publication of the NISRA Suicide Statistics 2024 report.

To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the November 2025 NISRA Suicide Statistics 2024 report, including (i) any additional actions to be taken; and (ii) any additional funding to be allocated towards suicide prevention.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the devastating impact of each and every suicide, not only in lives lost but also the impact on loved ones and communities. I believe that every suicide is preventable up to the point of completion, so while we may set realistic targets, the only acceptable number is zero. In light of the high levels of suicide recorded in 2024 I recently directed the lifting of the embargo of awareness raising for the Lifeline service. The ‘Talking Really Helps’ mass media campaign will run until March 2026. I would wish to emphasise that for anyone who is in distress or despair, the Lifeline helpline is available free of charge 24 hours a day, 7 days a week where trained counsellors are available to take your call on 0808 808 8000. A bid is also being considered for further awareness raising in the coming year.It is disappointing to note the further increase in the numbers of recorded suicide deaths. I am particularly concerned by the 3 year rolling average showing a small upward trend. This average is much more accurate than looking at single year differences which can have bigger fluctuations from year to year. The 3 year average has remained very stable for a long time. Any increase in that is concerning and highlights we must redouble our efforts to provide support to those most in need.We continue to see suicide as the leading cause of death in men under 50 and 79% of all suicide deaths registered in 2024 were male.Of particular concern is the increase in suicide of those aged over 65. It is very clear that further support is required for those in older age groups It is also worth highlighting that in the suicide deaths recorded in 2024: 61% were single, 8% were divorced and 6% were widowed. Family is a very strong protective factor against suicide where early support can be provided.Despite our strategic approach focusing on reducing the differential between the most deprived communities and the least deprived it is also very concerning to see the disparity widening between suicides registered in the most deprived and the least deprived areas in 2024.With regard to Protect Life 2 we will continue to focus resource on the most deprived communities. However, I recognise that many of the issues affecting those in the most deprived communities are outside the direct control of this Department. This underlines the importance of a cross government approach to the issue and full delivery of the action plan which cuts across all Departments.Bids have been made for additional funding to support the implementation of our action plans with a particular focus on Under 18 postvention, enhancement of self harm intervention programme and awareness raising for future years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has had any engagement with suicide prevention charities and organisations following the November 2025 publication of the NISRA Suicide Statistics 2024 report.
answer_text: The 2024 statistics were discussed at the Protect Life 2 Steering Group chaired by the Chief Medical Officer at its meeting on 4 December which is attended by a range of suicide prevention Community and Voluntary Group representatives. The Chief Medical Officer also wrote to the Steering Group and all local Protect Life Implementation Groups regarding the statistics on the day the Report issued.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Women’s Health Action Plan will contain plans for women's health hubs.
answer_text: The Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) report on Gynaecology included a recommendation to establish women’s health hubs to improve access to services and streamline care for women. Whilst there are variations in approach and terminology regarding women’s health hubs across the UK, the concept aims to provide a range of services with rapid, local access within a single visit, provide assessment and treatment where possible and reduce referrals into elective waiting lists. Despite the severe funding constraints facing the Health and Social Care system, work is currently underway to develop a suitable model for gynaecology ambulatory hubs which will work collaboratively with the Enhanced Primary Care Service to ensure patients are seen in the right place and at the right time, in line with the direction of travel towards a Neighbourhood Model of Care. In the meantime, this service continues to manage referrals for pessary fitting, HRT management, initial assessment and treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding, and contraceptive management including coil fitting.  Further detail will be set out as one of the priorities in the Women’s Health Action Plan, which I plan to issue after the current listening exercise is completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Clifton Gateway public realm scheme, alongside Ulster University.
answer_text: My department continues to engage with the Department for Infrastructure (DfI), in respect of the Eastern Transport Plan (ETP) 2035 and its impact on the Clifton Gateway scheme. My officials and their DfI counterparts are exploring potential opportunities for the joint delivery of public realm schemes and infrastructure projects in the future. This approach includes the possibility of developing the Clifton Gateway alongside Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2.	At present, there is no confirmed timescale for delivery of the public realm scheme, as it remains subject to the conclusion of the ongoing discussions with DfI.Ulster University is not currently involved in discussions between DfC and DfI. However, given its location the university will be engaged as a key stakeholder when discussions with DfI are concluded and a way forward has been established.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department works alongside local councils to plan for winter gritting services, to ensure maximum impact for unsafe areas.
answer_text: The Department provides gritting salt under existing agreements with a number of councils, for the removal of snow and ice from town centre footways and pedestrian areas. These agreements are activated during prolonged periods of severe weather.Priority is given to the provision of salt for the treatment of the gritted road network, after which the Department makes salt available to local Councils for the treatment of town centre footways and pedestrian areas. This agreement is for salt supplies in addition to stocks held by Councils for the treatment of council facilities.Salt bins and grit piles are also provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, but which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. These will be found at locations which meet certain criteria such as gradient, topography, proximity to salted network, etc.These grit piles and salt boxes are filled in advance of Winter and are regularly refilled by my Winter Service staff. Members of the public can also request refills via NI Direct.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 36508/22-27, whether there is an obligation to offer all parents a clinician, either medical or allied health professional, appointment following an autism diagnosis.
answer_text: There is no obligation to offer all parents a clinician appointment, either medical or allied health professional, following an autism diagnosis.As advised in AQW 36508/22-27, the assessing Clinician will make appropriate onward referrals if indicated based on the individual needs identified during the assessment. Onward referral may be to Occupational Therapy (OT), Speech and Language Therapy (SLT) and Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any criteria required for winter road gritting services in communities.
answer_text: My Department targets the limited resources available on the busier main through routes, as per the current winter service policy. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted, and provides action where it is most needed and most-effective.In addition to gritting this 28% Primary Salting Network, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, but which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. Where possible, once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is secured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion. The full policy can be viewed on the Department’s website below:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022	It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed, and I would urge motorists to drive with caution, take the ever-changing weather conditions into account and leave extra time for their journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is taking to ensure service users of day centres are able to avail of the spaces available to them, in particular in relation to any transport issues.
answer_text: My officials have liaised with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust regarding the transport issues impacting Day Centre provision. The Belfast Trust advise that they are working to address the issue of driver shortages. The Belfast Trust report that the Transport Management Team in conjunction with the Trust’s Day Care Management Team assess driver availability on a daily basis and reorganise and prioritise the service needs.Contingency arrangements also continue with the use of taxis as and where appropriate. However, I understand that these contingency arrangements are not suitable for everyone. To address the shortage of drivers, the Regional Transport Managers Forum meets on a regular basis throughout the year to agree how best to address driver shortages and vacant posts have been continually advertised across all HSCTs via BSO. HSCTs have also initiated driver development programs which are designed to upskill existing Trust staff and to pay for them to undertake their Full category D driving test.  Drivers are required to undergo Continual Professional Competency (CPC) training to maintain their ability to carry passengers, HSCTs arrange and pay for this training. In addition, the Belfast Trust reports that it is in early engagement with Belfast City Council on a structured recruitment and training programme for drivers, to address the shortage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when the needs assessment of ADHD services will be published.
answer_text: The final ADHD needs assessment report was received by the Department at the end of November 2025.  The report is currently being considered by officials, with a view to identifying next steps and arranging publication thereafter. The expectation is that the report will be published in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement his Department has had with GP representative bodies regarding shared care arrangements, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Regional Group on Specialist Medicines was established by my Department and has recommended that medicines suitable for shared care arrangements be designated to an advisory list, the Amber List. The Amber List is an advisory list where it is considered by the Regional Group, that responsibility for prescribing may be transferred from secondary to primary care when shared care arrangements for the patient have been established between the specialist and GP.  Such shared care arrangements will make recommendations on the respective clinical responsibilities of both parties. The Regional Group consider which amber medicines require a regional shared care guideline to be commissioned to cover arrangements across Northern Ireland.There are a number of GP Local Medical Committee (LMC) representatives who are members of the Regional Group for Specialist Medicines.  One of the roles of this group is to ratify the Shared Care Guidelines developed for the region.The Regional Group for Specialist Medicines meets four times a year and the Group last met on 2 December 2025.  Shared Care Guidelines for ratification are a standing item on the Agenda for every meeting of the Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rational as to why strokes have been downgraded from Category 1 to Category 2 in Northern Ireland Ambulance Service response mapping.
answer_text: My Department can confirm that there has been no downgrading of stroke calls within the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) response categorisation.Recent updates to categorisation have been made to improve recognition of more subtle signs of stroke, including loss of balance or coordination and onset of vision, reading and writing problems, beyond the more typical symptoms of facial weakness, limb weakness and sudden speech problems.Most 999 calls for acute stroke are categorised as Category 2, in line with practice across most of the UK and Ireland. This reflects their position as emergency calls which are serious but not immediately life-threatening, placing them on a par with conditions such as myocardial infraction (heart attack) and serious trauma. Category 2 calls are responded to as emergency calls and have a target response time of 18 minutes.Calls for patients with suspected stroke may receive a Category 3 or be passed for secondary clinical triage if symptoms have been present beyond the effective treatment window or if no clinical evidence of acute stroke is found during triage. Category 3 calls have a target response time of 1–2 hours.Category 1 is reserved for immediately life-threatening situations requiring urgent intervention, such as cardiac arrest, choking and anaphylaxis, with a target response time of 8 minutes. Stroke patients will be upgraded to Category 1 if they present with priority symptoms such as severe breathing difficulty, loss of consciousness or persistent fitting.The importance of timely ambulance responses for patient outcomes was highlighted in the ‘Getting It Right First Time’ (GIRFT) Review of Emergency Medicine. Ambulance handover delays at emergency departments (EDs) significantly impact NIAS’s ability to respond to emergency calls in the community, making improvements in handover times a key priority for my Department. NIAS receives over 200,000 calls annually and dispatches crews to approximately 170,000 incidents.At a regional workshop in August 2025, attended by the Permanent Secretary, Chief Medical Officer and Chief Nursing Officer, all Trusts agreed to implement initiatives aimed at reducing delays, with a target of achieving the two-hour standard by December 2025. To support this, £10 million has been repurposed for measures such as:Expansion of Hospital at Home services to increase community-based capacity;Additional short-stay inpatient beds to facilitate timely discharge;Frailty assessments and multidisciplinary reviews at the front door of acute hospitals;Expansion of Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) and ambulatory pathways across acute medicine, respiratory, cardiology and surgical specialties; andIncreased capacity for early review teams and discharge co-ordination.NIAS is also implementing service innovations to manage calls differently and free up ambulances for the most serious cases. This includes:Operating an Integrated Clinical Hub within the Emergency Operations Centre for enhanced

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of the Endometriosis Clinical Pathway Group, established as part of the Getting It Right First Time regional group.
answer_text: There is currently significant work underway across the health and care system to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology-specific conditions, such as endometriosis, within each Trust across Northern Ireland.My Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, which includes representation from Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioners. This forum is responsible for overseeing service improvements and the development of gynaecology pathways at primary, intermediate, secondary, and tertiary care levels. It also monitors the implementation of the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report.An Endometriosis Clinical Pathway Task and Finish Group has been formed to design a pathway for patients with minor, intermediate, or severe diagnoses. This pathway will ensure services meet the needs of women at different stages of the condition and will be standardised across Northern Ireland to minimise duplication of services. In February 2025, Trusts established a Gynaecology Provider Collaborative to support this work. Working with the Provider Collaborative, my Department aims to build stakeholder confidence and momentum by maximising capacity and addressing the longest gynaecology waiting lists, while simultaneously developing a sustainable, evidence-based service model at both Trust and regional levels.The Elective Care Framework has provided additional recurrent funding to address gaps in gynaecology services. My officials and Trusts continue to explore options to strengthen pathways and improve care for women with gynaecological conditions. Some recommendations will require investment, while others can be achieved through modernisation and reform.I have committed funding to develop endometriosis services across the region. My officials are working with Trusts and the Provider Collaborative to develop business cases for two regional centres in Northern Ireland, alongside enhanced local provision within each of the five Trusts. This approach ensures patients at all levels of severity are managed appropriately. The new pathway will also incorporate recently introduced NICE-approved drugs.  My officials will continue to explore options to strengthen pathways and improve care for women affected by endometriosis and other gynaecological conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will ensure that GPs and other healthcare professionals will have adequate training around the symptoms and diagnosis of endometriosis as part of the Women's Health Action Plan.
answer_text: Clinical education on endometriosis for healthcare professionals is part of the work of the clinical pathway group taking forward implementation of Gynae GIRFT, being led by SPPG. In addition, a Women’s Health Action Plan being developed will reflect the work that is ongoing in relation to gynaecological health, including endometriosis, and will highlight the importance of clinical education for GPs and others on various women’s health conditions.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of additional GPs that will be delivered as a result of commitments made to support practices to increase their capacity in his Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025-26.
answer_text: As in previous years, General Practice will continue to play a crucial role in helping to manage additional service demand and to keep people safe this winter. As independent contractors, GPs are responsible for identifying and recruiting the number of GPs and other staff required to deliver services. This winter, funding totalling £3.4m has been identified from within the overall General Medical Services 2025/26 budget to support GP services with the increased demand over the winter period. The Northern Ireland Local Enhanced Service Managing Winter Pressures for 2025/26 enables practices to put plans into place to manage the increase in demand and to provide additional direct clinical patient care over the winter period.  This enhanced service aims to:  Help practices cope with the increase in demand over the Winter 	period; Reduce Winter pressures on unscheduled care by increasing availability of patient care in GP practices through telephone & video consultations as well as direct face to face appointments where necessary; andEnsure practices can manage demand appropriately after bank 	holiday closures.My Department will also provide additional support from within existing budgets to assist GP practices across Northern Ireland to deliver proactive support and care to those in nursing and residential care homes through completing medical care plans, including assessing whether referral to an ED is needed, and a GP-led fortnightly proactive review. As a result of this expenditure, we anticipate the delivery of over 8,000 additional sessions to help manage winter pressures in General Practice and around 10,000 medical care plans for patients in nursing and residential care homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans for the role of GPs in providing endometriosis care.
To ask the Minister of Health whether there is a support pathway for families and young people following a diagnosis of autism.
answer_text: Within the Health and Social Care Trusts, Paediatric Autism services currently offer post diagnostic support including personalised needs-based care plans. At the end of the assessment process for Autism, parents and children/young people depending upon their age and level of understanding will have a clinical consultation which will include discussion around post-diagnostic support and intervention for ASD. The assessing Clinician may also refer to other services; based on the individual needs identified during the assessment; for example, Occupational Therapy (OT), Speech and Language Therapy (SLT) and Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS). While Trusts have developed a range of post-diagnostic supports within their existing resources, implementing a universal, standardised package is not feasible without regional commissioning, recurring investment, and multi-agency alignment. My Department has issued the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework for public consultation. The Framework endorses an inclusive, needs-based approach to neurodiversity designed to help reduce stigma and the perceived need to obtain a diagnosis in order to access support through Health and Education services. The Framework will include options for support at both pre and post assessment intervals whether a diagnosis is confirmed or not. Response to the consultation has been very positive and officials are currently considering the feedback on offer.My Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. Any decision to commission services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the pedestrian crossing at Prince Charles Way roundabout, at the junction towards the Church Road and O'Neill Road, will be complete.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.I can confirm that a full site survey and assessment has now been completed at the roundabout on Prince Charles Way close to Ashgrove Primary School and a score for the site has been calculated. The scoring for this site has indicated that it would be considered to be high priority within the Antrim and Newtownabbey council area.The site will remain on the Department’s prioritised list for inclusion in a future works programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures which can be taken to protect properties from recurrent car crashes and damage, due to being located on a junction.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.  At locations where recurrent vehicles collisions are known to occur, my officials undertake an assessment of the site and consider a range of road safety engineering measures that may minimise the risk in line with current policy guidance RSPPG E027 – ‘Road Safety Engineering Procedures.  Schemes are then assessed and prioritised for progression against other regional safety priorities within the available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of managed budgets across the Health and Social Care Trusts that currently provide them.
answer_text: My Department partnered with the Association for Real Change and IMPACT, Birmingham University, to complete a 12-month review of the current barriers to implementation of Managed Budgets in Northern Ireland. answer_text: Below are the details of the number and location of Changing Places facilities provided by each Health and Social Care Trust.    Northern Health and Social Care Trust  The NHSCT has two Changing Places facilities with one located at Antrim Hospital and one within the Ballymena Health & Care Centre.     South Eastern Health and Care Trust  The SEHSCT has one Changing Places facility within the Lisburn Primary and Community Care Centre located on the Lagan Valley Hospital Site.   Southern Health and Social Care Trust  The SHSCT has two fully compliant Changing Places facilities with one located at the Banbridge CTCC site and one located at the Blossom Paediatrics building at Craigavon Area Hospital.     Belfast Health and Social Care Trust  The BHSCT does not yet have a Changing Place facility in any of its acute hospital sites.     Western Health and Social Care Trust   No response received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to ensure that fully compliant Changing Places facilities are available within each hospital site.
answer_text: Since June 2022 amendments to building regulations requires that all new build hospitals and primary care building and those whole hospital or primary care buildings formed by a material change of use of existing facilities should provide at least one Changing Places facility. In light of the severe financial challenges facing the Northern Ireland Health & Social Care (HSC) sector, as well as the competing demands on the capital budget, my Department has no plans to centrally fund the provision of Changing Places facilities in any existing hospitals or health centres where currently not provided. HSC Trusts are however provided with general capital funding monies and may consider the provision of these facilities from this allocation based on their own assessment of need and options available. Hospitals within Northern Ireland currently provide public ambulant toilets and wheelchair accessible toilets, which allow a wheelchair user to use the facilities independently. In acute and community hospitals there will be comprehensive provision of hoisting and toileting facilities available to provide for their inpatient needs. In the case where a Changing Places facility is not available for an attending outpatient member of the public, the Trust may be able to make available a suitable alternative arrangement for an individual if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of empty homes in North Belfast.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) does not maintain a register of 'empty homes'. The number of domestic properties (excluding Caravans, Domestic Garages, and Domestic Stores) in the Belfast North Parliamentary Constituency at 30 September 2025 that are recorded as vacant in the rating system is 965. As rates on domestic properties is charged at 100% irrespective of whether the property is occupied or not, ratepayers may not inform Land & Property services of a property becoming vacant, therefore these figures may not reflect accurately an ‘empty homes’ position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how he plans to address the waiting list for children’s autism services.
answer_text: There has been a significant increase in the number of children being referred for an autism assessment and regrettably Autism Services do not currently have the capacity to meet this growing area of need. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. It is clear that additional investment in autism services is required if we are to reduce waiting lists and I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding. In May 2025, My Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework. The Framework was issued for public consultation and this exercise concluded on 26th September 2025. This Framework proposes an approach to neurodiversity which is inclusive and aligned to the needs of children and their families. The importance of diagnostic processes should not be underestimated, however the requirement to obtain a formal diagnosis as a pre-cursor to the offer of support and assistance consigns many children to lengthy periods of time spent in anticipation of support and intervention which would have been more appropriately offered at an earlier stage. The approach proposed will place more emphasis upon earlier intervention irrespective of the outcome of the formal diagnostic process. The Department is currently developing a regionally consistent neurodevelopmental pathway. This work will also assist in evaluating service capacity, scoping out workforce requirements and clarify areas for transformational investment. It is clearly necessary to extend the parameters of this work beyond the confines of the Department of Health and into the Education domain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on Belfast Streets Ahead phase in the north of the city centre
answer_text: My department continues to progress Phase 3 of the Belfast Streets Ahead (BSA) Public Realm programme in the north of the city centre.  The project has, however, been significantly impacted by the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) plans for the next phase of the Belfast Rapid Transit (BRT2) system and by the development of Eastern Transport Plan 2035 (ETP 2035).	My officials are currently liaising with their counterparts in DfI to gain a full understanding of the impacts of BRT2 and ETP 2035 and to explore the potential for joint delivery of BSA Phase 3 alongside BRT2.		Coordinating our approaches could allow both projects to progress in tandem, subject to feasibility and funding, which would minimise nugatory work and disruption for residents, businesses, and road users, while maximising the impact and value of public investment.		There is currently no confirmed timescale for the delivery of BSA Phase 3 in the north of the city centre and this will likely remain the case until discussions with DfI conclude.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Clifton Gateway Public Realm Scheme, given the Minister of Infrastructure has named the Antrim Road as the main route for the Belfast Rapid Transport project.
answer_text: The Minister for Infrastructure’s confirmation that the Antrim Road will be the preferred route for Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2 (BRT2) has provided some clarity regarding the Clifton Gateway Public Realm Scheme.Officials are currently engaging with counterparts in the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) to explore potential opportunities for joint delivery of the Clifton Gateway improvements alongside BRT2 infrastructure, recognising the strategic significance of this corridor.Subject to feasibility and funding, a coordinated approach could allow both projects to progress in tandem, minimising disruption for residents, businesses, and road users, while maximising the impact and value of public investment.	At present, there is no confirmed timescale for delivery of the Clifton Gateway scheme, as it remains subject to the conclusion of ongoing discussions with DfI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following media reports on 29 September 2025, whether the newly-announced children's residential home on the Derry/Donegal border run by Ann's Care Group will be private for-profit.
answer_text: Officials within my department have inquired with the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) regarding new registrations for Children’s Homes in the Western Trust area. At this time there have been no applications from the provider mentioned in various media outlets on 29 September 2025.  My Department will continue to liaise with RQIA in relation to new applications for registration in the children’s social care sector in Northern Ireland.While currently it is possible for a for-profit operation to register as a provider of a children’s home in Northern Ireland, only a small number have done so.  At 30 June 2024, there were 55 Children’s Residential Homes in Northern Ireland, 46 homes were statutory and 9 were independent sector providers, including providers operating on a not-for-profit and for-profit basis.  Statutory homes provided 268 places at an average of six places per home; independent homes provided 27 places at an average of three per home.An Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services in Northern Ireland was published in June 2023. That Review made a recommendation not to allow the privatisation of the care of children. In its response to the Review, the Department partially accepted that recommendation, recognising the need to keep private sector provision of children’s social care to a minimum, but also acknowledging the small number of for-profit providers currently operating here and the increasing pressure on services. My Department has established a programme of reform which is leading on the implementation of a number of the Review’s recommendations.As shown in the table below, the number of children in care in Northern Ireland has increased year on year.  My Department will continue to strive to ensure that children in care receive the best package of care available to them.31 March 20193,28131 March 20203,38331 March 20213,53031 March 20223.62431 March 20233,80131 March 20243,999My Department publishes Official Statistics on the number of children in care in the annual report ‘Children’s Social Care Statistics for Northern Ireland’ (Children’s social care statistics for Northern Ireland 2023/24 | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk).  The next release of these Official Statistics for year ending 31 March 2025 is due later this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any departmental or Education Authority guidance regarding the use of the removal of play, or outdoor time, as a disciplinary measure in schools, particularly primary schools.
answer_text: Article 3 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 as amended by Article 19 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 requires a school to have a discipline policy which outlines the behaviour it expects from pupils and the sanctions it will impose for breaches in discipline. The department has produced guidance on this issue entitled "Pastoral Care in Schools: Promoting Positive Behaviour" which sets out how a school should develop a discipline/positive behaviour policy using a whole school approach and in consultation with pupils and parents. The guidance notes that sanctions are an integral part of a school’s behaviour policy which help to uphold the rules and procedures, provide pupils with the security of clearly-defined boundaries and, in so doing, encourage appropriate and acceptable behaviour. The guidance highlights that it is important to ensure that pupils are fully aware of the conditions which will lead, in all likelihood, to the imposition of sanctions, and gives examples of sanctions, which includes restriction of access to extra-curricular facilities or activities for a period. In relation to this restriction specifically, the guidance notes that care should be taken to ensure that any restriction of a pupil’s access to facilities, activities or services does not detrimentally affect his or her access to the curriculum or place the pupil at an educational disadvantage in comparison with his/her peers.Responsibility for discipline within schools rests with the school principal and its Board of Governors (BoG) and they should promote positive behaviour among pupils attending the school to safeguard their welfare and facilitate their educational progress at school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the impact of the decision to halt the progression of the children's ward at Altnagelvin Area Hospital.
answer_text: There has been no decision to halt the progression of the Altnagelvin Paediatrics Unit project.  The future funding profile for the project will be considered as part of the development of the DoH Capital Investment Plan which is dependent upon the outcome of the NI Executive Budget 2026 process.  Paediatric services continue to be delivered at Altnagelvin Area Hospital, however, I recognise the importance of the improvements in the quality of care that will be delivered by the new facility and would hope that the project can be taken forward with as little delay as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his Department's priority list of capital projects; and (ii) whether that priority list is based on patient outcome.
answer_text: The constrained financial position facing the Department of Health (DoH) means that priority in the short to medium term is afforded to those projects which have already started, or which have made significant progress through the approvals process and are considered inescapable.  All capital projects funded by DoH are expected to contribute to the aims and objectives of the department with the overall mission being to improve the health and social well-being of the people of Northern Ireland.  Below is the list of the DoH capital projects that are either in implementation, there is some form of a contractual commitment or are required for the ongoing functioning of Health & Social Care (HSC) services.New Maternity Hospital, Royal Group of Hospitals (RGH)New Children's Hospital, RGHSite Infrastructure Works, RGHNIFRS Learning and Development Centre Strabane Health and Care Centre (City Deal) Altnagelvin Medical Education, Inclusive Futures Funding (IFF)WHSCT R&D Expansion (IFF)Glenmona Replacement Intensive Support Unit Regional Haematology Treatment Ward Midwifery Led Unit - Antrim Area Hospital Antrim Area Hospital Electrical InfrastructureDaisy Hill Hospital Low Voltage WorksLisnaskea Health Care Centre Congenital Heart Disease Professorship Academic Programme Managed Equipment Service (MES) ContractMcCloud Pension SystemEncompassNorthern Ireland Laboratory Information Management System (NILIMS)Northern Ireland Picture Archiving and Communications System Replacement (NIPACS+) - BSOBloodPatEquip Digital Identity ServiceGeneral Medical Services ICTVision Migration of GP practicesCushendall Fire Station Fintona Fire StationAltnagelvin Paediatrics Unit Newry Community Care and Treatment Centre Cityside Health and Care Centre Regional Radio-pharmacy FacilityGlenmona Separated Minors Unit Imaging AcademyMother and Baby Unit (Peri-Natal Mental Health)Craigavon Area Hospital Redevelopment- Initial PhaseAntrim Area Hospital Mental Health Inpatient Unit South Eastern Mental Health UnitCentre for Mental Health, OmaghAltnagelvin Emergency DepartmentIn addition to these specific projects, a significant proportion of the capital programme relates to maintaining existing services. Included in this category is the ongoing need to invest in medical equipment, the HSC and Northern Ireland Fire & Rescue Service fleet, ICT, smaller estates schemes and backlog maintenance. This also includes HSC R&D, ongoing investment in GP practices through the GP Improvement Scheme and to facilitate the rollout of Multi-Disciplinary Teams. This category of expenditure is deemed as inescapable given its importance in maintaining service delivery and managing risks to infrastructure failure. Although capital investment within DoH is diverse in nature, almost all of these projects have been proposed and progressed as they are expected to contribute to improving the supporting infrastructure which results in improved public health, patient outcomes or public safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who received robotic-assisted surgery, in 2023-2024, broken down by gender.
answer_text: Robotic-assisted surgery is a surgical model of care which is being adopted by many surgical specialities and was introduced into Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in 2018 within the specialty of Urology. Other surgical specialties, including Gynaecology and Colorectal, are now in a similar position to Urology in considering utilising Robotic-assisted surgery.  In 2023/24, 242 robotic assisted surgery procedures were completed by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, with 198 male patients and 44 female patients.  The majority of procedures were completed within the speciality of Urology.  In 2023/24 South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust completed 8 procedures, with 3 male and 5 female patients.  This service is delivered in the Ulster Independent Clinic, by a South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust Colorectal Surgeon who is trained in robotic assisted surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans to change the number of beds for inpatient care in Lakewood secure care unit.
answer_text: Lakewood Secure Care Unit is commissioned to provide care for up to sixteen young people aged 13-17 from across the five HSC Trusts in NI.The number of placements in use at a point in time is dependent on the assessment process which determines how safety and quality can be maintained while accommodating some of the regions most vulnerable and at-risk children and young people. Due to the competing needs of children and young people there are times when the placement capacity will not be utilised to its full function in order to ensure that the needs of the children remain paramount. There are no plans to increase the amount of commissioned secure beds in the region however there is extensive work being explored as to how Northern Ireland can improve its Secure Care portfolio. (i) The number of current places occupied within Lakewood Secure Care Centre is 8. (ii) The number of placements that are not being currently utilised is 8. When young people who have a greater complexity of need are admitted to Lakewood, this requires a higher level of staffing support.  In response, and to ensure the safety of everyone within the Centre, SEHSCT have at times reduced the capacity of Lakewood.  This is one of those times. While there was a consistent provision of placements for some considerable time, this has more recently been reduced to 8 beds, reflective of a temporary reduction in staffing capacity which has arisen following serious assaults on staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a 20mph speed limit zone can be installed at St Mary's on the Hill Primary School, Carmoney Road, Newtownabbey, particularly given the lack of a current crossing patrol in the area.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North and are listed on my Department’s website, link; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3.This will bring the total delivered across the North since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.While the Tranche 3 programme focuses on schools on rural roads where the national speed limit applies, I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas, like Hill Primary School, which would also benefit from a reduction in speed limits. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.My Department is also currently carrying out a review on a number of speed related matters including 20mph speed limits in general and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review.  Further consideration on the use of part time speed limits at schools will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will reconsider the installation of a pedestrian crossing at the Prince Charles Way roundabout, Belfast, where cars leave the junction towards the Church Road and O'Neill's Road, for the safety of children crossing to attend the local primary school, given another pedestrian crossing was installed at the O'Neill Road roundabout where cars exit towards Church Road.
answer_text: Following the procedures set out in RSPPG E005 Controlled Crossings: Assessment Procedures, officials from my Department completed an initial survey and assessment at Prince Charles Way roundabout for the provision of a pedestrian crossing. The survey found that the site surpassed the minimum score required to proceed to a detailed survey and assessment. I have been advised that the detailed survey and assessment will take place during the 2025/26 school year. Should the results from this survey indicate that a crossing is required, the site will be added to a prioritised list of sites that have met the criteria for the provision of a pedestrian crossing. My officials will inform you of the results of the detailed survey and assessment when completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 (the Act) received Royal Assent in April 2022.  The Act is substantial with many provisions inter-linked.  Extensive public consultation and approximately twenty-five pieces of subordinate legislation and accompanying guidance will be required to fully implement the Act. Following Royal Assent, a detailed phasing schedule was developed to support implementation over a five-year period from 2022 to 2027, subject to necessary Ministerial approvals, secured budgets and social care workforce capacity.  Phase 1 actions were implemented within the initial 2022/2023 timeframe.  However, it was not possible to proceed with the Phase 2 actions in 2023/2024 due to significant budgetary and social care workforce constraints.  As a result, those provisions which could be implemented on the basis that there were no additional resource implications were identified. The remaining Phase 2 provisions were delayed until 2024/2025.Whilst work has now resumed, the timescales for implementation of the Act will depend on the provision of additional and sustained funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the £2.5 million earmarked to mitigate the national insurance rise for General Dental Services.
answer_text: The £2.5 will be allocated to Dental Practices in proportion to the level of Health Service work they do. The payments will follow a similar schedule to Practice Allowance which is paid twice each year in November and April, based on Health Service earnings in the preceding six months.Officials are working with the Business Service Organisation on the operational details of the proposed method of allocation. Once finalised these details will be shared with the British Dental Association in advance of the first payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the dentistry cost of service review, including (i) the experts who will be undertaking the review; and (ii) a timeline.
answer_text: Professor Ciaran O’Neill has been appointed to carry out the General Dental Service Cost of Service Review.Prof. O’Neill has the necessary experience and expertise to carry out this specialist piece of work, being Professor of Health Economics within the Centre for Public Health in Queens University Belfast and having completed a review of the Northern Ireland General Dental Service contract in 2008.The review is due to be completed by March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the additional £1.6 million payment announced in May 2025 as part of the dentistry investment proposals.

To ask the Minister of Health, following the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration Fifty Third Report: 2025, to detail any engagement he has had with UK counterparts regarding the recommendation to develop a UK-wide Index of Dental Costs.
answer_text: I have had no formal engagement with UK counterparts on the development of an Index of Dental Costs.A General Dental Service Cost of Service Review is being undertaken, with expenses being included within the scope. This recommendation from the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration Fifty Third Report will be considered following the results of this review and in line with the reform of General Dental Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 22008/22-27, for an update on the cost benefit analysis for the use of robotic assisted surgery across other specialties, including gynaecology.
answer_text: Under the General Surgery Network remit, Robotic Assisted Surgery has been identified as a key priority to be taken forward.Work is underway with key stakeholders to develop a regional pan specialism implementation plan for Northern Ireland.  Evidence and data are currently being gathered to support this work through a number of workshops held. In addition to Urology, other specialisms of Gynaecology, Colorectal and Thoracic surgery are being considered.Significant investment will be required to fully implement regional access to robotic assisted surgery over a phased timeframe.  Investment will be required for the robotic platform as well as the revenue costs. However, key drivers for change include sustainability of services; workforce recruitment and retention; training and development; clinical advantages; and better patient outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any action she is taking to address the increase in young people not in education, employment or training in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale as to why Northern Ireland has not introduced a hierarchy of road users, despite this being introduced elsewhere in the UK.
answer_text: I am aware that in Britain a substantive update to the Highway Code was made in 2022. The most significant change was the introduction of the concept of a Hierarchy of Users. The intended objective of the Hierarchy was to enhance the safety of those considered to be the most vulnerable by placing greatest responsibility on those in charge of vehicles that can cause the greatest harm in the event of a collision.	My Department considers any initiatives that can improve road safety however it is imperative that my limited resources are concentrated on areas with the greatest evidence of positive impact.  My Department published the Road Safety Strategy on 16 September 2024 and an associated Action Plan. A link to the Strategy and Action Plan is attached for your information.	Road Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland to 2030 	Evidence on the impact of the changes to the Highway Code in Britain is not yet available to demonstrate whether these have been effective. It is therefore not currently prioritised within the Road Safety Strategy Action Plan. This will be kept under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why the planned opening of Redwood has been delayed.
answer_text: The South Eastern HSC Trust has been working with an independent provider to open the Redwood facility in Belfast for Children with a Learning Disability. It had been anticipated that this facility would open in September 2025. However, due to issues pertaining to the lease of the property, and the time required to register the facility as a Children’s Home with RQIA, the opening of the home will be delayed until November 2025. There is also an estimated 6 - 8 weeks of building work required to the Redwood facility, and this work has been progressed. Upon completion of the works and necessary risk assessments, such as fire and legionella, the RQIA will then assess the Trust application to register the Building as a Registered Children’s Home. There is a natural timeframe for these processes, which will impact upon the final completion or opening date.  The Trust representatives meet on a weekly basis which has cross Directorate representation and this meeting is chaired by the Executive Director of Social Work to ensure that all of the necessary components are progressing.  The proposed opening of Redwood is on schedule for November 2025, and the Director and Assistant Director meet with the families every two weeks to keep them updated on progress. The delay to Redwood will postpone the planned availability of Residential Short Breaks in Lindsay House, which will also impact families in the Belfast HSC Trust. The South Eastern HSC Trust has expressed regret regarding this setback, and will continue to work with all families to provide interim supports during this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of the Departmental Accountability Team with regards to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As Health Committee were previously advised in June 2025 the Department had established oversight, accountability and assurance mechanisms in regards to both cardiac surgery service issues and wider culture and leadership implications for the Belfast Trust. These actions sit within the Department’s performance accountability arrangements i.e. the Support & Intervention Framework (Level 5).A programme of fortnightly meetings have been held, and to date 14 have taken place involving Departmental Officials, PHA, Trust and Independent Experts. With the next meeting broadened to include RQIA and PCC. My officials have separately met with the Vice Chair and Non Executive Directors of the Belfast Trust to outline clear expectations.  The initial focus for cardiac service improvement was the development of an interim action plan to progress recommendations from the Independent Expert Review report and subsequently the focus has moved to seeking assurance on progress made. The structure of the meetings enable constructive challenge, advice and support to the Belfast Trust drawing on input from the Independent Experts. With regards to the wider culture & leadership oversight the Trust is taking forward wider actions which are expected to be reviewed by the incoming Chief Executive. Work to date, has and will continue, to be informed by the ongoing work of one of the Independent Experts whose initial diagnostic phase is expected to close in the next couple of weeks.I receive regular updates on progress from my Departmental officials and the Independent Experts.  While good progress has been made we have all acknowledged that changes to culture will require considerable time and effort. I expect the Belfast Trust Board and Executive Team to continue to drive improvement in this matter. There has been no de-escalation from Level 5 at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline why her Department changed its definition of active travel spend between February 2024 and June 2025 and reclassified £37 million of existing expenditure as active travel spend.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he has had any engagement with his counterpart in Ireland regarding a second provider arrangement for CAR-T-cell therapy.
answer_text: In line with the Memorandum of Understanding between Ireland, Northern Ireland and the National Cancer Institute in USA, Departmental officials continue to work collaboratively with Republic of Ireland (RoI) officials and clinical officials, establishing partnerships and working to improve cancer services on an all-island basis.RoI officials have indicated that their CAR T-cell therapy service is currently operating at capacity and cannot support additional patients accessing these services from Northern Ireland on a cross-border basis at this time. To develop additional capacity would require significant capital investment and therefore take longer to implement than the proposed timeline to develop a Northern Ireland CAR T-cell therapy service. The current position where patients travel to Great Britain for CAR T-cell therapy remains the only viable option for adults from Northern Ireland requiring this treatment at this time.My Departmental officials will continue to work closely with RoI cancer policy and clinical leads on the development of  opportunities for cross-border services for new and emerging therapies and service demand consistent with the commitments of the MOU

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the challenges faced by LGBTQIA+ people in relation to domestic and intimate partner abuse.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions being taken to ensure adequate supplies of oxygen are available for both hospitals and community care settings.
answer_text: A procured Oxygen Service is currently provided by BOC Homecare Service for patients who require home treatment with oxygen in Northern Ireland. HSC Trusts and GPs access this same service. 				To ensure there are adequate supplies and to ensure the correct patients get oxygen therapy in line with their needs there is an agreed prioritisation process with Trust respiratory leads.				SPPG, together with colleagues from each of the 5 Trust areas, meet with BOC on a bi-monthly basis to discuss the contract performance in relation to the KPIs specified within the procured oxygen contract.  				SPPG have contacted BOC in response to your query and I can confirm there are no current shortages in oxygen or delays in delivery of home oxygen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures are being explored to enable patients who are both (i) ineligible for the Dental Access Scheme due to needing ongoing treatment; and (ii) unable to access private dental treatment.
answer_text: I acknowledge that there are notable challenges facing many patients, particularly those not registered with a dental practice, in accessing dental care when they need it. General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are self-employed, independent contractors who are free to choose the amount of Health Service (HS) care they provide.For many months now, my Department has focused efforts on improving access for patients with targeted investments.This includes the Dental Access Scheme (DAS) which allows unregistered patients with an urgent or pressing (but non-urgent) need to receive treatment. The need for ongoing treatment does not disqualify a patient from accessing the DAS however, that ongoing need will not be treated via the DAS. While I acknowledge that this will not cover every case, it ensures that those in the most need are able to receive the treatment they require. In June I announced a further £7m investment for 2025/26 to continue supporting the service and to improve access for patients while reform is progressed. The investment is funding the continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme and 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions, and root canals, alongside a new £1.6m allocation to provide additional support to dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care for their patients.My officials are working on further options to improve access for patients however, any decisions need to be made in the face of a £600m deficit for my Department, which severely limits my ability to implement the kind of transformative change we would all like to see.Longer term, my Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan includes a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on engagement between his Department, the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and the Royal College of Psychiatrists around the business case for a new mother and baby home.
answer_text: An earlier independent review identified that the model of the proposed 6/7 bedded Mother and Baby Unit should be that as recommended by the Royal College of Psychiatrists and the location of the Mother and Baby Unit should be on the City Hospital site, within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. The Public Health Agency have provided a Needs Analysis to derive the optimal bed number taking into account recommendations from the Royal College of Psychiatrist’s for the provision of perinatal mental health services for child bearing women https://www.rcpsych.ac.uk/docs/default-source/improving-care/better-mh-policy/college-reports/college-report-cr232---perinatal-mental-heath-services    The needs analysis proposes that a 6-bed unit is appropriate at this time. The Department have formally confirmed this position with Belfast Trust on 11 August 2025.  Belfast Trust has advised that the business case will be finalised within 6 weeks of receipt of the confirmation (22nd September).The ability to take forward the development of the Mother and Baby Unit will remain subject to the necessary funding being identified.Improving mental health services, including perinatal care, remains a key priority for me.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department's engagement with the PSNI regarding Operation Begrain.
answer_text: Ahead of the formal launch of Operation Begrain on 28 November 2023 officials from my department participated in a PSNI Information Session for External Partners on 22 November 2023.The PSNI hosted a meeting of the External Partnership Forum on 18 June 2024 which two of my officials attended, the purpose of which was to enable the PSNI to provide an update on Operation Begrain and for external partners to share updates on their own respective remits.My officials and health and social care organisations held several discussions with the PSNI leads in 2024 to agree a regional approach to an Information Data Sharing Agreement between the PSNI and Operation Begrain external partners.  An Information Sharing Agreement was signed between the Department of Health and PSNI on 11 April 2025.My Department will continue to cooperate fully with the PSNI as it progresses its investigations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on Phase Three of the Neurology Deceased Patients Review.
answer_text: Following on from my Written Ministerial Statement found at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-07/WRITTEN%20STATEMENT%20TO%20THE%20ASSEMBLY%20Neurology%20Deceased%20Patients%20Review%20%28DPR%29%20Update_0.pdf on the 9th July 2025 providing an update on the Deceased Patient Review (DPR), I can confirm that Phase 3 Group 1 reviews have begun and the RQIA anticipate receiving the individual case summary reports and overall reports from the RCP for the 18 deceased patients in this group by late summer/early autumn 2025.Phase 3 Group 2, the final Phase of the DPR, comprises 8 families who indicated they wished to proceed with a review of their deceased family member’s clinical records.   In August 2025 a Direct Award Contract (DAC) from RQIA for the Royal College of Physicians received approval and RQIA issued a commissioning letter for the Royal College of Physicians to conduct a clinical record review for the final group of eight deceased patients. It is the intention that the overall work to complete the DPR will be completed by spring/early summer 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the status of School Enhancement Programme applications for primary schools in North Belfast.
answer_text: There are two primary schools with projects announced to advance in design under the second call of the School Enhancement Programme (SEP2) in North Belfast.  The status of each is as follows: Firstly, Our Lady of Lourdes Primary School is at RIBA Stage 4 (Technical Design) and a submission from the design team is with my Department for review.  Design work on the project is completed, however, construction costs exceed the standard SEP2 funding threshold.  My officials are currently considering how to progress the project and whether it may be appropriate to exceed the SEP funding limits in this instance.Cliftonville Controlled Integrated Primary School is currently in business case development.Additionally, there are two post-primary schools announced under SEP2 in North Belfast:The first of these is St Malachy’s College, which I have previously announced to proceed to construction.  Design work has been completed on this project and construction procurement is due to commence shortly.The second post-primary SEP project in North Belfast is Belfast Royal Academy which is at RIBA Stage 2 (Concept Design).Funding continues to be available to progress SEP2 projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  However, aside from those projects which I have previously announced to proceed to construction, such as St Malachy’s College, any decision to release projects to tender for a contractor will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether NICE approve the use of Givinostat as a regular treatment for patients with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy; and (ii) what actions the Belfast Trust will take to prepare for the administration of the treatment.
answer_text: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) is currently appraising givinostat for the treatment of Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy in people aged six and over.  A final decision and guidance is currently expected to be published by NICE on 18 September 2025.  The Belfast Trust has considered the additional capacity that would be required to administer givinostat and I have requested that the Trust put plans in place to enable implementation to proceed at pace in the event of a positive NICE recommendation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on efforts to provide Givinostat through the Early Access Programme.
answer_text: Compassionate use schemes such as Early Access Programmes (EAP) permit health providers to make new licensed treatments available to patients free of charge while they are being assessed for clinical and cost-effectiveness by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE).  However, these schemes do not provide any additional resources which are often required to safely administer the drug and monitor patients, as is the case with givinostat.Whilst it is an operational decision for Health and Social Care Trusts to participate in an EAP and to identify the additional resources required prior to a recommendation by NICE, all our Health and Social Care Trusts are constrained by the severe budgetary pressures currently facing the Health and Social Care (HSC) system and the urgent need to reduce costs.It is my understanding that in light of this, it has not been possible for the Belfast Trust to identify the additional funding that would be required to support the EAP for givinostat.However, I am acutely aware of the time-sensitive need to access givinostat promptly, and so I have requested that the Belfast Trust put plans in place to enable implementation to proceed at pace in the event of a positive recommendation by NICE this Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the reason for the discrepancy in cost for Givinostat between the Belfast Trust and figures provided by Oxford University Hospitals.
answer_text: At present Givinostat is not commissioned within Northern Ireland, as its introduction relies upon the NICE appraisal process, where a decision is expected to be announced in September 2025.  The costs provided by Oxford University Hospital relate to the availability of Givinostat within the Early Access Programme (EAP) arrangements, which are a direct relationship between the Trust and the Pharmaceutical company pending regulatory deliberations, and therefore outside normal NHS commissioning arrangements. Belfast Trust staff have advised that costs indicated represent the costs for full implementation if and when NICE approval comes through and have been used as a guide in responding to any costs queries. As the costs of the two organisations are not directly comparable the explanation of any apparent discrepancy is not considered as an appropriate way forward.In the event that the NICE appraisal recommends Givinostat, my officials will engage with Belfast Trust to seek to introduce the medicine for NI patients, in a timely and cost-effective manner paying regard to existing staffing and infrastructure needs of the Belfast Trust Paediatric Neurology Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will ensure that intersex people are included in the provision of gender-based healthcare, ensuring that alignment to either the male or female sex does not exclude them from accessing the required healthcare.
answer_text: The Department of Health is committed to ensuring that all individuals, including those who are intersex, have equitable access to appropriate and high-quality healthcare services. We recognise that gender-based healthcare must be inclusive and responsive to the needs of people whose biological characteristics do not align strictly with binary definitions of male or female.Our policies are guided by the principles of equality and non-discrimination, as set out under Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998. We are working with stakeholders, including healthcare professionals and advocacy groups, to improve awareness and service provision that reflects the diversity of patient needs. This includes ensuring that clinical pathways, data collection, and service design are inclusive of intersex people.The Department remains committed to fostering a healthcare system that respects and supports the dignity and rights of all individuals, regardless of sex characteristics.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans the Department has to develop a policy on meanwhile use of vacant government buildings by the arts sector.
answer_text: The Department of Finance is responsible for the NICS office estate and so would be responsible for any policy on meanwhile use. The strategic capital needs of the arts and wider heritage sectors is one of the issues being considered in the context the development of a new arts policy under the Heritage, Culture and Creativity Programme that I established in July last year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider reducing the speed limit on the O’Neill Road, Newtownabbey, given the location of the Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2 route and the access to the (i) local park for residents; (ii) local recycling centre; and (iii) the Northern Ireland Children's Hospice for patients and their families.
answer_text: My Department’s policy for setting the appropriate speed limit for a road aims to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads taking into account factors such as: the characteristic of the road; the speed of existing traffic; the degree of frontage development; and road traffic collision data, which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI.The current speed limits on the O’Neill Road are in line with our policy document and considered to be appropriate.However, I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominantly residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.  I have also asked that consideration be also given to the national speed limits that apply on rural roads and HGV speed limits.To ensure that other’s views are considered in the setting of speed limits here, I plan to hold a public consultation exercise later this year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the historical clerical child abuse research study.
answer_text: We recognise the devastating impact that clerical child abuse has had on many people. Throughout this process the Junior Ministers have engaged with victims and survivors to hear their stories and their views on what is needed. We are very grateful to all those who have participated in the process and shared their experiences. Work has now completed on three separate pieces of research: one examining records held by faith organisations and statutory agencies; one recording experiences and expectations of victims and survivors; and one examining the safeguarding policies and practices of faith groups. We understand we will shortly receive the research reports and recommendations from the Interdepartmental Working Group. We will take time to reflect on these and carefully consider the next steps.
