To ask the Minister of Education for an update on placements for children with a statement of Special Educational Needs for September 2025.
answer_text: Ensuring that all children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) have a school placement that is appropriate to meet their needs, is and will continue to be, a primary focus for my department and the Education Authority (EA).Since 2021, 595 specialist provision classes in 229 schools have been established but the system has still struggled to keep pace with the rising demand.  In January the EA advised that its early planning assumptions for September 2025 showed a need for approximately 1300 additional places, with similar increased geographical pressure areas as last year. Prior to Easter, Senior EA officials took forward an intensive round of engagements with those schools in areas with the greatest demand for capacity in September 2025. The EA has worked hard to increase capacity across the school estate, and as of 16 June 2025, it advises that there is an assumed pressure of around 237 places remaining for September 2025. On 10 June last year, I reported that the number of places required for children in either a Special School or Specialist Provision was 470 for September 2024. In order to address these challenges, in April 2024, I announced the establishment of a SEN Capital Programme, which marked a significant step change in the approach to SEN capital investment and ensured that the system would be put on a stronger strategic footing across future years. Further I wrote to a number of schools in particular areas of need in early June and the EA has been following up with these schools to ascertain their ability and willingness to establish specialist provision for September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the impact of the Europe-wide supply issue of Creon on patients.
answer_text: Medicines are manufactured as part of a highly complex, regulated and global industry; therefore, medicine shortages are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons. Whilst supply issues can’t always be prevented, there are well-established tools and processes to mitigate risks to patients which are agreed UK-wide and locally via medicine shortage management groups.Although Creon® preparations remain available, my Department is aware that the quantities are limited and capped across the UK due to manufacturing constraints until January 2026, which may result in some localised and sporadic supply disruptions.Consequently, several of the well-established tools and processes have been implemented to help mitigate the impact of these disruptions on patients across the UK, including Northern Ireland. This has included providing healthcare professionals with advice and actions to take via National Patient Safety Alerts and recommendations to utilise the services available on the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service (SPS) website, including the SPS medicines supply tool. Additionally, Creon® and other pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) products have been added to the restricted export and hoarding list, Serious Shortages Protocols have been issued to community pharmacies for Creon® 10,000 and 25,000 capsules and unlicensed imports have been sourced from other markets.Any patients impacted by this supply disruption should contact a healthcare professional for advice on actions to take.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an outline of the forthcoming legislation on Truth Recovery and Redress for Mother and Baby Institutions.
answer_text: We met with a delegation of victims and survivors from the Consultation Forum in advance of the introduction of the Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill on 16 June 2025. 	The Bill completed its Second Stage on 24 June 2025 and began its Committee Stage the following day.This Bill represents a significant milestone – it will help to support truth, acknowledgement and accountability for victims and survivors.  This legislation will establish an independent statutory inquiry with significant powers, and secondly, it will provide an initial redress payment to thousands of victims and survivors.This is a complex and sensitive piece of legislation, with many constraints and often differing views to balance in the decision-making process.  There has been constructive engagement with the Executive Office Committee, and we look forward to supporting them in their important scrutiny role. We appreciate the Member has been involved in this work, and we would be happy for our officials to provide a dedicated briefing on the legislation.

To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has assessed whether the £27.19 per week Personal Expenses Allowance for residents living in residential care is sufficient.
answer_text: The Personal Expenses Allowance (PEA) rate is specified in the annual Charging for Residential Accommodation Guide (CRAG) circular. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail who is the regulatory body for sonographers.
answer_text: There is no dedicated regulatory body for sonographers. The title ‘sonographer’ is not a protected title. The current model in Northern Ireland is that ultrasound is within the scope of practice of a range of professions including obstetricians and gynaecologists who are regulated by the General Medical Council (GMC), Midwives who are regulated by the Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC), and radiographers who are regulated by the Health and Care Professions Council (HCPC). There are no restrictions on someone trained in ultrasound/sonography using the job title ‘sonographer.’ However, a person is not statutorily registered as a sonographer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the regulatory requirements for sonographers.
answer_text: There is no separate statutory registration of sonographers in the United Kingdom (UK) and ‘sonographer’ is not a protected title.  In practice, sonographers come from a background of midwifery or radiography. Those sonographers who are midwives will be required to hold a midwifery qualification recognised in the UK and must be registered with the Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC).  Those who are radiographers will require a professional radiography qualification recognised in the UK and must be registered with the Health and Care Professions Council (HCPC). Medical staff undertaking ultrasound procedures are registered with General Medical Council (GMC).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the regulatory arrangements for the independent sector.

To ask the Minister of Health, given the complex nature of needs of individuals housed by the Cedar Foundation in The Mews and following the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority Failure to Comply Notice issued on 17 April 2025, to detail what work social services and the learning disability units within (i) his Department; and (ii) the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, are taking to ensure residents needs, both medically and socially, are met.
answer_text: The Mews Supported Living Service is registered with and subject to regulation by the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA). It is the responsibility of RQIA to assure the quality of services provided by my Department, statutory and independent sector bodies.It is RQIA’s responsibility to ensure that every aspect of care reaches the standards set by my Department and expected by the public.RQIA issued two failure to comply notices and a notice of proposal in respect of new admissions to The Mews Supported Living Facility on 17th May 2025. RQIA have issued 17 required actions to comply with regulations which robustly outline required improvements to ensure that resident’s needs, both medically and socially, are met. RQIA are also leading a regional group comprising relevant HSC statutory sector stakeholders to ensure whole system oversight and information sharing in relation the Mews.  In addition, my Department has liaised with the BHSCT who have statutory responsibility to ensure the social and medical needs of residents are met. The BHSCT have reported that they have a contract in place with the Cedar Foundation and an associated care management process in place. Since enforcement notices have been served to the Cedar Foundation in relation to the Mews Supported Living Facility, the BHSCT Learning Disability Team have engaged with RQIA and the Cedar Foundation. An action is now in plan in place to address concerns. This action plan includes a review of the needs of all Mews service users. An independent consultant has been engaged to assist with this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline why earnings from pilot personal dental services for health service work is not being treated as eligible earnings for purposes of maternity pay, contrary to the definition of gross earnings under Determination 5 of the Statement of Dental Remuneration 2025.
answer_text: Gross earnings as defined in the under Determination 5 of the Statement of Dental Remuneration 2025 refer to payments made to General Dental Practitioners for the provision of General Dental Services (GDS) or pilot Personal Dental Services (PDS). As a result, any payments made to an individual General Dental Practitioner under a pilot PDS contribute to Maternity pay and other such benefits.However, GDS and some pilot PDS schemes also include payments made to a practice rather than a practitioner. These practice-based payments do not contribute to maternity pay or other benefits.Personal Dental Services Pilots are commissioned under various arrangements, determined during the development stage of each pilot. Full consideration is given to how payment arrangements impact upon practice and practitioner payments and allowances, as this will impact the overall cost, informing the decision on whether or not the pilot is ultimately commissioned.The payment arrangements for any pilot will be clearly defined in advance of any General Dental Practitioner agreeing to participate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the resettlement of Muckamore Abbey Hospital residents.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline the policy and guidance for the resettlement of Muckamore Abbey Hospital residents.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline the procedure for long-term placements for individuals with complex needs and learning difficulties, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Individual Trust responses are set out below:  Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT)Children’s Services-All the needs of a Looked After Child are reviewed via the statutory Looked After Child process where the need for placement is discussed and agreed. The multi-disciplinary team involved with the child meet to consider the needs of the child and develop a care plan which involves parents and carers. All Trust facilities are considered. Following further discussion, the most appropriate placement which best suits the needs of the child is agreed. In the instance where Trust facilities are unsuitable, then alternative placements are explored, which can include the UK or the Republic of Ireland.Adults Services:The support needs of Adults with a Learning Disability, including determination of the need for a long-term placement, is based on a holistic assessment of the Service User’s needs. The assessment which has input from the Service User, their family and multi-disciplinary team members has a particular focus on the social and health needs of the Service User which in turn informs the level of support, including the nature of placement that is required. Placements are provided in accordance with existing contracts, and appropriate processes such as procurement and commissioning processes to ensure the suitability of the placement in meeting identified Service User’s needs.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT)Children’s ServicesThe focus of intervention with children with disabilities centres on early intervention, support and prevention of family breakdown to enable children and young people to remain in the care of their parents/carers. For any child who is admitted to care, care planning considerations include the viability and reunification if this is possible to their family or whether they should continue to be cared for outside of parental care.  Any child or young person admitted to care is reviewed via the Looked After Children’s Procedures, with LAC reviews convened as per policy to formally review the child/young person’s circumstances and to formulate their care plan.Any change to a long-term care plan is considered and analysed at each Looked After Child Review and any change to a long-term placement is considered by all professionals involved with the family. The decision is based on the best interests of the child or young person. When a long-term plan is agreed, the Trust considers both in house options and private providers to determine the most suitable placement for a child or young person. This is undertaken in partnership with families.Adult Services:The BHSCT Adult Intellectual Disability/Learning Disability Service completes assessments and care management reviews in line with Care Management Standards to assess needs for long term placements. Placements are provided in accordance with existing contracts, and appropriate processes for example, procurement and commissioning processes to ensure the suitability

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether family support systems are taken into account when determining long-term placements for individuals with complex needs and learning difficulties.
answer_text: All Health and Social Care Trusts take into account family support systems when determining long-term placements for individuals with complex needs and learning disabilities within Children and Adult Services as detailed below.Children’s Services:Family support systems form a key part of a child’s assessment: Understanding the Needs of Children in Northern Ireland (UNOCINI). Having a robust understanding of the family support system and its functionality is integral to assessing the support needs of any child, not exclusively the determination of long-term placements for children with complex needs and/or learning disability.  Where a child is considered ‘on the edge of care’, Social Workers will seek to establish a support network of appropriate family members to support the situation so that children might remain at home. When it is not possible for children to remain at home, Social Workers will explore any potential kinship care options whereby family members can be assessed as potential Foster Carers for the child(ren) or whether they can provide any support to a care placement.  Family interaction and support forms a key part of any long-term placement for a child with a disability, whether they are living in a kinship placement, Trust Foster Care or Residential Care. Unless there is a reason for family members not to spend time with a child in care, contact with family is promoted and facilitated as part of associated care planning. Engagement with family support systems when a child is in a long-term placement would be an inherent part of care planning for the child and would be regularly reviewed under the Child Looked After process.Adult Services:Adults with a learning disability and complex needs receive an assessment of need using a standardised assessment tool, The Northern Ireland Single Assessment Tool (NISAT). This is completed by a Multi-Disciplinary Team and identifies an individual’s assessed needs to inform the individual’s Care Plan. The NISAT considers the person holistically, including consideration of family support systems, co-habitants and carers to support imminent and future care planning and determination of service provision/placements. Carers Assessments are offered using regionally agreed assessment tools i.e. the NISAT ‘Carers Support and Needs Assessment’ or the ‘The Carer’s Conversation Wheel’, with service provision targeted to meet assessed needs within available resources. Independent Advocacy is offered to assist the individual and support families through the assessment and care planning process. Long term placements are subject to regular monitoring and review with families and carers involved in reviews, to ensure the placement continues to meet the individual’s assessed needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to the Commissioner for Children and Young People for Northern Ireland’s Annual Report and Accounts for the year ended 31 March 2024 which reported that only one department undertook a Child Rights Impact Assessment on its draft budget, to outline how his Department is ensuring children’s rights are protected in its budget decisions.
answer_text: My Department takes its responsibilities under Section 75 of the NI Act (1998) seriously and remains committed to meeting our statutory obligations regarding the promotion of equality and good relations.All departmental allocations and measures to live within budgets are consulted on through Equality Impact Assessments which consider impacts on all Section 75 groups; and, where a specific Child Rights Impact Assessment is deemed to be required, this will be undertaken.  Whilst my Department’s 2025-26 Budget position is challenging, in allocating the budget, no areas of service delivery are proposed for reduction. My Department’s budget will continue to be allocated to support existing statutory and public service delivery, and in line with previous Budget EQIAs, has been allocated to mitigate any adverse impacts on Section 75 categories, including young people.  Throughout 2025-26, within my Department’s available funding envelope, I will strive to deliver the best possible outcomes for people, including children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on his Department's engagement with the UK Government regarding the Hillsborough Law.
answer_text: I welcome the proposed Hillsborough Law as a way of increasing candour and transparency in the public sector.The Executive has agreed to support the extension of the provisions of the proposed Hillsborough Law legislation here, subject to a Legislative Consent Motion of the Assembly.  We agree that our support will depend upon our assessment of the Bill as it is published, and our analysis of its effect in this jurisdiction.I met Nick Thomas-Symonds, the Minister for the Cabinet Office, on 11 March to discuss the proposed Bill, and my officials are in contact with the Bill team and the implementation team in Whitehall.  Department of Finance officials have also convened a cross-departmental working group to consider the drafting of the legislation and its implementation in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the current position of Eden View in terms of its ability to offer short-term respite provision for children with complex needs.
answer_text: Staffing shortages continue to impact on the ability of the Northern Health and Social Care Trust to offer Short Breaks within Eden View. On the basis that safe staffing levels are not currently available RQIA cannot permit Short Breaks to reopen. The Northern HSC Trust has engaged an external recruitment agency to maximise the recruitment to vacancies in Eden View. Assuming a level of success - it is estimated that staff recruited will be available to take up post by the end of June 2025. Summer 2025 is estimated to reintroduce Short Breaks in Eden View on a phased basis (as soon as staffing levels reach a point to open 1 Short Break bed). It is anticipated that Eden View will resume full Short Break capacity (i.e. up to 3 Short Break Beds based on the needs of the children attending 7 days per week) by Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions he has had with the UK Government in relation to the introduction of the Hillsborough Law and its possible inclusion in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I have spoken to the Minister for the Cabinet Office, Nick Thomas-Symonds MP, and indicated that the proposed Hillsborough Law Bill should be drafted to extend here.  Officials in the two administrations have been working together to achieve that.  I have emphasised that this is conditional upon a Legislative Consent Motion being agreed by the Executive and Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the provision of respite services at Whitehaven in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) whether his Department has completed any work in relation to the shared use of sites for permanent residential and temporary respite provision.
answer_text: (i) Whitehaven continues to have resident children with plans in place to transition to supported living accommodation. These plans mean Short Breaks at Whitehaven will reopen on a phased basis from Summer 2025. The RQIA have a requirement to only permit limited Short Breaks to reopen alongside the resident children - given the complexity of these children’s needs. This is based on the compatibility of the children and a risk assessment, and has only offered very limited opportunity to provide Short Breaks. Whitehaven expects to resume reduced Short Break capacity in Summer 2025, and increase to full capacity from Autumn 2025.(ii) My Department is leading on a Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme, which was established in response to Professor Ray Jones’ Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services. The Reform Programme contains a specific workstream (Workstream 3: Residential Care) which is tasked with determining how we can better understand and meet the residential needs of children and young people in care across Northern Ireland.  This work is aimed at improving the living arrangements, and ultimately the outcomes, for children and young people who are being cared for in residential care by our Health and Social Care Trusts.Within this workstream, a specific task and finish group is considering the introduction of multi-site children’s homes. This means an arrangement where children are cared for across a number of buildings, which are collectively registered as a single children’s home.  The scope of this model of provision to accommodate respite or short break care remains to be determined.It is anticipated that recommendations in respect of multi-building children’s homes will be considered by the Reform Programme Board in the first half of 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a timeline for the implementation of recommendations for Health and Social Care Trusts arising from the Marcella Domestic Homicide Review.
answer_text: Firstly I would like to extend my sympathies to the victim’s family and friends, who continue to be impacted by her tragic loss. The Domestic Homicide Review was carried out to explore the circumstances of this devastating event, and to identify learning to help professionals involved to enhance their understanding, responses and practice across agencies in order to improve the protection of victims of domestic violence and their families in the future.The review identified ten overarching recommendations. Three of these recommendations have also been made in other DHRs; whilst these reviews were published previously, they relate to deaths which were broadly contemporaneous with the death of Marcella, and therefore it is unsurprising that some of the learning and recommendations in this review reflects those in earlier reports. A further 23 recommendations were identified by agencies themselves who were involved in the DHR. A multiagency Senior Oversight Group meets regularly to review progress of the DHR Report recommendations. To date, of the 33 recommendations, 31 involved one or more agencies within the Department of Health (HSCTS, Children’s Court Guardian Service formerly NIGALA, NIAS, SPPG). 22 recommendations have been completed prior to the publishing of the DHR report. 9 further recommendations are in progress and the estimated timescale for completion of these recommendations in full is April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has had any engagement with his counterparts in the UK Government in relation to a redress scheme for those injured by vaginal mesh.
answer_text: The Patient Safety Commissioner for England, Dr Henrietta Hughes, made recommendations on redress for those who were harmed by both Pelvic Mesh implants and the medicine Sodium Valproate in the “Hughes Report”, which was published on 07 February 2024. Whilst Dr Hughes’ remit was specifically for England, it is acknowledged that patients from across the UK have been affected by these issues.  The three Devolved Administrations are awaiting consideration and assessment from the Department of Health & Social Care (DHSC), however the UK Government has not yet formally responded to the Hughes Report and a UK Government decision on redress has not yet been taken.I wrote to Baroness Merron, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Patient Safety, Women’s Health & Mental Health at DHSC, requesting an update on the consideration of the Hughes Report recommendations and have sought assurances that consultation with the Devolved Administrations will take place in a timely manner and in advance of any UK Government decisions. Baroness Merron has informed me that the UK Government is carefully considering the report before coming to a decision. She recognises that any response by the UK Government is likely to have implications for the Devolved Administrations and is keen to work closely with officials across the UK in considering the report’s recommendations. She has provided assurance that my officials and I will be engaged in a timely manner and in advance of any UK Government Response.The approach to redress for those who have been harmed by both Pelvic Mesh and Sodium Valproate in Northern Ireland will be informed by the UK Government position, which is not yet determined.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taken to support those who have been injured by vaginal mesh.
answer_text: My Department recurrently funded the Regional Uro-Gynae Mesh Centre in the Belfast Trust. This service delivers:A surgical pathway involving outpatient clinics and inpatient theatre lists with input from consultants in Uro-Gynae, Urology, plastics and colorectal specialties, if required.A non-surgical pathway involving a multi-disciplinary team with input from physiotherapy, psychology and consultant anaesthetists in the pain service.To reduce waiting times in this specialist service the Department of Health, have offered non-recurrent funding to deliver increased capacity throughout 2025/2026 and the Belfast Trust is considering any opportunities it would have to offer additional capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 23709/22-27, whether the other Health and Social Care Trusts will be encouraged to introduce Phagenyx as an evidenced-based treatment offering cost saving and life enhancing outcomes for patients.
answer_text: As previously advised, phagenyx is offered as a treatment across a number of services at Belfast Trust including the Neurosurgery and Stroke services.  The treatment is also available at South Eastern Trust, specifically within the Stroke Unit, as a treatment modality for dysphagia. The remaining Trusts have indicated that the treatment is not currently available.The Department notes that NICE Interventional Procedure Guidance 781 advises that pharyngeal electrical stimulation for neurogenic dysphagia can be used for people with neurogenic dysphagia who have a tracheostomy after stroke while more evidence is generated. It can only be used with special arrangements for clinical governance, consent, and audit or research.While my Department notes the NICE guidance above, there is no formal requirement for Trusts to adopt treatments which are included within this NICE programme. Therefore treatment choice will remain the responsibility of the treating clinician for each individual.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on adult safeguarding concerns at the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Unit at Belfast City Hospital.
answer_text: Patient safety is the number one priority for my Department and my officials will always remain vigilant to any issues which may impact on patient safety.My Department were first made aware of building issues to the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre based at Belfast City Hospital on 17th September 2024.Since this time my officials have engaged with Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to address the issues identified and concerns raised.Belfast Trust Mental Health Services have confirmed that they do not have any adult safeguarding concerns as a result of water ingress in the Acute Mental Health inpatient Centre nor have they advised my Department of any concern in relation to patient safety as a result of water ingress or building issues.The Trust have confirmed that they continue to monitor the situation closely with regards to impact on patient safety and experience.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has recently completed exploratory works in two bedrooms within the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre. Apart from the two rooms where the exploratory works have been undertaken, the unit remains fully operational at present. The Trust’s early assessment does not indicate that the unit will need to close while remedial works take place.  A partial decant may be necessary to facilitate the remediation works, however I have sought assurances on behalf of service users and their families that the Trust will ensure that care is provided in an alternative suitable facility, should this be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to introduce podiatrists as first contact practitioners, given the increasing rates of musculoskeletal issues, diabetes, foot ulceration and associated infection and major and minor amputation.
answer_text: Podiatry services are available across all 5 Trusts providing both routine and emergency podiatry care to those patients who are at risk of developing limb or life threatening pathology.Podiatry services are open access services, meaning that people can contact the service directly to obtain an appointment and do not have to be referred to the service by another health care professional. There are currently no plans to introduce a first contact podiatrist role into primary care settings at this time.The Department is developing a rehabilitation framework for Northern Ireland, the framework will support the strategic design and development of rehabilitation services across Northern Ireland. Podiatry services would be seen as a key service in the delivery of rehabilitation for patients who have experienced amputation following active foot disease or for those patients with Musculo skeletal pathology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why patients have had their ADHD medication withdrawn by their GP following the cancellation of their shared care arrangements; and (ii) whether his Department will seek to change this policy to enable patients who were already receiving the medication through shared care to continue to do so.
answer_text: The legacy Health and Social Care Board issued correspondence on a number of occasions to private providers and general practice, most recently in June 2021, regarding requests for HSC prescriptions following private consultation.  The purpose of this correspondence was to remind both sectors of their clinical responsibilities in relation to prescribing of medications, including Red-Amber list drugs, to patients under their care. In particular, the limitations with regards to requests to GPs for transfer of ongoing prescribing of medication which for the patient’s GP may not be clinically acceptable.  This guidance has not changed.	To further facilitate appropriate shared care arrangements, my Department issued ‘Private (non-HSC) Prescribing Requests Guidance for Primary Care in September 2024. This guidance (Microsoft Word - SPPG guidance - Request to prescribe following private consultations September 2024 FV) provides a reminder that the legal responsibility for prescribing lies with the prescriber who signs the prescription, and it is the responsibility of the individual prescriber to prescribe within their own level of competence and scope of practice.Whilst there is no obligation on GPs to prescribe, in instances where a GP considers that the medication being requested is clinically appropriate, falls within their area of competence, does not breach the terms of their contract or indemnity cover, and is in keeping with the General Medical Council (GMC) guidance on Good Medical Practice, then they may choose to prescribe.If a GP makes the decision to end a shared care arrangement, best practice would suggest that the GP discusses this with the patient to outline the rationale for the decision.As clinical and legal liability associated with a prescribed medication falls to the doctor who issues and signs the script, a GP must be confident in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to them and the patient, by the recommending specialist.  My Department does not intend to issue new or amended guidance, clinical decisions remain that of medical professionals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on plans to introduce a multi-disciplinary team model at the (i) Mater; and (ii) Knockbracken out of hours GP services.
answer_text: SPPG commissions GP Out of Hours (OOHs) services across Northern Ireland from five GP OOHs Providers.  The operational delivery of services (including staffing) is the responsibility of providers.  Belfast Trust is responsible for the provision of GP OOHs services for the population of Belfast.  All GP OOHs providers are expected to review activity levels and capacity challenges to ensure the efficient use of resources and provision of an effective service.I am aware that the Belfast Trust have recently implemented nursing staff within the GP OOHs team as part of the service's direction to develop a multidisciplinary team approach to out-of-hours care.  Belfast Trust have advised that the service has also begun implementing nurse triage, including utilising independent nurse prescribers at weekends.  The service is predominantly a telephone triage and assessment service and nursing staff are based at the Mater site, however cover calls received at both bases.  Future development of the multidisciplinary team will include the recruitment of pharmacists, advanced nurse practitioners, and Mental Health Practitioners.  Belfast Trust advise there is no fixed timescale for this to be fully implemented.My department does encourage the development of a multidisciplinary workforce in the interests of good patient care and service resilience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current provision of ear wax removal services, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Ear wax removal services are delivered by primary and secondary care.   Some GPs provide ear wax removal services at their practice but in recent years, changes to National Institute of Clinical Excellence (NICE) clinical guidelines have resulted in fewer practices being able to treat patents.   Ear wax removal is now treated by using micro suction as a result of these guidelines.In the Northern Trust there is currently 1 full time ENT Nurse who undertakes this role, patients are prioritised according to those patients who meet the criteria and must have ongoing reviews within the service.The Southern Trust provides an ENT nurse led ear wax removal service in Craigavon Hospital on Tuesday afternoons, in Daisy Hill on Mondays and Banbridge Health and Care Centre on Monday and Wednesday afternoons. South Tyrone Hospital holds clinics twice a month.The South Eastern Trust provides ear wax removal at clinics in the Ulster Hospital, Lagan Valley Hospital & the Downe Hospital sites by trained ENT nurses.  Patients can be referred for micro-suction, however this service is only commissioned for patients where there is a clinical contraindication to the procedure in Primary care.  Those patients that are referred to the Trust are seen based on their clinical urgency. Hearing aid service users within Belfast HSC Trust with significant wax impacting hearing aid use or overall hearing are offered a referral to the nurse-led micro suction clinic in which they are given priority to access the system.  In the Western Trust, full day clinics are held in Altnagelvin Hospital on Mondays, Tuesdays and Thursdays. A further clinic is held on Friday afternoons.Omagh Health Centre & Primary Care Complex holds full day clinics for ear care patients including post op mastoid surgery, otitis externa and complicated wax patients on Wednesdays.These clinics, however, are only seeing urgent/high risk and (GP) referral patients like Chronic otitis externa, tympanic membrane perforation, attic retraction, post op mastoidectomy patients, and otitis externa due to the backlog in outpatient appointments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will update the public guidance on ear wax removal services.
answer_text: Following a number of questions recently on this subject, officials have contacted Trusts and Primary Care, highlighting the need to provide HSC public advice on ear wax removal.  In the first instance, however, patients should always contact their GP, Pharmacist or healthcare professional to seek advice on self-management of ear wax removal.  Patients can then be referred to either primary care or secondary care clinics to have ear wax removed.Unfortunately, as with many services in the HSC, demand in primary and secondary care exceeds capacity resulting in lengthening waiting times for treatment.  My Department continues to optimise all available resources whilst seeking additional funding to improve waiting times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the plans for expansion of respite facilities in Greenhill, including the number of beds that will be available.
answer_text: The Trust currently funds Greenhill for two beds 5 nights per week. The additional investment will enable the Trust to expand this provision to 2 beds 7 nights per week and this expansion will be contingent upon the success recruitment of an appropriate workforce. A dedicated recruitment campaign will commence during April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20558/22-27, for an update on the review of the delegation of healthcare and nursing tasks under Direct Payments.
answer_text: My Department’s Chief Nursing Officer has commissioned the Northern Ireland Practice and Education Council for Nursing and Midwifery (NIPEC) to lead a review of the Direct Payments process to address the issues that have arisen in relation to the delegation of healthcare tasks. This approach includes engagement with key stakeholders, and meetings have already been held with people with lived experience and advocacy groups to establish the practices currently in operation and identify viable pathways to facilitate the safe and effective delegation of healthcare in line with the governing guidelines and legislation. The review is anticipated to be completed by Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cost of administrating the payment of Direct Payments.
answer_text: The cost of administering Direct Payments would be a cost to each of the Health and Social Care Trusts and my department would not hold this information.The amount paid out by each HSC Trust in the last quarter of 2024 is listed on the table below:TRUSTAMOUNT PAID DURING QUARTER OCT- DEC 2024 BHSCT£3,312,658.51NHSCT£3,492,656.81SEHSCT£4,471,473.93SHSCT£3,890,005.22WHSCT£5,863,182.47TOTAL£21,029,976.94(These amounts include one-off payments which ceased during the quarter)The total amount paid at quarter end on the 31st December 2023 was £18,511,214.63 and at quarter end on the 31st December 2022 was £16,665,518.64, therefore indicating a rise in the uptake of Direct payments regionally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average amount paid out through Direct Payments, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The cost of administering Direct Payments would be a cost to each of the Health and Social Care Trusts and my department would not hold this information.The amount paid out by each HSC Trust in the last quarter of 2024 is listed on the table below:TRUSTAMOUNT PAID DURING QUARTER OCT- DEC 2024 BHSCT£3,312,658.51NHSCT£3,492,656.81SEHSCT£4,471,473.93SHSCT£3,890,005.22WHSCT£5,863,182.47TOTAL£21,029,976.94(These amounts include one-off payments which ceased during the quarter)The total amount paid at quarter end on the 31st December 2023 was £18,511,214.63 and at quarter end on the 31st December 2022 was £16,665,518.64, therefore indicating a rise in the uptake of Direct payments regionally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on Nature Recovery projects at Divis and the Black Mountain.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support available to patients who have had ADHD medication withdrawn by their GP following the cancellation of their shared care arrangements.
answer_text: Regarding provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), demand for assessment and diagnosis has grown across Trusts. All Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.The Department is commissioning a research exercise into the current and projected demand with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability. The exercise will seek to establish the prevalence of ADHD for both children and adults in Northern Ireland, examine best practice from other jurisdictions and identify challenges and barriers in relation to ADHD diagnosis and treatment. Importantly, the research exercise will be expected to make recommendations on what any future commissioned service might look like in terms of, for example, costs, staffing profiles and recommended care pathways.A research lead has now been identified and work on the research has commenced, with an anticipated completion date of end June 2025.Any future commissioned service will be subject to both budget availability and evidenced demand, but the research, once completed, will help provide a firm evidence base to inform a future model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the facilities currently providing respite care, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Western Health and Social Care TrustChildren’s Disability ServicesRosebud Cottage in Northern Sector of the Trust currently provides respite care for Children with Disabilities.  This unit is operational on a part time basis only with incremental increase planned to 6 days per week by May 2025.Avalon House is a short break facility in the Southern Sector of the Trust however this unit is currently repurposed to provide Medium to Long term Care for 4 young people.  This unit will remain as a residential facility for the foreseeable.  Butterfly Lodge is located on the Altnagelvin site and is a small unit where children with complex physical health care needs can avail of a short break. A team of children’s healthcare assistants support children with complex physical health care needs. After core hours care packages are allocated, with the remaining hours allocated to give short breaks to children and families. Some of these breaks are provided in the home and some are provided in Butterfly lodge.Adult Learning Disability Services  The Trust has the following statutory facilities for respite/short breaks: -Adult Cottage L ’Derry– 5 bedsBeltany House, Omagh – 3 bedsCreamery House and Mantlin Court Residential Homes Kesh are used to provide respite/short breaks at times when there is capacity within the residential population (maximum 1 bed)Older Peoples Services The Trust purchase other respite/short break bed provision from within the Independent Residential & Nursing Home Sector predominantly within the Trust area, but at times from within other Trust areas, depending on need.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust Children’s Disability ServicesThe South Eastern HSC Trust (SET) currently has one Residential short Breaks Children’s Home, Green Hill, located in Newcastle County Down. This home, with the new Ministerial investment will be budgeted to deliver two beds per night seven nights per week. However, this will be contingent in a successful workforce recruitment campaign. The Trust also avails of Residential Short Breaks for children with complex medical needs and this is provided by the Belfast Trust, in Forest Lodge, Belfast.Adult Learning DisabilityThe SET has 1 statutory short breaks facility for adults with Learning Disabilities.  A panel is held monthly when the registered manager and Community Social Work Managers allocate nights for service users based on assessed need.SET also have block contracts with private providers for respite/short breaks for adults with Learning Disabilities across the 3 sectors of the Trust.  Additional beds can also be spot purchased if required and are available.  Respite/short breaks with private providers are also allocated as appropriate in accordance with assessed need.Older Peoples Services In SET Older Peoples Services there are 10 block booked respite/short break beds available in both independent sector care homes and in Trust statutory residential facilities: 2 in North Down & Ards (1 bed

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when (i) Lindsey House; and (ii) Redwood will be available to provide respite care.
answer_text: The Trust plans to have Redwood Children’s Home operational by September 2025 and it will be registered as a Long-term home delivering two placements. One of the two placements is intended to be a shared care involving two young people. This is intended to offer enhanced support to two families who need more than a short break but less than a full-time residential placement. Redwood will not deliver short breaks or respite. The opening of Redwood will enable the reopening of Lindsay House to deliver Short Breaks by September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to increase inpatient bed capacity for mental health usage.
answer_text: All Trusts are experiencing significant pressures as a consequence of increased demands upon services, including increased number of new referrals, increased complexity and acuity of people presenting to services and increased workforce pressures as a result of increased vacancy levels.There are currently 393 commissioned inpatient mental health beds for the region. My officials keep demand and occupancy under review, however at this time there are no plans to increase inpatient bed capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether The Brook is currently being utilised by the Northern Health and Social Care Trust as either a respite facility or residential facility.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of the Social Care Collaborative Forum in relation to care home standards.
answer_text: A Working Group, chaired by my Department has been established to undertake a review of both Nursing and Residential Care Home Standards.  The review will involve ongoing collaboration and engagement with relevant social care stakeholders to ensure there is clarity between what is considered to be best practice and what will be measured under the inspection frameworks. This review will allow my Department to implement relevant learning and will assist with wider implementation of the Enhanced Clinical Care Framework. Following the initial meeting of the Working Group my Department engaged external support to undertake a benchmarking exercise to assess the existing Standards (and supporting documentation) against relevant external reports, recent developments, guidance, policy and procedures. The purpose of this exercise was to identify potential gaps and revisions that may be necessary to ensure the Standards are reflective of current best practice and guidance.We have received this report and are currently considering next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the range of services and pathways included in the time critical waiting lists, as outlined in the Programme for Government 2024-2027.
answer_text: The time critical waiting lists include adult and paediatric patients, where a delay in undertaking the required procedure may result in significant adverse clinical outcomes.Time critical procedures have a limited treatment window and cover many specialties including patients waiting for cardiac surgery, specialist spinal orthopaedics, glaucoma, retinal detachments, pyeloplasty, paediatric gastrostomy procedures and a range of diagnostic imaging tests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 20555/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the availability of Phagenyx to treat dysphagia across each of the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Phagenyx is offered as a treatment across a number of services at Belfast Trust including the Neurosurgery and Stroke services.  The treatment is also available at South Eastern Trust, specifically within the Stroke Unit, as a treatment modality for dysphagia. The Trust has a strict protocol in place for the use of Phagenyx which includes detailed access criteria developed in line with research evidence supporting the use of the treatment.The remaining Trusts have indicated that the treatment is not currently available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce annual cancer screening for those who are exposed to higher levels of contaminants through work, such as firefighters.
answer_text: Screening policy across the UK is informed by the recommendations from the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific committee which advises the four UK countries on screening matters.  Currently, there is no recommendation by the UK NSC for a population screening programme, or targeted screening programme, based on occupational exposure.  The Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations 2002 (COSHH) requires all employers to assess the risk to their employees in relation to substances that are hazardous and "to prevent or adequately control" those risks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Women's Health Action Plan will be published.
answer_text: Queen's University Belfast and Derry Well Women have recently extended the closing date for the Women’s Health Survey until the end of March. Work to develop a Women’s Health Action Plan is ongoing.  My Department recently hosted a number of online engagement events with women's representative groups and healthcare professionals and will be reviewing the input received over the coming weeks to help inform the priorities in the action plan. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the process; and (ii) the criteria used to allocate funding from the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26.
answer_text: The Core Grant Funding Scheme for 2025-26 was co-designed in 2024 with Community and Voluntary Sector representatives, and included the following scoring criteria:Evidence of how an organisation supports the department’s strategic objectives;Evidence that an organisation is involved in carrying out relevant activities;Evidence that an organisation will deliver clear outcomes, including how those will be measured and the targets that will be met;Evidence that an organisation has delivered similar outcomes previously; Evidence that an organisation has suitable monitoring and control systems in place, and suitably qualified and experienced staff; andEvidence that an organisation will work in partnership across the statutory, community and voluntary sectors. These scoring criteria and the application process are listed and explained in the guidance for applicants, available on the departmental website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current treatments available for those diagnosed with, or suspected of having, postural tachycardia syndrome.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail why Givinostat, a drug to treat Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy, is not being made available.
answer_text: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) is currently evaluating givinostat (Duvyzat®) for the treatment of Duchenne muscular dystrophy in people of 6 years and over, with final guidance expected to publish in July 2025.When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdfWhilst the manufacturer has made givinostat available via an Early Access Programme (EAP) for eligible patients in parts of the UK, the Belfast HSC Trust, which delivers Paediatric Neuromuscular services for the region, has advised that it is unfortunately not in a position to facilitate the EAP due to the associated resource implications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when Givinostat will be made available to treat Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability in Northern Ireland.  Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within HSC organisations.  This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions.  In practice this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also routinely available in Northern Ireland.NICE are currently in the early stages of evaluating givinostat (Duvyzat®) for the treatment of Duchenne muscular dystrophy in people 6 years and over, with final guidance expected to publish in July 2025.When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdfIn circumstances where NICE is yet to complete a full appraisal, or does not recommend a particular drug, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) does not routinely commission that therapy for use in Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail arrangements for respite provision in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust should the move to Redwood not go ahead due to issues in relation to fire safety provisions.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust fully anticipates that Redwood will become a registered Home. However, in the event of an unforeseen delay - the Trust intends to continue to seek to expand the provision of Residential Short Breaks, through the Short Breaks Home, Greenhill, based in Downpatrick. The South Eastern HSC Trust also plans to expand the Community In Reach Short Breaks through the Ministerial investment in Disability Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20011/22-27, to detail the specialties (i) considered; (ii) assessed; and (iii) that will be progressed for use of robotic-assisted surgery.
answer_text: The General Surgery Network, which now sits under the Strategic Planning and Performance Group of the Department of Health, has made a commitment to consider how Robot Assisted Surgery can be implemented in Northern Ireland across a number of specialities. In addition to Urology, other specialties of Gynaecology, Colorectal and Thoracic surgery will be considered, however significant investment will be required.    Work has been instructed for the completion of a cost benefit analysis.  In addition, information on patient safety, improved patient outcomes, the needs, training requirements and facilities also needs to be considered, which will be different for each specialism.  There is also a need to consider the impact Robot Assisted Surgery may have on non- Robot Assisted Surgery, while ensuring regional equity of access from a workforce perspective as well as the patient perspective.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures he will take to ensure the Western Health and Social Care Trust fully complies with the independent review announced by the Minister of Justice into the case of Jonathan Creswell.
answer_text: I am aware of the review that the Justice Minister has initiated in relation to the case of Jonathan Creswell and that draft terms of reference have been prepared and shared with various authorities including the Chief Executive of the Western Health and Social Care Trust. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of Serious Adverse Incidents.
answer_text: My Department’s review of the extant Serious Adverse Incidents (SAI) Procedure is progressing.To appropriately inform and shape the draft proposals, my officials have collaborated and extensively engaged with a number of key stakeholders.  My Department has taken the necessary time to consider in detail the range of comments and feedback received from these stakeholders, and to ensure that where appropriate, relevant points have been taken account of in the draft proposals.The draft proposals describe the high-level strategic approach to learning from Patient Safety Incidents and aim to rebalance and reframe the current approach, moving away from a prescriptive procedure towards a set of overarching principles supported by more detailed guidance documents.My Department will be launching a 12-week public consultation on the draft Framework and supporting documentation in the coming weeks.  This is an important opportunity, which will enable an even wider range of interested individuals and groups to input to, and comment on, the proposed approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current protocol and procedures in relation to Serious Adverse Incident processes that Health and Social Care Trusts must follow, including (i) timelines for completion; and (ii) the sharing of information with those patients and their families involved.
answer_text: There is a requirement for all Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations to report Serious Adverse Incidents (SAIs) to the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG). SPPG work jointly with the Public Health Agency (PHA) and collaboratively with the Regulation and Quality Improvement (RQIA) to exercise this requirement via the ‘Regional Procedure for the reporting and Follow Up of Serious Adverse Incidents (SAIs)’.   The procedure provides guidance to HSC Organisations in relation to SAIs arising during the course of their business or commissioned service. It aims to put in place the mechanisms to effectively identify and share learning both locally and regionally with a focus on safety and quality; leading to service improvements.  The SAI Procedure can be accessed on the Health and Social Care website via https://online.hscni.net/wpfd_file/procedure-for-the-reporting-and-follow-up-of-sais-2016/.  In line with the SAI Procedure, SAI reviews should be conducted at a level 1, 2 or 3, which is appropriate and proportionate to the complexity of the incident under review.   Timescales for completion of review and submission to SPPG for each level of review is as follows: Level 1 SAI Review – within 8 weeksLevel 2 SAI Review – within 12 weeksLevel 3 SAI Review – timescale to be agreed between the reporting organisation and the SPPG/PHA DROFollowing an SAI it is important, in the spirit of honesty and openness, to ensure a consistent approach is afforded to the level of service user / family engagement across the region. When engaging with service users/family/carers, HSC Trusts should refer to addendum 1 of the SAI Procedure ‘A Guide for Health and Social Care Staff Engagement/Communication with Service User/Family/Cares following a SAI.’  This guide assists HSC Trusts to ensure effective communication with the service user/family/carer is undertaken in an open, transparent, informed, consistent and timely manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the establishment of a Just Transition Commission.
answer_text: AQO 1493/22-27Answer: The establishment of a Just Transition Commission is a key priority for me. On 11 November 2024 I launched a consultation on the establishment of the Commission.  The consultation focused on the detailed proposals to establish the Commission, including the type of public body the Commission should be, its composition, the Commission’s role and its legislated functions. Views were also sought on the proposed sectors to be represented on the Commission, its reporting arrangements and the secretariat and support required for it to function.My officials extended the consultation at stakeholder’s request and the consultation closed on 20 January 2025. My department is now analysing the 100 written responses received as well as the outcome of the stakeholder engagement sessions that were conducted as part of the consultation. A consultation summary of responses report will be published in due course. The findings from the consultation will be used to inform my decisions on the final content of the draft regulations that will establish the Commission. I will then seek agreement from my Executive colleagues and the regulations will then be laid in draft in this Assembly for debate and approval.Once the Regulations are in place to establish the Commission in law, a recruitment competition to appoint members to the Commission will be undertaken via the public appointments process. Our draft Programme for Government details the Executive’s commitment to establishing the Just Transition Commission and I am taking every step to ensure that we have the Commission established as soon as possible. The Commission will play a vital role as Northern Ireland moves towards a net zero economy and I want to ensure we have the right people on the Commission with broad sectoral representation to ensure it is fully effective. The Just Transition Commission is an important body that will support all departments in ensuring a net zero transition is fair and just. I will continue to update members as the progress on the establishment of the Commission.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of how the £13 million multi-year funding for children’s respite services, announced in October 2024, will be spent by each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: On 23 October 2024, I was pleased to be able to approve additional regional funding for the Children with Disability service across all HSC Trusts of up to £13m a year in multi-year funding, including up to £2m for the rest of the current financial year.It is intended that this additional funding will assist with increasing short breaks capacity and expanding family, therapeutic and behavioural supports in the community across all HSC Trusts.In terms of the distribution of funding this has been agreed that it would be split per a capitation model based on population size. At this time, the DoH SPPG has reviewed Trusts Prioritisation Proposals, in terms of their proposed Action plans to assist with alleviating some of the pressures experienced within the Children with Disability Service.Each Trust is currently in the process of working with the DoH SPPG to complete the Business Cases for the Final Prioritisation Proposals, in order to allow the allocation of funding.My Department and HSC Trusts continue to work collaboratively in terms of progressing matters. In relation to the short-term action plans – there are various initiatives across multiple Trusts to improve family and community support services for Children with Disability, enhance short break provision and expand targeted care programs. Collectively, Trusts aim to meet the short-term priorities, such as increasing short break provision and delivering tailored family support services. In addition, the Trusts do have plans to increase contracts with a range of voluntary and community services to provide additional family support, or breaks from caring through youth club type provision, after school clubs and enhanced opportunities for socialisation and personal development. Until the procurement process is completed and matters finalised – it would not be appropriate to provide all detail regarding the proposed organisations that the Trusts intend to use to provide support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 19007/22-27; and (ii) AQW 19942/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he will make the necessary legislative change to ensure that carers may provide delegated healthcare or nursing tasks under Direct Payments.
answer_text: The legislation covering the use of Direct Payments (DPs) to address identified care needs stipulates the type of care provision for which DPs can be used. My Department is aware that issues have arisen in some Health and Social Care Trusts around the delegation of healthcare tasks and regrets any disruption to care, or distress experienced, by those individuals and families affected.  The delegation of healthcare is a complex issue which needs to be taken forward within the legal framework (with associated governance) while ensuring safe and practical care. My Department is urgently undertaking a review of the process of such care provision and is engaging with stakeholders and families to address the issues that have arisen and make recommendations on the way forward.  I look forward to receiving those recommendations on completion of this work and will seek to implement the changes necessary to address this important issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of Direct Payments, specifically in relation to delivery of delegated healthcare or nursing tasks.
answer_text: I recognise that issues have arisen in some Health and Social Care Trusts around the delegation of healthcare tasks, and I regret any disruption to care, or distress experienced, by those affected. My Department is urgently undertaking a review of the process of care provision where Direct Payments (DPs) are used in relation to the provision of healthcare tasks. This includes engagement with stakeholders and families to address the issues that have arisen and make recommendations on the way forward.  I look forward to receiving those recommendations on completion of this work and will seek to implement the changes necessary to address this important issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the introduction of a Regional Obesity Management Service.

To ask the Minister of Justice whether she is able to commission a serious case review under the Public Protection Arrangements for Northern Ireland to consider the potential referral failures in the case of Jonathan Creswell.
answer_text: The Public Protection Arrangements Northern Ireland (PPANI) bring agencies together to assess and manage the risk posed by certain sexual and violent offenders.  PPANI must operate in line with statutory guidance issued by my Department under Article 50 of the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2008.This statutory guidance makes provision for Serious Case Reviews where someone who is being managed through the public protection arrangements is charged with a further serious sexual or violent offence, or there has been a significant failure in how they were managed.  As Jonathan Creswell was not being managed under PPANI at the time of Katie Simpson’s murder, such a review would be speculative and beyond the scope of a meaningful SCR.  However, I recognise that there may have been points at which Jonathan Creswell could have been referred into PPANI for management under those arrangements.  It is vital that we fully understand the circumstances and take account of any learning around the management of serious offenders. I have therefore commissioned the independent Lay Advisers, who are appointed by statute to oversee and monitor PPANI, to review the general robustness and operation of the referral process.  I am also committed to a specific review of the Creswell case. This review is intended to establish when he came into contact with any of the PPANI agencies; if there were opportunities to refer him into the arrangements; and to identify learning.  The review will also consider those points which have been raised in correspondence to my Department and to the PPANI co-ordinator.  The full terms of reference are being finalised and it is my intention to appoint an independent reviewer to undertake this work.

To ask the Minister of Health whether robotic-assisted surgery will be permitted to treat endometriosis.
answer_text: Robotic-assisted surgery is a surgical model of care which is being adopted by many surgical specialities and was introduced into Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in 2018 within the specialty of Urology. Other surgical specialties, including Gynaecology, are now in a similar position to Urology in considering utilising Robotic-assisted surgery.  This could include robotic-assisted surgery for endometriosis if there was sufficient resource to support this. Under the remit of the General Surgery Network, initial discussions and preparation work has commenced to consider the development of a regional phased plan for Robotic-assisted surgery within Northern Ireland. However, significant capital investment, in addition to workforce training, would be required to further develop Robotic-assisted surgery across specialisms within Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who have received robotic-assisted surgery in Northern Ireland in each year since 2019, broken down by gender.
answer_text: Since 2019, a total of 599 patients have received robotic-assisted surgery in Northern Ireland to date, 513 male patients and 86 female patients.  Robotic-assisted surgery is a surgical model of care which is being adopted by many surgical specialities and was introduced into Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in 2018. Belfast Health and Social Care Trusts completed 52 procedures in 2019/20, all male patients; 11 procedures in 2020/21, all male patients; 144 procedures in 2021/22, with 123 male patients and 21 female patients; 142 procedures in 2022/23, with 126 male patients and 16 female patients; and 242 procedures in 2023/24, with 198 male patients and 44 female patients.  The majority of procedures were within the speciality of Urology.  In 2023/24 South Eastern Trust completed 8 procedures, with 3 male and 5 female patients.  This service is delivered in the Ulster Independent Clinic, by a South Eastern Trust Colorectal Surgeon who is trained in robotic assisted surgery.
