question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the reinstatement of the Gender Identity Service.
answer_text: My Department has been working together with the Belfast HSC Trust to address the issues of waiting times, resources, and to develop a new model of care for Gender Identity Services for transgender people living in NI.  As part of this process, a Gender Identity Service Pathway Review Group was established and Chaired by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), with membership on the group including, the Public Health Agency, the Patient Client Council, the Royal College of General Practitioners and officials from Policy in my Department. The work of the Pathway Review Group began in September 2019.  There was significant engagement undertaken to help inform the review, notwithstanding the obvious challenges posed by the COVID-19 pandemic. A Gender Identity Liaison Panel, which sits under the main Pathway Review Group, was also established to enable those with lived experience to provide input to the review. On completion of the review, my officials have continued  to  work closely and collaboratively with the Belfast HSC Trust, the Gender Identity Review Group and Gender Identity Liaison Group to develop a business case for the first phase of service expansion, which identifies the extra resources required to enhance the capacity of the services to meet the growing demand and includes additionality to address the implications of the Cass Review report. Funding of around £740k will be required recurrently to fund the service development.  The business case details the extra medical and nursing resources required, including additional endocrinology provision. General Practitioner shared care arrangements are currently being explored for Phase 2 of this service development. The business case was submitted for consideration in November 2024. My officials are working closely with Belfast Trust as part of the necessary approval process. I am committed to improving wider gender identity service provision for children, young people and adults in NI. Despite the very challenging financial position facing my Department, I have advised my officials that I wish to prioritise the development of this service to ensure the appropriate advice and support is available to individuals and their families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to ensure all deaf children have the opportunity to access early, effective support to develop language and communication in the early years, including Auditory Verbal therapy.
answer_text: There are around 1,700 deaf children in Northern Ireland. Northern Ireland has one of the best Newborn Hearing Screening Programmes in the world and this state-of-the-art hearing technology (mainly hearing aids and cochlear implants) is available to babies and young children across NI. Each year approximately 500 babies have a positive hearing test who require referral for diagnostic tests by 3 months of age.About half the incidence of childhood deafness is known to be attributed to genetic causes. Deafness can be inherited, meaning it’s passed down in families, even where there appears to be no family history of deafness due to gene variants. However, some gene variants which cause deafness can also arise for the first time in a child, meaning there’s a genetic reason, but it’s not inherited from a parent. It is estimated that approximately 90% of deaf children come from families with no direct experience of deafness but the gene variant passed to them has resulted in deafness. A cochlear implant is an electronic system that stimulates the hearing nerve directly. It can give a sensation of sound to profoundly deaf children and adults who have been unable to hear using the most powerful hearing aids available. After cochlear implants have been fitted, children and adults can learn about the sounds around them and develop their listening and spoken language skills over time, in line with their communication potential. The implant team in Belfast Trust is multidisciplinary, involving people from many different specialist areas. These people include the Consultant Audio vestibular Physician, Ear, Nose and Throat (ENT) Surgeons, Audiologists, Speech and Language Therapists, Teachers of the Deaf and Clinical Psychologists. AVUK offers specialised training for the HSC and other professionals to improve and, according to their letter, benefits of Auditory Verbal therapy (AVT) are clear. They state that approximately 80% of children who attend AVUK’s Auditory Verbal Programme for at least two years achieve the same level of spoken language as their hearing peers, rising to 97% of children without additional needs. With the majority of these children attending mainstream schools and attaining educational outcomes on a par with hearing children.SPPG has reached out to clinical colleagues for some initial input on Auditory Verbal Therapy (AVT) for non-hearing children and while they have suggested that there is a lack of well-controlled studies addressing the use of AVT, they have also suggested that this would not necessarily suggest AVT may be an ineffective intervention in the habilitation of children with cochlear implants.  Therefore, more information is required but this will be reviewed/agreed by clinicians in due course. AVUK therapy is not a currently commissioned service within Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has evaluated the specialist workforce required to meet the needs of babies, children and young people with speech, language and communication needs, including Auditory Verbal therapists.
answer_text: The Speech and Language Workforce Review 2019-2029 evaluated the speech and language therapy workforce required to deliver services to the whole demographic of Northern Ireland. It is widely accepted that since this review and the recommendations made therein that demands on the speech and language service have increased. The Advisory Committee of Allied Health Professions was reconvened in March 2024. My Chief Allied Health Professions Officer has commissioned the workforce subcommittee of my Advisory Committee of Allied Health Professions to consider all workforce issues for Allied Health Professions.  As part of this work, the committee will critically review the recommendations in the Speech and Language Workforce Review 2019-2029 in order to inform the future workforce requirements.  I anticipate receiving their advice in coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress with the Department's adoption of the roads and footpaths in Loughshore Manor, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: Loughshore Manor is under construction and will be offered for adoption by the developer in accordance with the provisions contained within the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980. It remains the responsibility and the intention of the developer to achieve adoption. Under the Order, the Department has enforcement powers if a developer fails with these obligations. At present there are no indications that the developer intends to default on these responsibilities.My Department continues to monitor the development, offering advice and guidance when required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the criteria for an individual to be discharged from a hospital into a care home.
answer_text: Discharge from hospital into a care home is following a multi-disciplinary assessment process to establish the patient’s needs.  There are different circumstances in which individuals discharge to a care home.  Some patients may need an extra period of rehabilitation and this may be provided in a care home before long-term care needs are established.   With other patients there may be limited rehabilitation and a care home is required to meet their longer-term needs.  Additionally, due to the regional shortage of homecare workers patients may discharge temporarily to a care home to await a package of care if essential care is not available at the point of hospital discharge.  My department has waived the fees in these circumstances.  In recognition of the pressures on the HSC system my department is progressing on the maximisation of care home capacity and homecare through the Social Care Collaborative Forum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will be reviewing the terms and conditions of staff delivering neighbourhood renewal programmes.
answer_text: My Department is not the direct employer for staff in any of the voluntary and community groups it funds and has no role in setting salaries or employment terms and conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 17904/22-27, to detail (i) the work required at the Redwood facility on Belmont Road, Belfast, to meet current fire regulations; and (ii) the impact this work will have on the estimated opening date of the site.
answer_text: The existing fire safety provisions at Redwood have not been maintained as required by the fire safety legislation i.e. ensuring any provisions or systems in place ‘are subject to a suitable system of maintenance and are maintained in an efficient state, in efficient working order and in good repair’ - The Fire Safety Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2010, Part 3, Regulation 16. The South Eastern Trust estates team are continuing to work through the Fire Risk Survey in order to ascertain the extent of the works that are needing to be undertaken. The fire risk assessment methodology requires these works to be completed prior to occupation if not already occupied i.e. ‘Considerable resources might have to be allocated to reduce the risk.  If the building is unoccupied, it should not be occupied until the risk has been reduced’. The opening of the Redwood site will depend on the extent and cost of the works that will be required to be undertaken and the timeframe that it will take to do the necessary works to ensure the building is fit for purpose.  It is likely that the building will cost in excess of £50,000 to rectify fire doors with estimates of other required works being progressed through the Fire Risk Survey. Barnardo’s at this stage are only offering a lease of 18 months therefore the Trust will have to consider the affordability and value for money of renting the facility given the investment it would need to make to meet the fire regulation guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the recent death of a cyclist at Clifton Street, whether he will consider bringing forward the delivery of the Crumlin Road - Oldpark Road - Ballysillan scheme, as outlined in the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan 2022-31.
answer_text: I was very sorry to hear of the recent death of Mr McMahon and offer my deepest sympathies to his family and friends. As with all fatal collisions my Department will be working closely with the PSNI to assist them with their investigation. In recognition of the importance of quality pedestrian and cycling infrastructure, scheme design needs to be thorough if we want to deliver to the highest standard possible. This can often mean several rounds of engagement with community and other key stakeholders before we commence statutory processes to implement legislation for new cycle routes.Design work is ongoing for the majority of the short term schemes within the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan. My Department aims to progress as many of these schemes as possible in the time period up to the end of 2025, subject to staff resource, budget availability, and the successful completion of statutory processes.Phasing of future design development for Belfast Cycling Network medium and long term schemes is being considered in line with the emerging Eastern Transport Plan and other departmental projects such as Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taken to ensure access to cardiac rehabilitation is available to patients in all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Cardiac rehabilitation is available to patients in the five Health and Social Care Trusts. The SPPG has established a Cardiac Rehabilitation Strategic Leadership Group, which has been working on devising a standardised cardiac rehabilitation pathway.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department's review of the Neighbourhood Renewal strategy.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the review of archived records being carried out as part of the research into Mother and Baby Homes and Magdalene Laundries.
answer_text: We thank the Member for highlighting this vital piece of work.  These records could potentially hold vital information for individuals and their loved ones, that will help piece together this difficult part of our past.   We met the co-chairs of the Truth Recovery Independent Panel, Professors Leanne McCormick and Sean O’Connell, on 4th November, and were pleased to hear of the positive progress made by the Panel and Public Record Office [PRONI] in relation to Institutional records.  During the past year PRONI has preserved and digitised over 3,000 records, and there are agreements in place to digitise further institutional collections.  Some records, including cross border, and some adoption files, are more complex to access.  However, officials are working with the relevant organisations to secure and improve access to these valuable records. PRONI is also working with statutory bodies, including the Health Trusts and Courts Service, to identify and gather relevant official records.   PRONI has engaged with the Diocese of Down and Connor and with Belfast City Council in relation to assistance with access to burial records. The Diocese is also currently digitising Milltown Cemetery burial registers to make them available online.  They have also agreed to provide PRONI with the original volumes early in the new year, so that individual burial entries can be cross-referenced with the records of the institutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure who is responsible for the clearance of fallen leaves from footpaths and roads which have become hazardous.
answer_text: Councils are responsible for cleaning roads and footways, with the exception of Designated Roads. Article 7 of the Litter (Northern Ireland) Order 1994 (as amended by the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011) places a duty on District Councils in relation to the cleaning of roads, including the removal of detritus, such as leaves, from the carriageway and footway. My Department is responsible for keeping Designated Roads, such as the motorways, clear of litter and detritus.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how he ensures that temporary infrastructure, erected during roadworks being carried out by either his Department or contractors, accounts for the needs of pedestrians.
answer_text: Where works on or adjacent to the road network require restrictions to the available width of the footway or carriageway, it is the responsibility of the scheme designer to ensure that appropriate Temporary Traffic Management is designed and implemented. This may include temporary infrastructure, erected during roadworks, to facilitate the safe passage of all road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when respite facilities in (i) Redwood; and (ii) Greenhill will be open.
answer_text: Greenhill is open and Redwood is a proposed new short breaks facility which has not yet opened. The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust currently delivers residential Short Breaks from Greenhill.  It is the Trusts intention to transfer this service from Greenhill to the Redwood site on the Belmont Road in Belfast. A definitive time frame for this proposed move is not available at this point in time as an assessment is ongoing about the work that will be required to upgrade Redwood to current Fire regulation standards. Barnardos, who own Redwood, have commissioned a fire risk survey which will inform the next steps. Trust Estates department and the RQIA are actively involved in the discussions to create the circumstances to enable Redwood to open.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the findings of the feasibility study into extending the North-South Glider route to Glengormley and Carryduff will be published.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he will be reviewing the guidance around eligibility for prostate cancer testing, in line with the review announced in England.
answer_text: Screening for prostate cancer is currently not recommended by the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific committee which advises the four nations. They last considered the evidence for screening for prostate cancer in 2020 and concluded that screening was not recommended.  This is because of the poor ability of the prostate specific antigen (PSA) test to predict cancer, which could lead to unnecessary tests that carry risks of potentially life-changing harm. Additionally, some prostate cancers may not produce elevated PSA levels, leading to false-negative results that provide false reassurance. The UK NSC has commissioned a prostate cancer screening evidence review and cost effectiveness model, and it is expected to review this work during 2025. The UK NSC process will include a public consultation. The UK Health Departments, through the National Institute of Health and Care Research (NIHR), have invested in a significant programme of research to address the evidence gaps in this important area. A clinical trial (called the TRANSFORM trial), which will compare the most promising tests and provide evidence about the best way to screen for prostate cancer, is due to start in early 2025.My department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on proposals for screening programmes. Anyone with symptoms which they believe may be related to the prostate gland should contact their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the training Health and Social Care staff receive in relation to booking emergency sign language interpreting services.
answer_text: The Regional Communication Support Service (RCSS) was introduced on 1st April 2023.  The service is provided by Sign Language Interactions (SLI) by Sorenson is for people who are Deaf, deafblind, or hard of hearing, who will need an interpreter when they are accessing Health and Social Care services across Northern Ireland.  Initial training on the RCSS was delivered to HSC staff by SLI.  This was offered across all HCS Trusts and accessing interpreters in an emergency was covered as part of the training.  SLI are currently developing podcasts which will include how to book interpreters.The Health and Social Care trusts are committed to delivering services that are accessible to people who are deaf and hard of hearing.  Trusts provide a variety of ways that HSC staff are trained in relation to booking sign language interpreting services, which includes emergency situations, and comprises some of the following:staff mandatory regional training, for example, “Equality, Good Relations and Human Rights”; “Making a Difference eLearning” and “Working well with Interpreters” training sessions.HSC staff access to internal staff intranets of which there are interpreting and translation sections; regular updates to booking sign language interpreters; newsletters.promotional material, such as deaf awareness posters / leaflets / desktop messages / screensavers / how to book a sign language interpreter which includes a QR code.supports / guidance for HSC staff to communicate more effectively, for example “Making Communication Accessible”.dedicated trust sensory support teams which provide training and support at a multi-agency level throughout the year; to develop the knowledge of our service and to encourage and provide direction on the use of SL interpreters and sign video.dedicated equality units.deaf awareness training delivered by British Deaf Association (BDA).“Listening Workshop” with the deaf community and trust staff.“Working well with Interpreters” training sessions which includes information on how to book interpreters.toolkits for staffJAM (Just A Minute) card, providing support to those with communication difficulties.HSC Disability Action Plan 2024 – 2029HSC trusts in Northern Ireland worked collaboratively on the development of their new five-year “Disability Action Plan and Equality Action Plan (2024-2029)”.  The plans outline actions and initiatives to address section 75 inequalities, improve equality of opportunity, promote positive attitudes for disabled people and improve their participation in public life.  A specific action is included in the 2024 – 2029 plan in relation to deaf and hard of hearing service users.  This commits all trusts to continue the roll out of the health and social care RCSS for People who are Deaf, deafblind and hard of hearing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he plans to ensure that deaf people can access emergency sign language interpreting services if in need of urgent medical assistance.
answer_text: The Regional Communication Support Service (RCSS) was introduced on 1st April 2023 and offers a range of in-person and remote sign language interpreting services for health and social care appointments.  The service is provided by Sign Language Interactions (SLI) by Sorenson.It is apparent that the number of interpreters is insufficient to meet current and future demands in NI.  SLI are working to increase the number of interpreters in NI.   DfC are currently investing in an Interpreter Training Programme delivered by Foyle Deaf Association which provides for the training and registering of six British Sign Language and four Irish Sign Language interpreters.  Additionally, the Department is investing in a further increase in the numbers of British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language interpreters through funding to Queen’s University Centre for Translation and Interpreting to deliver an MA in Interpreting for up to 20 Deaf and hearing students.  This two-year course launched in September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14759/22-27, for an update on the progress of the waiting list initiative for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery.
answer_text: On the 24th October there were 74 children currently on the waiting list for this elective procedure. However, this number changes regularly with some children getting the procedure carried out whilst other children are added to the waiting list for the procedure.As stated previously my officials have been working with Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) to identify measures in order to try and address this ongoing issue.  The Belfast Trust has commenced additional clinics and theatre sessions to help reduce paediatric waiting times and additional paediatric gastrostomy procedures have been approved through waiting list initiative funding.  The Trust has now scheduled additional weekend theatre sessions dates between October and December to help address the backlog.  A paediatric surgeon has now completed a validation of Peg Tube waiting lists to identify which children would be clinically suitable to transfer to Alder Hey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure. The clinical criteria for transfer has been agreed by the specialist team to determine this. BHSCT is currently in negotiations with Alder Hey Hospital to identify what capacity they may have to offer. However, like RBHSC, they are currently also experiencing unscheduled seasonal pressures. The capacity required will be pre-assessment of these complex children, theatre capacity, inpatient bed capacity (children stay between 48-72 hours), parental training to manage the peg tube, equipment and food, and a follow up appointment. Further discussions are also underway with South Eastern Trust to provide the Belfast Trust paediatric surgeon with a surgical list in the Ulster Hospital. Belfast Trust Gastrostomy Specialist Nurses will provide training to staff in South Eastern Trust to help facilitate this. A number of children have been identified to be managed on the South Eastern Trust theatre list.A further review of surgical work performed in RBHSC is underway and any work that can be redirected to District General Hospitals will be transferred. This may free up capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery and other more complex surgery that can only be carried out in RBHSC.The Trust are also examining partnership working with the Private Sector however there are a number of areas that the Trust would need to have assurance on for the delivery of safe and effective care of these complex children many of which have other multiple co-morbidities.My officials will continue to meet with BHSCT to discuss these potential solutions and examine their progress and success.I want to assure you that officials in the Department as well as colleagues in BHSCT are continuing to work hard to try and address this issue but, given the ongoing pressures on the service and demand for theatre capacity, this will mean that this will not be solved immediately.  We will continue to assess and gauge progress on this to ensure that all of the children and young people rece

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14950/22-27, for an outline of actions taken to explore the feasibility of an all-island approach to paediatric pathologist services.
answer_text: I am committed to finding a better resolution to the issue of local access to perinatal and paediatric pathology services. As I noted in my response to AQW 14950/22-27, the prospect of a dedicated paediatric pathology service for Northern Ireland is very remoteWhile my first preference would be the restoration of a dedicated paediatric pathology service for Northern Ireland, such an outcome continues to remain incredibly challenging as as these services are under considerable pressure across the UK and globally due to a shortage of paediatric pathologists. I also confirmed that I am therefore keen to explore the feasibility of an all-island approach to these services, and recently advised the Health Committee that .  I have had two conversations with Minister Donnelly about the possibility of future co-operation on this issue which, whilst still  and discussions are therefore at the a very early stages of development and consideration, have been very positive. As these services move to more centralised models nationally, In the meantime, my Department’s first priority is to ensure that Northern Ireland families continue to have access to high-quality, reliable and timely pathology services at Alder Hey, to ensure that parents can continue to get the medical answers they need following their loss. Continued access has recently been secured by a further extension of these arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether earnings generated from (i) out-of-hours General Dental Services; (ii) the Personal Dental Services Pilot will be considered eligible for purposes of calculating maternity pay and allowances for General Dental Practitioners.
answer_text: General Dental Services earnings from participation in the Out of Hours scheme are included in the allowances calculations for General Dental Practitioners.Personal Dental Services Pilots are commissioned under various arrangements, determined during the development stage of each pilot. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Rainbow Lodge is currently offering short stay or respite provision.
answer_text: The Northern HSC Trust took over the running of Rainbow Lodge from Praxis Care on 1st April 2024. The facility is now called Eden View.Eden View, is a specialist residential unit in Ballymena for children (aged 5-18 years) with a severe learning disability and severely challenging behaviour providing both short break and long-term residential places. Eden View has 3 full time care residential beds and 3 Short Break beds. The Short Break Beds capacity is dependent on the staff ratio required for each child.Prior to 1st April 2024, the RQIA had imposed a condition that the Short Break Service within Eden View should be closed, due to very critical staffing shortages and this remains in place.The Northern HSC Trust remain in communication with the RQIA with regard to the lifting of this condition in respect of Short Breaks.The Trust is engaged in rolling recruitment, and the staffing situation within Eden View is reviewed daily.The Northern HSC Trust recognises the significant impact that the closure of a Short Break service within Eden View is having for families, and remains committed to resuming the Short Break Service within Eden View as soon as safe staffing levels are achieved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action being taken to address waiting times for an endometriosis diagnosis.
answer_text: Endometriosis is a complex condition that affects patients to differing degrees.  A diagnosis is determined based on the individual’s presenting symptoms and associated medical and surgical history, as well as the results obtained from a wide range of clinical tests and diagnostic procedures. Endometriosis diagnosis takes place as part of the Gynaecology pathway.Patients are managed based on clinical urgency and therefore waiting times can vary depending on each patient’s clinical condition.Following the recent publication of the Getting It Right First Time Report, which provided a review of gynaecology services in Northern Ireland, including those suffering endometriosis, and the 20 recommendations to improve gynaecology services, the Department of Health’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group have established a forum with Trust, GP and PHA representation to oversee ongoing service improvement in this speciality and ensure that this improvement is implemented at a local and regional level. As part of this work, Task and Finish groups are being established to take forward improvement work, and pathway development for a range of areas, including Endometriosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will ensure the Safeguarding Board  for Northern Ireland publishes safeguarding policy advice for organisations online, to ensure all organisations have access to advice on the issue of the safeguarding of young people.
answer_text: Policy guidance relating to child safeguarding/protection has existed for nearly three decades. The latest guidance, Co-operating to Safeguard Children and Young People in Northern Ireland (CTSCYP), was originally published by the Department of Health in March 2016 and re-published in August 2017, following review. This policy replaced the Co-operating to Safeguard Children guidance issued in 2003. It provides the overarching policy framework for safeguarding children and young people in the statutory, private, independent, community, voluntary and faith sectors. It outlines how communities, organisations and individuals must work both individually and in partnership to ensure children and young people are safeguarded as effectively as possible. CTSCYP is underpinned by regional operational child protection policies and procedures which were developed by the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) – a statutory partnership arrangement to safeguard children and young people in Northern Ireland and improve their welfare. The production of child safeguarding policies and procedures is a statutory function of the SBNI. Both CTSCYP and SBNI policies and procedures have been published online and, in line with established policy process, will be subject to further review and revision as necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any engagement her Department has had with the UK Government in relation to reducing VAT for the hospitality, tourism and beauty sectors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether children receive their days in respite which they are owed but were lost due to last-minute cancellations.
answer_text: It is recognised that regionally availability of short breaks / respite for children with disability is a pressure across the HSC Trusts, and my department is aware that families have been impacted by the current limited availability of short break support for their children. Department officials have contacted all five HSC Trusts in respect of the query that you have raised regarding short break / respite cancellations, and if children receive the days that they are owed if these are cancelled.The Belfast HSC Trust has informed my department that there are two facilities that provide overnight short breaks for children within the Belfast Trust area. One of these Short Break facilities is a Trust provision, whilst the other an external provider. The Belfast HSC Trust has stated that if overnight short break provision has had to be cancelled by the Trust provision which is a very rare occurrence, then alternative short break provision is provided to ensure families receive the respite required. The Belfast Trust’s external short break provision has been operational since July 2024, and the Belfast HSC Trust report that no issues of cancellations have been evident to date. However, the Trust has stated that if the provider has to cancel for whatever reason, every effort would be made to make the short break / respite up.The South Eastern HSC Trust has informed my department that in the event of Short Breaks being cancelled at short notice - the Service will adopt a flexible approach and work with the family in a bid to reschedule the provision of the Short Breaks. Furthermore, in the event of rescheduling, the South Eastern HSC Trust advise that the Service will generally provide an alternative to support the family such as community outreach Short Breaks through the community outreach service, LynC outreach. The LynC Outreach Service within SEHSCT focuses on supporting extremely vulnerable young people and their families who are on the edge of care or where their home circumstances are under significant pressures. The LynC Outreach Service consists of a small group of skilled mix staff (from nursing and social care), and the service can provide opportunity for day short breaks for families who are experiencing significant pressures (i.e. through taking the young person out of the family home to engage in activities). The Northern HSC Trust has advised my department that there are several reasons that children may lose out on their respite due to cancellations, either on the part of the provider or occasionally parental cancellation. The Northern HSC Trust informed my department that unfortunately the Trust is not always in the position to be able to provide additional respite to cover those cancellations.  Within the Northern HSC Trust it can also be dependent on the service provision. Through Sharing the Care (short break fostering service) there is more opportunity to offer additional respite for emergency cancellations, however, within the Trus

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the new respite centre in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust will be open and used at full capacity for respite.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust currently delivers residential Short Breaks from the Home at Green Hill located in Newcastle. It is the Trust’s intention to transfer this service to the Redwood site on the Belmont Road in Belfast however, providing a definitive time frame for this proposed move is not possible at this point in time due to the need to upgrade the premises. The Trust is engaged with the Barnardos charity in a bid to secure a lease for the property however, there may be considerable works required to upgrade the Home to current Fire regulations. Barnardos has commissioned a fire risk survey which will inform the next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department's policy on children's respite services includes direction to move children outside Northern Ireland to access these services.
answer_text: It is not my Department's policy to rely on out of jurisdiction placements for residential purposes. Out of jurisdiction placements for assessment and treatment are used in exceptional circumstances and monitored continuously with a view of bringing people supported home at the earliest opportunity.However, it is acknowledged that regionally availability of short breaks is significantly limited. There has been an increase in the number of children and young people with disabilities and there has been an increasing demand for access to short breaks services. Some children and young people with a disability have also required longer-term residential placements, and this has led to occasions when overnight short breaks units have had children and young people placed there for longer than 90 days. This has meant that these units at times cannot maintain a short breaks service.The Department’s strategic response has been set out through a Framework for Children with Disabilities.  This has been partly progressed through the Children’s Services Strategic Reform Programme, including specific workstreams on children with a disability and residential placement capacity.While the implementation of the Framework will take time and investment to improve outcomes, it is recognised that an urgent regional response is required to enable access to short breaks. My officials convened a regional summit with Trusts, RQIA and PHA on 26 September 2024, to identify areas that could be rapidly progressed to address the immediate unmet needs of children with disabilities.  Costed proposals are being finalised for my consideration in the coming days. I fully recognise the current situation is unsustainable and am determined to see urgent improvements in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the link between the Tackling Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime Programme and the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.

To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the policy on third party contributions contained in his Department's 2010 charging guidance, to detail the factors considered in each case when deciding which care home accommodation is appropriate.
answer_text: In relation to placement in a residential care home or nursing home (under Circular HSC (ECCU) 1/2010 - Care Management, Provision of Services and Charging Guidance), case managers will need to ensure, that: • the home has the necessary skills to support and to care for the service user particularly where challenging behaviours are known to be present and risk management processes, where needed, are in place; • appropriate follow-up and access, by the home, to advice and support from HSC Trust staff experienced in managing challenging behaviours is available in relation to the placement and afterwards for as long as is considered necessary; • care plans developed within the home, including management and back-up strategies, are fit for purpose and reviewed on a regular basis dictated by the need of the service user and any presenting risks; and • the home is operating in compliance with relevant regulations and associated standards which will be informed by Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) inspection reports.HSC Trusts must contract for placements at the most competitive rate available, which they consider suitable for meeting the individual’s assessed need even where this is not obtainable at the regional rate.Should a service user select accommodation which is more expensive than other appropriate accommodation which the commissioning Trust can secure at a lower rate (for example, because they wish to have a better room in a particular home or a more expensive care home closer to family), that Trust will facilitate that choice, but the additional cost must be met by a third party such as a family member or friend.  This is known as a top-up. Whilst the aim should always be to facilitate choice for service users’, Trusts have a parallel responsibility to secure the best value for money and protect the public funds for which they are accountable.  Likewise, Independent Care Home providers are entitled to set a level of fee which they believe is financially viable and reflects the service provided by them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Health and Social Care workforce census will be updated to take account of the introduction of the Specialist role, as introduced by the 2021 Specialist Contract.
answer_text: The Specialist medical role was introduced in 2021.  At present, due to low numbers and to minimise the risk of personal disclosure when information is disaggregated by categories such as age group or gender, these Specialist medical staff are included in the SAS grades category within the HSC Workforce Census. As this staff group grows over time, inclusion in the HSC Workforce Census as a separate category will be reviewed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in relation to (i) plans to address the waiting list for paediatric percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tube surgery; and (ii) support currently being provided to children and their families on the waiting list.
answer_text: The issue of access to percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) insertion for children and young people is an important issue as is the range of all surgical procedures that face children and young people in Northern Ireland.  This procedure is a complex procedure that requires specialised clinical and nursing input prior to, during and post procedure.  My officials within SPPG have now carried out some initial scoping on the question that you have raised with senior officials in BHSCT and have been informed of the following plans to help address this issue.A Paediatric Surgeon is currently going through an urgent validation of Peg Tube waiting lists.  This process will identity which children are suitable for potential transfer to Alder Hey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure.  BHSCT are carrying out discussions with Alder Hey to ascertain the feasibility of this arrangement.  If agreed clinical criteria, agreed by the specialist team, will be in place to determine what children will be transferred.Belfast Trust is planning to provide a waiting list initiative in October on a Friday, with patients being nursed in RBHSC over the weekend. This will be dependent on theatre and bed resource availability.  Gastrostomy surgery is also planned in the Ulster Hospital.  Belfast Gastrostomy Nurses will provide training to staff in SET to help facilitate this.There is also currently a review of surgical work performed in RBHSC and any work that can be redirected to District General Hospitals will be transferred.  This may free up capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery.The Trust are also examining the potential of partnership working with Private Sector.  This would mean using RBHSC capacity over the weekend to deliver Waiting List Initiatives in the Children’s Hospital. This will be dependent on theatre and bed resource availability.  (ii) 	All of these discussions are at initial stages. In the meantime, paediatricians and paediatric surgeons along with other clinical teams at the RBHSC will continue to support these children and their families. Whilst children are on the waiting list they will be regularly assessed by paediatricians and paediatric surgeons to assess their clinical status and if there are signs of deterioration they will be reprioritised to mitigate the potential of adverse outcomes.My Officials will continue to meet with BHSCT to discuss these potential solutions to examine their progress and success.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on work to amend the Pharmaceutical Services Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1997 and the Health and Personal Social Services (General Medical Services Contracts) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 to enable dietitians to have supplementary prescribing rights in a primary care setting.
answer_text: Following amendments made in 2016 to the UK-wide Human Medicines Regulations 2012, additional amendments were made to both the Health and Personal Social Services (General Medical Services Contracts) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 and the Health and Personal Social Services Pharmaceutical Services Regulations (NI) 1997 in order to add dietitian supplementary prescribers to those who are able to prescribe under the workings of a General Medical Services Contract and make provision for a community pharmacist to dispense against a HS21 prescription that is written by a dietitian supplementary prescriber. The Pharmaceutical Services (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022 were laid on 8 February 2022 and came into operation from 1 March 2022 and the Health and Personal Social Services (General Medical Services Contracts) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022 were laid on 24 March 2022 and came into operation on 14 April 2022.However, at present there are no models of supplementary prescribing by dietitians in Primary care being delivered in Northern Ireland. A new models of prescribing pilot delivered back in 2021 enabled supplementary prescribing annotated dietitians to provide oral nutritional supplements in care homes and work is ongoing regionally to look at how this can model be progressed. There are also annotated dietitians working in Acute hospital settings across Northern Ireland who are able to practice supplementary prescribing. Physiotherapy, Podiatry and Paramedics are practicing as non-medical prescribers in primary care settings and there are tight governance arrangements in place for these services. These governance arrangements will be mirrored for any new Allied Health Professional service practicing as independent or supplementary prescribers. All staff delivering either supplementary or independent prescribing must have completed the appropriate post graduate training and must be annotated on the Health and Care Professions Council register accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Dental Access Scheme.
answer_text: A Northern Ireland-wide Dental Access Scheme (DAS) commenced on 1st August 2024.  This new scheme aims to improve access (during the working week) for unregistered patients experiencing an emergency, urgent, or pressing dental condition.  There are currently 28 practices participating in the scheme and over 1200 patients were seen as part of the scheme in its first four weeks.  Details of the scheme (including participating practices) can be viewed at:  https://online.hscni.net/dental-access-scheme/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the prescribing rights available to dietitians.
To ask the Minister of Health when he intends to reform the General Dental Services contract.
answer_text: The need for dental reform has been acknowledged for a number of years, however, the pandemic, followed by the absence of an Executive impacted progress in this area.Most recently, efforts have been focused on addressing the immediate access issues patients are experiencing and establishing options to support the sustainability of dental practices while reform is progressed. This has resulted in the £9.2 million additional investment for 2024/25, which was announced on 27 March 2024. Assessment of the initial impact of this investment is currently being undertaken.I am conscious of the developments in the rest of the UK, with Scotland, England and Wales adopting diverging models of dental remuneration. Officials are liaising with their counterparts in each region to monitor the impact of these models and assess the suitability of adopting similar approaches in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the blue-green algae at Ligoniel Dam, North Belfast.
answer_text: Question 9 - Nuala McAllisterTo ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the blue-green algae at Ligoniel Dam, North Belfast.AQO 759/22-27Answer:My officials met with the Chief Executive of the Ligoniel Improvement Association on the 7 August 2024. The Association has responsibility for management of the site. During the meeting officials were able to provide information around water testing, as well as details of DAERA funding channels that could be used to enhance the water environment at the site. The bloom that occurred earlier in the summer was no longer evident at the time of the visit, however I wish to thank the managers of the site for their swift action in warning users at the time once alerted.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will adopt Napoleon's Gate, Shore Road, Belfast, using provisions under the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980.
answer_text: I can confirm that the development at Napoleons Gate, Belfast was formally adopted into the public road network on 27 February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the publication of the Department’s blueprint for the reconfiguration of hospitals.
answer_text: Shortly after taking office, I received a copy of the draft Acute Hospital Reconfiguration Blueprint. I have rightly taken some time to consider it. Since we are in a pre-election period, I will not publish the draft Blueprint before the Westminster Election.However, I can confirm that I intend to publish the draft Blueprint shortly after the upcoming Westminster General Election.It is important to note that the Blueprint will be published in draft form with the intention to commence a pre-consultation engagement exercise with key stakeholders. This pre-consultation engagement exercise will be followed by a full public consultation later in the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how GPs can provide support to those who have received a private diagnosis of Lyme Disease.
answer_text: As independent contractors, GPs are responsible for making a clinical decision on the appropriate care for their patients. If a patient presents to their GP with a private diagnosis of a medical condition, including Lyme Disease, the GP may decide to request any appropriate test to form their own diagnosis as in treating the patient they would be taking on all governance for the safe prescribing and monitoring of a specialist medication which may fall outside their usual scope of professional practice.Shared Care Agreements detail the respective clinical responsibilities of specialists and GPs for prescribing of particular medications which are initiated by specialists, but where it is considered that responsibility for prescribing may be transferred from secondary to primary care once arrangements have been established and agreed by both parties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the guidance provided to GPs in relation to the (i) diagnosis; and (ii) treatment of Lyme Disease.
answer_text: In June 2018, Public Health colleagues wrote to all GP Practices directing them to the NICE Guidelines on the diagnosis and management of Lyme Disease.The NICE guidance included information about awareness, diagnosis, management and information for people with Lyme disease, as well requesting medical practitioners to report all confirmed or suspected cases of Lyme disease to the PHA Duty Room.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to conduct a public health campaign, alongside the Public Health Agency, to raise awareness and encouragement for stem cell donation.
answer_text: Raising awareness about stem cell donation is generally led by a number of charities through UK-wide communications and campaigns which provide information about the signs and symptoms of blood cancer and other blood disorders, and inform people about how they can join the network of national and international stem cell registries.  Registering as a stem cell donor can potentially help a patient in another part of the world for whom a related donor match has not been identified.  My Department has supported previous campaigns to raise awareness about stem cell donation, and plans to do so again this September for Blood Cancer Awareness Month.  It is hoped that this will encourage informed discussion and positive action around this important subject.The Department’s Arm’s Length Bodies, including the Public Health Agency, Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and the Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service have also supported stem cell donation awareness through signposting and providing information about joining the stem cell registries, and promoting the campaign activities of charities and individual families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of specialist posts created since the introduction of the specialist contract in 2021.
answer_text: Since the introduction of the specialist contract in 2021, a total of 39 specialist posts have been created, 29 of which are currently filled and 10 are being recruited to.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the planning approval for the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s new children’s hospital is due to expire.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s New Children’s Hospital planning approval is not subject to expiry as the approved works have commenced.  The scope of the works included within the planning approval for the New Children’s Hospital project included the demolition of Bostock House.  Planning Service deemed that Bostock House demolitions commenced on 14 December 2020 and therefore the planning approval is not subject to expiry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the review of the full business case for the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s new children’s hospital will be completed.
answer_text: The Full Business Case for the new Children's Hospital is under review and will be completed once all relevant documentation has been provided. The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and the Department continue to work towards a business case decision by July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will be engaging with the British Medical Association ahead of industrial action planned between 6 June and 8 June 2024.
answer_text: Unfortunately, I was unable to meet with representatives of the Junior Doctors’ Committee prior to their second 48-hour strike from 6 to 8 June.  I did however meet with representatives of the Junior Doctors’ Committee on 8 July to discuss their pay issues and have outlined my commitment to ongoing engagement.I have also confirmed a meeting with the Chair of the BMA (NI) Council and the individual Committee Chairs on 23 July to discuss the pressures that the HSC will collectively face in 2024 and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he plans to meet relatives of the deceased patients of Michael Watt.
answer_text: A Written Ministerial Statement was published on 3 July 2024, detailing the agreed next steps and future approach in respect of the Neurology Deceased Patients Review. The statement included my commitment to offer to meet with a number of the DPR Phase Two families and arrangements for this will be confirmed in the weeks ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether relatives of the deceased patients of Michael Watt will be reimbursed for legal action taken in the lead up to case reviews.
answer_text: Neurology compensation arrangements were announced by Ministerial Statement to the Assembly on 9 June 2022, by the former Minister of Health. These arrangements included a streamlined process, supported by a set of guiding principles and the potential to instruct a “joint expert” to provide a joint medical report, at no expense to eligible patients/families. The Assembly Statement can be found at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-wms-090622.pdfAs in the normal course of litigation, in the event that legal action is successful, claiming back costs may be possible. Patients and families considering  legal action should discuss costs, potential for claiming back costs and potential available funding with their solicitor at the beginning of the legal process, alongside the merits of their case. The Department’s Neurology Recall Compensation webpage provides further relevant information at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/neurology-recall-compensation

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the review of cases of the deceased patients of Michael Watt will be completed.
answer_text: My Department and the RQIA continue to work together to consider all matters to determine the next steps for the Deceased Patients Review (DPR). From the outset it was envisaged that the DPR would be undertaken with a phased approach.The pace and extent of progress to determine the next steps for the DPR work has been guided by a number of factors such as: the learnings from the work completed to date (including family expectations and experiences, ethical considerations and systems learning); the time requirements for future work; and the resource requirements (including staff and budget requirements).My Department remains mindful of the delicate nature of this work and intends to provide confirmation regarding the DPR next steps as soon as practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the announcement of a £9.2 million dental access scheme on 27 March 2024, to detail why (i) the British Dental Association were not provided the details in advance; (ii) the scheme only applies to the 2024-25 financial year when associated business cases will not be completed until the end of May 2024 or, in some cases, August 2024.
answer_text: Whilst the specifics of the Dental Access Scheme were not discussed with the BDA due to time constraints, it is a scheme based on the already existing Pilot for Unregistered Patients and Asylum Seekers Scheme (PUPAS) that has been in place since October 2022.  Further, before going out to expressions of interest for the scheme, there was detailed engagement with providers.Similarly the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme was a reinstatement of a previously existing scheme that ceased when no further funding could be secured.Business cases for each of the investment streams have all now been completed.  The Department has confirmed that the 30% enhancement to fees associated with fillings extractions and root canal treatment will be applied to all qualifying treatment completed in 2024/25, that will be back-dated to April 2024.  The Enhanced Child Examination Scheme commenced on 10 June. The Dental Access Scheme is a formal pilot Personal Dental Service (PDS) scheme which has a longer operational lead in time and will commence on 1 August 2024. As a pilot PDS, there is a potential to run it for 3 years, pending positive evaluation and continued funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what diagnosis and treatment pathways are available for children suffering from the conditions PANS/PANDAS.
answer_text: I would first of all say that I recognise that this condition causes distress and concern for children and their families.  Although rare there has been increasing occurrences of presentations of Paediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated (PANDA) to paediatric services and Paediatric Acute-onset Neuropsychiatric Syndrome (PANS).Where a presentation occurs, the child will normally be seen at their local District General Hospital by a paediatrician who will assess the symptoms as there are usually a number of symptoms related to this.  The paediatrician will review each presentation and based on this may request additional support from the regional neurological service in BHSCT or from their local CAMHS service, amongst others in order to put in place a treatment plan.Treatment options will vary dependent on the clinical presentation and paediatricians would welcome more information to treat patients more effectively.  Plans are in place regionally to create a clinically led network that allows to clinicians share best practice and gain more understanding of new and emerging issues.  It is hoped that conditions such as PANS/PANDA will be included in this.  It must be noted, as there are also potentially neuropsychiatric implications with this disorder, there will be a need to engage with CAMHS colleagues in Trusts as they have a crucial role to play in care and treatment of children with this condition.We must recognise that there are significant demands on CAMHS services as well as other paediatric services across the HSC, which are impacting on increased waiting times and the SPPG are working with Trusts to identify improvements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether there will be specialist treatment and assessment beds available for those with a learning disability before the proposed closure of Muckamore Abbey Hospital goes ahead; and (ii) whether this will impact the date of the proposed closure.
answer_text: Outside of Muckamore Abbey Hospital there are several commissioned specialist beds to provide inpatient assessment and treatment for adults with learning disabilities.  However, it is recognised that additional beds are required to meet current levels of need and work is underway to ensure the availability of localised assessment and treatment beds in each Trust.A Regional Resettlement Oversight Board has been established to monitor resettlement and maintain momentum.  This also aims to improve the availability of the specialist beds in Northern Ireland.It is further recognised that specialist community assessment and treatment services need to be expanded to improve mental health outcomes for this population and reduce the level of need for inpatient services.  To that end, my Department is finalising a service model for learning disability which aims to provide a regionally consistent service that better responds to need at an earlier stage in the community.A draft model has been developed and shared with the sector for feedback.  Work is already underway to develop a costed implementation plan and my officials are engaging more widely with relevant stakeholders in anticipation of a public consultation.     The closure of the hospital is dependent on all the remaining in-patients being successfully resettled to new homes in the community. The most recent assessment from the Regional Resettlement Oversight Board is that not all of the patients remaining in the hospital will be resettled before the end of June.  In light of this, I announced on 5 June a short extension to the timeframe for the closure of the Hospital.  The safety and well-being of the remaining patients is of paramount importance to me, and I am determined that each of the remaining patients is afforded the necessary time to enable them to successfully transition to their new homes. The resettlement process, particularly for those patients who have spent a considerable part of their lives in Muckamore, can be complex and any attempt to rush this work risks the breakdown of individual community placements. I know such breakdowns can be a difficult and traumatic experience for patients and for their families.In recognition of this, I have decided that setting a further deadline at this point for the hospital’s closure is not helpful, but I want to make very clear that this does not mean there is any change to the policy position, which remains that no-one should be required to live in a hospital.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will include (i) the protection; and (ii) the examination of burial sites of babies in the terms of reference of an inquiry into Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.
answer_text: The proposed public inquiry legislation will have powers equivalent to those provided for in the Inquiries Act 2005 as recommended in the Truth Recovery Design Panel Report. The primary purpose of the inquiry will be to inquire into the facts to establish what happened, why it happened and who was accountable. The public consultation on the Public Inquiry and Redress Scheme which launched on the 27th June proposed that the Inquiry will have investigatory powers to compel the production of evidence in relation to the deaths and burials of those admitted in the institutions, or those born while their mothers where in the relevant institutions. Establishing the evidence base in relation to such deaths, including any discrepancies between death and burial records, is a critical first step to ensuring truth, acknowledgement and accountability.  The consultation will provide an important opportunity for victims and survivors and other interested parties to comment in detail on these proposals. This will inform Ministerial decisions on the legislation to be introduced to the Assembly. It will also be an area which the non-statutory Independent Panel will consider in its work. The Inquiry terms of reference will also be informed by the recommendations of the Independent Panel and consultation with the Inquiry Chairperson.    The protection and any examination of burial sites of unmarked graves is a broader issue, extending beyond the planned inquiry. However, given the linkages to Truth Recovery, TEO officials have been engaging with officials in the Department for Communities in relation to this including on the pre-existing powers and duties in relation to protection and examination. We appreciate this is a sensitive issue – we have asked officials to continue to engage and keep us informed on progress on this important issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when a framework for children with disabilities will be made available.
answer_text: A central component of the Learning Disability Strategic Plan is to finalise and implement a draft Framework for Children with Disabilities. The Framework aims to (i) improve the current offer of early help and community-based support; (ii) develop additional effective residential short breaks and short break fostering; (iii) rethink our approach to residential provision and out of home placements; and (iv) improve transition pathways into adulthood.In the past 12 months, work has been progressed to address the healthcare needs of this population and ensure better connectivity to Education. In terms of implementation, recommendations are being progressed through several workstreams as part of the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme, specifically: (i) residential placement capacity; (ii) fostering; (iii) children with disability; and (iv) family support.In terms of next steps, the Framework is currently being finalised and subject to my approval, it will then be published on the Department of Health website. A costed implementation plan will follow.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to commissioning an independent evaluation of the effectiveness of direct payments administered to carers of children and young people with complex needs.
answer_text: The Department of Health is currently in the process of finalising The Children With Disability Framework in response to the increasing challenges faced by families of Children With Disability, and the corresponding difficulties experienced by Trusts in meeting rising demands and increased complexity of need. There has not yet been an independent evaluation of Direct Payments. However, one of the recommendations that is included within The Children’s Disability Framework is that a Task and Finish group should be established to explore the issues in the use of Direct Payments, and to make recommendations for any necessary change. It is acknowledged within the draft Children’s Disability Framework that there is a need to consider how families can be supported to make the most effective use of direct payments. As outlined above, the Department of Health recognises the need to review Direct Payments, and it is anticipated that a review will occur in the near future following finalisation of The Children’s Disability Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of junior doctors currently working on band 3 rotas.
answer_text: Information provided by the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency (the single lead employer for junior doctors in training) shows that there were 241 junior doctors working on a band 3 rota at May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what mechanism is in place to allow available respite provision to be shared across, and between, Health and Social Care Trusts.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work underway to ensure demand for respite provision for children with complex needs and their families is being met.
answer_text: Short Break and respite care provide valuable opportunities for children & young people with complex needs to spend time away from their parents / primary carers, whilst also allowing parents / primary carers to have a break from their caring responsibilities. Across Northern Ireland short breaks for children with disability is delivered by the five Health and Social Care Trusts in partnership with the community and voluntary sector. Access to and the frequency of short breaks is dependent on individually assessed need.It is acknowledged that regionally availability for young people with disability is a pressure across the HSC Trusts.There has been an increase in children & young people reported to have disability and there has been an increasing demand for access to short breaks & respite services. My Department continues to work with each of the HSC Trusts in terms of closely monitoring regional service provision for children with disability. To gain a better understanding of short break provision across the region, my Department has developed a monitoring framework to capture monthly data from individual HSC Trusts – inclusive of waiting lists for short break services within each Trust area for young people with disability. This will assist with informing of regional and local pressures.There has been additional funding provided for services for Children with Disability in recent years. Since 2020, including additional funding for 2023/24, specific recurrent funding of approximately £7.5m has been provided for services and supports for Children with a Disability, which includes over £1m specifically in relation to short breaks (respite) and family support enhancement services.The Children With Disability Framework is also currently being finalised by my Department in response to increasing challenges faced by families of Children With Disability and the corresponding difficulties experienced by Trusts in meeting rising demands and increased complexity of need. It is recognised that a review and reform of how services are structured, delivered and resourced, both from a regional perspective and locally in each of the Trusts is required. This includes the provision of Short Break.  The full implementation of the Children With Disability Framework will better enable Trusts and independent sector providers to respond to the pressures across Health and Social Care, specifically: (i) increasing demand for early and community-based support; (ii) increasing demand for respite care/short breaks; (iii) increasing demand for more bespoke residential placements; and (iv) better planning and management of complex transitions from children to adult services.A Children With Disability Workstream has also been established as part of the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Board.  This workstream will be looking specifically at the regional position in respect of Children With Disability, and will include a focus on short breaks / resp

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what advice he would provide to parents or caregivers to ensure they can access assistance for children with complex needs in an emergency situation.
answer_text: Each HSC Trust has a Children’s With Disability Team, which provides assessment, services and support to children with disability and complex needs in accordance with individually assessed need. The HSC Trust’s Children’s With Disability Teams work with parents and families of children with disability to ensure the holistic needs of the child(ren) are met. Should an emergency situation arise that relates to support required from social services and is outside the normal working hours of the HSC Trust Children With Disability Team (i.e. Mon – Fri 9:00am – 5:00pm) - the Regional Emergency Social Work Service should be contacted by parents / carers for advice, assistance and support.  If an emergency situation occurs for children with complex needs that requires urgent medical attention – parents or carers should contact the emergency health services for immediate assistance (i.e. Northern Ireland Ambulance Service / Hospital A & E).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress of the implementation of recommendations arising from the consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care.
answer_text: Following the publication of the Evaluation and Summary Report on the Reform of Adult Social Care Consultation my Department established the Social Care Collaborative Forum to implement the proposals arising out of the consultation.The Forum provides a formal mechanism for officials and representatives of the Social Care Sector (statutory, voluntary/community and private sectors), to work together as partners to build shared values and deliver improvements that will support and sustain social care now and into the future. The work of the Collaborative Forum is being taken forward through a number of workstreams and an exercise has been undertaken by my officials to align, where appropriate, the recommendations within consultation to these workstreams.  All of the proposed reforms are subject to confirmation of funding and will therefore require prioritisation and planning, and the outlining of a long-term budget to ensure realistic delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether nursing home fees will be considered as part of a review of fees for care homes, as recommended following the consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care.
answer_text: Following the publication of the Evaluation and Summary Report on the Reform of Adult Social Care Consultation my Department established the Social Care Collaborative Forum to implement the proposals arising out of the consultation.The Forum provides a formal mechanism for officials and representatives of the Social Care Sector (statutory, voluntary/community and private sectors), to work together as partners to build shared values and deliver improvements that will support and sustain social care now and into the future. The work of the Collaborative Forum is being taken forward through a number of workstreams, one of which will consider future charging arrangements for nursing and residential care homes as well as third-party top-up fees.The timeline for the work of the Forum is expected to continue into 2025.All of the proposed reforms are subject to confirmation of funding and will therefore require prioritisation and planning, and the outlining of a long-term budget to ensure realistic delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he plans to undertake a review of third-party top-up fees for care homes, as recommended following the consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care.
answer_text: Following the publication of the Evaluation and Summary Report on the Reform of Adult Social Care Consultation my Department established the Social Care Collaborative Forum to implement the proposals arising out of the consultation.The Forum provides a formal mechanism for officials and representatives of the Social Care Sector (statutory, voluntary/community and private sectors), to work together as partners to build shared values and deliver improvements that will support and sustain social care now and into the future. The work of the Collaborative Forum is being taken forward through a number of workstreams, one of which will consider future charging arrangements for nursing and residential care homes as well as third-party top-up fees.The timeline for the work of the Forum is expected to continue into 2025.All of the proposed reforms are subject to confirmation of funding and will therefore require prioritisation and planning, and the outlining of a long-term budget to ensure realistic delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current status of patient access to trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) for new eligible breast cancer patients.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. Where NICE has recommended trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) for use, it is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, where infrastructure is in place and the service has capacity.NICE are currently considering the use of trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) for treating HER2-low metastatic or unresectable breast cancer in adults. A date for final guidance is to be confirmed.Under the extant HSC Managed Entry process, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) which is responsible for commissioning, may look to decisions made by other relevant UK bodies to inform commissioning decisions in the absence of a NICE determination. In this case, trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) is currently accepted for use in Northern Ireland for HER2-low metastatic breast cancer in line with the Scottish Medicines Consortium (SMC) advice and is available to patients in NI on an interim commissioning (cost per case) basis, pending any recommendation from NICE to inform longer-term commissioning decisions.When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdfIn the interim, the Department’s SPPG, in line with the SMC advice, where infrastructure is in place and the service has capacity, will continue to make this drug available to patients in NI on an interim commissioning (cost per case) basis, until the NICE final guidance is published.
