To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 7672/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the policy changes needed to enable independent sector access to NHS Patient Combined Electronic Records, as per Independent Neurology Inquiry recommendations.
answer_text: My Department agrees with the Independent Neurology Inquiry recommendations and supports the positive contribution to patient safety and quality of care that is achievable through access to accurate and timely clinical information throughout the care continuum, based on consent and / or legitimate interest.  As of July 2024, my Chief Digital Information Officer has initiated a Discovery Project, that builds on earlier engagements, through which relevant stakeholders can explore all the options for improved sharing of information between HSC and independent sector providers and develop the framework enabling and underpinning policy requirements.The project will consider professional and clinical matters, legal and contractual issues, Information Governance and consent frameworks, Cyber Security requirements, and adjustments to the existing technical infrastructure required to enable safe, secure and appropriate healthcare information between HSC and our Independent Sector providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department will take to address the pay contracts for junior doctors.
answer_text: My officials have met with representatives from the BMA Junior Doctor Committee on several occasions at which a number of pay related and non-pay related issues where discussed.  The BMA have been advised that any increase in pay can only be delivered through contract reform which would bring efficiency, modernisation of terms and conditions, and improvements for both the junior doctor cohort and for the wider HSC. Such contract reform could be backdated to 1 April 2024 to provide assurance of a commitment to address pay, in parallel with an undertaking from my Department to an intensive programme of work to bring about the required contractual changes.Disappointingly the BMA JDC have unilaterally disengaged from these discussions. They have subsequently advised that their members will be taking 48-hour industrial action in May and June.Despite this my door remains open to the BMA (JDC) to engage in dialogue on these issues.  We have also advised the BMA (JDC) that the Labour Relations Agency is willing to work with both parties to try to find a resolution to the dispute.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce a pay award similar to that in Scotland for junior doctors before the upcoming strikes.
answer_text: I have already made clear my disappointment at the proposed budgetary allocation for my Department in 2024/25. The current allocation represents a 2.3% real terms cut on what my Department spent received last year, this leaves very little room for manoeuvre on pay at this time.   Any additionality for junior doctors can only be delivered through contract reform which can bring efficiency, modernisation of terms and conditions, and improvements for both the junior doctor cohort and for the wider HSC.  My door remains open to the BMA (JDC) to engage in dialogue on these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on potential shortages of arthritis medication.
answer_text: Dependent on the type and severity, there are a wide variety of medications that can be used for the treatment of arthritis, ranging from simple oral and topical analgesics to biological treatments. My Department is not currently aware of supply disruptions for the majority of treatments used for the two most common types of arthritis- rheumatoid and osteoarthritis. However, my Department is aware of a supply disruption to Erelzi® (etanercept) 50mg solution for injection in pre-filled pens, which can be used for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis, and subsequently issued a National Patient Safety Alert on 3 May 2024:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hss-md-13-2024.pdf. It is important to note that medicines are manufactured as part of a global and highly complex industry and therefore shortages of medicines are not uncommon. There are national and locally tried and tested mitigations in place for dealing with any supply disruptions to ensure that patients can continue to get medicine supplies they need, including providing advice to healthcare professionals on appropriate actions to take during periods of disruption, such as National Patient Safety Alerts.The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supplies into the UK and works closely with the other devolved governments, including my Department, and other relevant stakeholders to put in place mitigations should any disruptions arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the protocol for the acceptance of referrals into the Gateway Services team from the PSNI.
answer_text: Children’s Gateway teams consider referrals from all organisations, including the PSNI, utilising the regional UNOCINI guidance and under the statutory responsibility set out in the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 to safeguard and promote the welfare of children within its area who are in need. Gateway teams screen and threshold referrals received to determine the most appropriate response, either through initial assessment by a Social Worker, onward sign posting or no further action. PSNI referrals for alleged or suspected child abuse are considered under the ‘Protocol for Joint Investigation by Social Workers and Police Officer of Alleged or Suspected Cases of Child Abuse, April 2021’. Following a referral under this protocol Gateway teams and PSNI will jointly consider relevant information known and make an assessment of the information, allegation and context before a decision regarding a joint investigation is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on measures he intends to take to address issues associated with blue-green algae in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: There are no quick fixes for the blue green algal blooms at Lough Neagh.  It is widely accepted that the blooms are highly likely to recur both in 2024 and in subsequent years. Real and sustained improvements will take decades.However, I am pleased to confirm that my Department is already taking forward a range of measures aimed at addressing the problem. DAERA is leading a cross-departmental Water Quality Steering Group that is developing a Lough Neagh Report, this will include actions to tackle blue-green algae and secure longer-term improvements in water quality across Northern Ireland.Once the Environmental Improvement Plan has received Executive approval I will move to publish without delay. Improving water quality will require a significant investment. Inadequate resources will have a detrimental impact on funding the interventions needed.Failing to address the problems will, in the longer term, result in the significant impacts we saw in 2023 becoming the norm. This is simply not something I will contemplate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of long Covid services being conducted by (i) his Department; and (ii) the Public Health Agency.
answer_text: My Department is working with the Public Health Agency (PHA) on an evaluation of long COVID services.  This includes an assessment of the ongoing demand for services; a review of current service provision; and, taking cognisance of service developments elsewhere in the UK. The evaluation is anticipated to be completed by early summer 2024 and where there is a new service model, this will be subject to normal business case approvals.As per above, my Department is working with the PHA on this evaluation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how funding will be used in the 2024-25 financial year to support those with long Covid.
answer_text: Funding of £1.9m will be used in the 2024-25 financial year to continue to provide the services that have been established in all 5 HSC Trusts to support adults living with this condition:Strand 1 - Post-COVID-19 syndrome (PCS) patients referred by primary or secondary care to a one-stop-shop MDT assessment servicePatients can be referred to the MDT assessment clinic from primary and secondary care.  Patients receive an holistic assessment to include:identifying patients with breathlessness who may benefit from pulmonary rehabilitation; and, those who may need onward referral to secondary or community services and/or signposting to self-management resources.Strand 2: - Bespoke pulmonary rehabilitation / dysfunctional breathing service for patients with significant respiratory symptoms post-COVID-19The purpose of this strand is to put in place timely, effective arrangements for outpatient rehabilitation following COVID-19 resulting in significant respiratory symptoms.  Strand 3 - Patients discharged from critical care (both COVID-19 and non-COVID-19)The purpose of this strand is to put in place timely, effective arrangements for the follow-up and assessment of people discharged from critical care (both COVID-19 and non-COVID-19), reflecting the known risk of long-term sequelae following a long stay in critical care.Strand 4 - Strengthening psychology support to all TrustsThe psychological impact of COVID-19 (depression, anxiety disorders, PTSD, traumatic bereavement, psychosis, risk to self and/or others) is recognised in extant national guidance.  Each Trust has been supported to recruit an additional senior psychologist, with their main role supporting strand 3, but also providing advice to staff working in strands 1 and 2.Strand 5 - Regional MDT clinical physiology, SLT and dietetic support Selected patients may need diagnostic tests by respiratory clinical physiologists, onward referral for dietetic and speech and language therapy (SLT) from the one-stop-shop MDT assessment service, the critical care follow-up and pulmonary rehabilitation strands.  Strand 6 - Signposting and access to self-management resourcesPatients in NI can access self-management resources which include the NHS App Your Covid Recovery (Managing The Effects | Your COVID Recovery) In line with clinical advice from paediatric specialists in Northern Ireland, children who have not reached their 16th birthday and who have persistent symptoms after a diagnosis of COVID, and who require more specialist care, can be referred to Trust paediatric services in the usual way to receive appropriate assessment and treatment in line with their individual clinical needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many children are currently residing in Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre.
answer_text: On 30 April 2024, there were 8 children residing in Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre. In the last 12 months, the number of children residing in Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre has fluctuated between 3 and 21.Every autumn, NISRA publishes annual Youth Justice Workload Statistics which include a range of data on Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre.  The most recent report covers 2022-23 and is available on the Department of Justice web-site at:  Youth Justice Annual Workload Statistics 2022-23 | Department of Justice (justice-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how Health and Social Care Trusts are ensuring more staff are on site at each Emergency Department when the roll-out of the Encompass programme begins in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in June.
answer_text: The encompass programme deployment plans provide for significant support for Trust staff during the period of a Go-Live with specially trained Super Users and Floor Walkers available on Trust sites, and technical staff from Epic and encompass providing off-site support on a 24/7 basis during that period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there will be resources from the digital infrastructure teams from the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and his Department on site at each hospital location in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust for the roll-out of the Encompass programme.
answer_text: The encompass programme will provide significant support for Belfast Trust during the period of Go-Live. There will be approximately 1,000 Super Users and Floor Walkers on Trust sites, with technical staff from EPIC and the encompass programme providing off-site support on a 24/7 basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the commissioning of (i) Atogepant for preventative use; and (ii) Rimegepant for preventative and acute use, for the treatment of migraines.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland. This process is relevant to the commissioning of treatments for migraines.NICE are currently considering the use of atogepant (Aquipta®) for preventing migraine and a final determination is expected to be published in May 2024. Under the extant HSC Managed Entry process, my Department may look to decisions made by other relevant UK bodies to inform commissioning decisions in the absence of a NICE determination. In this regard, Atogepant (Aquipta®) is already accepted for use for the prophylaxis of migraine in adults who have at least 4 migraine days per month in line with advice from the Scottish Medicines Consortium (SMC2259). This position will be reviewed following publication of final NICE guidance, to inform long term commissioning decisions.In Northern Ireland, rimegepant (Vydura®) is accepted for use as an option for the acute treatment of migraine with or without aura in adults (NICE TA919) and as an option for preventing episodic migraine in adults who have at least 4 and fewer than 15 migraine attacks per month, only if at least 3 preventative treatments have not worked (TA906).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Mobile Health Unit run by the Welcome Association.
answer_text: I understand that the Mobile Health Unit run by the Welcome Organisation brings healthcare to people affected by homelessness ‘where they are at’. The Welcome organisation works alongside healthcare professionals within the Belfast Inclusion Health Service and the Western Trust to provide a range of services including drop-in clinics, vaccine boosters, sexual health checks and ‘on the ground’ healthcare and more.The needs of those experiencing homelessness are often complex and I am fully supportive of services which help improve the accessibility of healthcare for this cohort.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will introduce electronic prescribing for GPs.
answer_text: I can confirm that the Strategic Planning and Performance Group in my Department is currently leading on the ePharmacy Programme. The ePharmacy Programme includes the Electronic Transfer of Prescriptions (ETP) Project to replace the current hand-written prescription system in Northern Ireland with an electronic system. The ETP project is currently at Outline Business Case (OBC) stage and undergoing discovery work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how much funding is provided to train specialist nurses, which will help free up doctors.
answer_text: Post registration education is commissioned to ensure the Nursing and Midwifery workforce is adequately equipped with the necessary skills and knowledge to deliver safe and effective care and transformational change.  It is not linked to a Doctor substitution model.Numbers and costs vary from year to year depending on population health and service sustainability needs. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how much funding is currently being provided for public awareness campaigns around cancer symptoms.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his consideration of legislation and policy options following the consultation on Minimum Unit Pricing in 2022.
To ask the Minister of Health how he plans to prioritise actions to be implemented from the Cancer Strategy.
answer_text: A Cancer Programme Board was established in November 2022 as part of the development of cancer programme governance structures to provide strategic oversight and decision making in relation to the implementation of Cancer Strategy actions. Departmental Officials have been working closely with Cancer Programme Board members to ensure that funding allocated to date has been provided for the progression of identified strategy priorities. Further prioritisation of any additional cancer strategy actions other that those being currently delivered will not be possible without further significant investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his plans to improve air quality.
answer_text: My officials have been progressing work on Northern Ireland’s first Clean Air Strategy in the absence of an Executive.Now that the Executive has returned, I will work with my officials and Ministerial colleagues to finalise this important cross-cutting strategy.While I have yet to make my decision on the policies and actions to be included, once finalised, this Strategy will be central to improving air quality for all in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to implement robust cancer prevention strategies as highlighted in the Cancer Strategy.
answer_text: Action 1 of the Cancer Strategy seeks to "increase public awareness of cancer-related risk factors through specific strategies on tobacco, substance use, skin cancer prevention, and overweight and obesity – including diet and physical exercise”.Making Life Better (MLB), the Executive’s overarching strategic framework, seeks to support the delivery of this action through work to improve health and address health inequalities. MLB is supported by a number of DoH led strategies that are designed to help improve population health and wellbeing through targeting specific areas where health inequalities are most prominent in our society. These include:The 2011 Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy which has been extended to September this year. We will shortly consider next steps in this area.The Tobacco Control Strategy for NI which has been extended and continues to provide strategic direction in this area of work. Work on a successor strategy has been delayed by current demands of potential NI inclusion in the UK Government Tobacco and Vapes Bill. We will recommence that work as soon as we can.The Department recently consulted on a new obesity strategic framework - 'Healthy Futures - A Strategic Framework to Prevent the Harm caused by Obesity, and Improve Diets and Levels of Physical Activity in Northern Ireland'. Officials are reviewing and collating the consultation responses on the Healthy Futures strategic framework and will prepare consultation outcome reports for consideration in due course. Work to develop specific actions and outcomes will follow.The substance use strategy, Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, recognises the impact the use of alcohol and other drugs can have on cancer-related risk and seeks to inform and educate the public about this, and provide services which reduce substance use related harm.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on a new Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy.
answer_text: Work is well advanced on the new 7 year Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy for the period 2024-2031. It is jointly led by my Department and the Department of Health, with input from the Department of Education, the Department for Communities and the Executive Office.  Under this new Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy we plan to take forward a range of work to protect and support victims and to address abusive behaviours which will benefit all victims of domestic and sexual abuse.  The draft Strategy consists of 5 pillars; Partnership, Prevention, Children and Young people, Support and Provision and Justice.   I want to thank voluntary and community sector partners who have  provided invaluable input and helped reflect the brave voices and opinions of those with lived experience in the Strategy.  I am confident that this will lead to a more effective strategy that really makes a difference to those affected by domestic and sexual abuse and their families. I recognise the need for alignment with the Executive Office’s Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy; this has been pivotal in our approach.   I believe it is appropriate that we have two strategies and it is important that we recognise the distinctions between them (that is, that the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy will encompass all victims irrespective of age, race, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation, ethnicity, and background, and the EVAWG Strategy will also need to address wider forms of gender-based violence predominantly affecting women and girls).  Nonetheless it is crucial that we recognise their interdependencies and work collectively to ensure that there is alignment and that they complement and reinforce each other. My Department will continue to work collaboratively with TEO to ensure join up, amplify our collective responses and provide effective responses for victims.  Officials from my Department and from the Department of Health are due to brief our respective Committees on 30 May and a date is currently being sought for clearance by the Executive. I intend to publish the Strategy, Performance Framework and Action plan, jointly with the Minister for Health in June.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to reform the third party contribution system for residential and nursing homes.
answer_text: Arrangements for charging for personal social services provided in residential care homes and nursing homes, including third party top ups is detailed in Departmental guidance ECCU 1/2010 ‘Care Management, Provision of Services and Charging Guidance’ (https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dhssps/hsc-eccu-1-2010.pdf).  My Department’s Social Care Collaborative Forum provides a formal mechanism for officials and representatives of the Social Care Sector  (statutory, voluntary/community and private sectors), to work together as partners to build shared values and deliver improvements that will support and sustain social care now and into the future.  As part of its remit, the Forum will review proposals arising out of the consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care which included future charging arrangements within the sector. Progress against all aspects of the Forum’s work will be reported on and where specific proposals are being made in respect of charging for care these will be subject to public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the £23,250 capital threshold for financial support with residential and nursing home fees was last reviewed.
answer_text: The capital threshold is reviewed annually with the last assessment completed in February 2024.Given the ongoing significant financial challenges facing the HSC in Northern Ireland and in light of the wider considerations on the Reform of Adult Social Care, I have kept capital limits at their existing level for the 2024/25 financial year.The Charging for Residential Accommodation Guide (CRAG) circular advising of the current capital limits was issued on 21 March 2024 and has been published on the Departmental Website at the following page:- https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/guidance-charging-residential-accommodation

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline plans for the continuation of the Urban Villages Initiative.
answer_text: The Urban Villages programme has been successful in working with communities to develop Strategic Frameworks and having a presence on the ground through Local Area Teams. There have been many positive outcomes such as new play parks, community shared spaces and learning events. A copy of the latest Programme Evaluation is available in the Assembly Library TEO officials have developed a range of options on the future of the programme. We look forward to updating Members once we have considered how best to continue with our aim of promoting thriving places and achieving the best outcomes for all our citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what sustainable solutions he plans to implement to ensure the bus lane on Antrim Road, Belfast, remains clear during its hours of operation.
answer_text: In 2015 the Department became responsible for the enforcement of Moving Traffic Offences (MTOs) in bus lanes in the Greater Belfast area.The Antrim Road Bus Lane currently operates as a tidal flow Bus Lane. The hours of operation are, Southbound: 07:30 - 09:30 and Northbound: 15:30 - 18:30, and enforcement consists of A mobile CCTV enforcement vehicle that patrols the bus lane during the morning and evening peak periods, Traffic Attendants on scooters who patrol the route every morning and afternoon Monday – Friday; andThe Remove and Clamp operation will also patrol once-a-day as a minimum Monday to Friday.It is anticipated that the proposed G3 route will run along the Antrim Road, and we expect the hours of operation will be the same as the East/West Glider route i.e. 7am to 7pm Monday to Saturday. As such, my Department would envisage an increase in demand for MTO enforcement to manage the proposed increase of hours of operation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of pathways for referrals to clinical psychology and mental health services for stroke patients, which was due to be completed in March 2024 as set out in the Reshaping Stroke Care Action Plan.
answer_text: Clinical psychology is a core discipline in the multidisciplinary stroke team, providing direct care and supporting all members of the stroke multidisciplinary team engaged with those who experience psychological problems following a stroke.The full implementation of clinical psychology pathways has been delayed by workforce and financial challenges. Four of the five Health & Social Care Trust funded clinical psychology posts have been vacant despite attempts to fill. Also, there is recognition that funded levels in Northern Ireland are below those recommended in the National Clinical Guidelines for Stroke, UK & Ireland in a difficult financial climate. However, the NI Stroke Network is supporting the scoping of alternative pathways for clinical psychology intervention as well as the preceptorship model for career progression to support recruitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any additional measures he plans to introduce to address pavement parking.
answer_text: During November 2023 the Department made legislation that added to the list of locations where parking wholly or partly on a footway is prohibited to now include beside bus lanes, bus stops, mandatory cycle lanes and mandatory school keep clears. The Department routinely reminds drivers to ‘think before you park’ and continues to take action against vehicles that are in contravention of parking restrictions, including those that apply to footways.  On 6 December 2023 the Department released a Findings Report on the responses received to the consultation on inconsiderate pavement parking.  I plan to issue a formal response to the consultation exercise when I have had time to consider the Findings Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider increasing the (i) hourly rate of payment; and (ii) preparation fee for NHS interpreters, which has not been reviewed for 18 years.
answer_text: Face-to-face spoken language interpreters are independent self-employed contractors who are not employed by the Health and Social Care (HSC) service but are paid for their service through the Business Services Organisation (BSO) at an agreed rate.HSC Interpreters must be registered with BSO and adhere to the HSC Terms of Engagement.  This stipulates an hourly rate of pay as well as a preparation payment.  The Department is aware that pay has not increased for a number of years and the impact this is having on the valuable service that interpreters provide. You will however be aware of the significant financial constraints within the wider Health Service.  There has been a number of correspondences to my office regarding this issue.  As a result, I have asked the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to review (i) the hourly rate of pay and (ii) the preparation fee for interpreters.I will endeavour to do the best I can to come to a suitable resolution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the introduction of the Footways (Prohibition of Waiting) Order (Northern Ireland) 2023, to detail the guidance which has been issued to traffic attendants regarding pavement parking.
answer_text: The Footways (Prohibition of Waiting) Order (Northern Ireland) 2023 came into operation on the 3rd November 2023. To enable the enforcement of these new restrictions, several new contravention codes were created as follows:Code 34P - Parked on a footway adjacent to a Bus Lane.Code 47F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a Bus Stop / Stand.Code 48F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a Mandatory School Keep Clear.Code 49F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a Mandatory Cycle Lane Traffic Attendants (TAs) are employed by NSL Ltd and prior to the enforcement of these new contraventions, my Department provided NSL with details of the exemptions, and the observation and grace periods associated with each contravention. NSL, in turn, trained their TAs on how to enforce the new contraventions.  Further details of these new contraventions can be found at the following link https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/penalty-charge-notice-pcn-contravention-codes

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the forthcoming amended EU Mercury Regulation, to detail actions and timescales his officials will be working to, to avoid direct application of a forthcoming ban or phaseout of dental amalgam from applying in Northern Ireland from 1 January 2025.
answer_text: The current position in the UK is that the four UK Chief Dental Officers and their respective Health Departments have incorporated measures to phase down the use of dental amalgam, rather than phase out. These include factors to minimise environmental discharge, improve collection of amalgam waste and changes in service delivery and support for alternative materials.  This remains the policy position and preference of the Department.In relation to timescales, whilst the text of the proposed new EU laws has been agreed between EU institutions, a plenary vote is still required.  We expect this to take place in April 2024, with publication occurring thereafter, at which point the Democratic Scrutiny Committee (DSC) is likely to receive formal notification from the UK Government and may commence a formal Inquiry.In anticipation of a notification, the committee has sought briefings from interested parties, including the British Dental Association, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Department of Health.In parallel, my Department has established an NI Mercury Working Group so that stakeholders from DoH, DAERA and others can monitor the passage of the new regulations, assess impacts, engage with the DSC process and mitigate risks as far as possible for patients and practices in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department’s policy on dental amalgam use continues to be for a phasedown approach, as stated in the 2019 Northern Ireland Plan to Phase down the use of Dental Amalgam.
answer_text: The current position in the UK is that the four UK Chief Dental Officers and their respective Health Departments have incorporated measures to phase down the use of dental amalgam, rather than phase out. These include factors to minimise environmental discharge, improve collection of amalgam waste and changes in service delivery and support for alternative materials.  This remains the policy position and preference of the Department.In relation to timescales, whilst the text of the proposed new EU laws has been agreed between EU institutions, a plenary vote is still required.  We expect this to take place in April 2024, with publication occurring thereafter, at which point the Democratic Scrutiny Committee (DSC) is likely to receive formal notification from the UK Government and may commence a formal Inquiry.In anticipation of a notification, the committee has sought briefings from interested parties, including the British Dental Association, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Department of Health.In parallel, my Department has established an NI Mercury Working Group so that stakeholders from DoH, DAERA and others can monitor the passage of the new regulations, assess impacts, engage with the DSC process and mitigate risks as far as possible for patients and practices in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the introduction of the Footways (Prohibition of Waiting) Order (Northern Ireland) 2023, to detail (i) the number of parking tickets issued for vehicles parked on pavements; and (ii) the number of tickets issued for the same period in the preceding year.
answer_text: The Footways (Prohibition of Waiting) Order (Northern Ireland) 2023 came into operation on the 3rd November 2023. To enable the enforcement of these new restrictions, several new contravention codes were created as follows:Code 34P - Parked on a footway adjacent to a Bus Lane.Code 47F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a Bus Stop / Stand.Code 48F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a Mandatory School Keep Clear.Code 49F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a Mandatory Cycle Lane  The number of PCNs issued against these new contraventions since they were introduced in November 2023 are detailed in the table below. As these are new contraventions, there is no comparable figures for the same period last year.Contravention CodeTotal number of PCNs (3rd Nov. 23 - 12th Mar. 24)Code 34P108Code 47F51Code 48F0Code 49F5However, there are other contravention codes that have existed prior to November 2023 that relate to footway parking and the number of PCNs issued for these contraventions since November 2023 and for the same period last year are detailed in the table below.Contravention CodeTotal number of PCNs (3rd Nov. 22 - 12th Mar. 23)Total number of PCNs (3rd Nov. 23 - 12th Mar. 24)46F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a clearway17160199F - Parked on a footway adjacent to a controlled area at Zebra, Pelican, and Puffin crossings. 3388Finally, it is not possible to detail the total number of PCNs issued to vehicles parked on a footway as parking restrictions marked on the road, for example yellow lines, also apply to the footway or verge. As PCNs do not record the location of the vehicle, it is not possible to distinguish between those PCNs issued to vehicles parked on the footway and those parked on the road. Please also note that parking on a footway causing an obstruction is not a decriminalised offence and responsibility for this remains with the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work ongoing in his Department for the prevention of Type 2 diabetes.
answer_text: The Department of Health requires the delivery of a regional, evidence-based diabetes prevention programme for NI by the Public Health Agency (PHA). The overall aim of the programme is to reduce the incidence of Type 2 Diabetes by identifying individuals at high risk and offering referral to an evidence-based behaviour change programmeThe Diabetes Prevention Programme was commissioned and regionally implemented by PHA to be delivered across all five Heath and Social Care Trusts. PHA invests in a number of obesity prevention programmes that align to diabetes prevention and continues to work with a range of other partners to promote activities that will encourage healthy lifestyles that contribute to obesity prevention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current investment from his Department into the prevention of Type 2 diabetes.
answer_text: Risk factors for type 2 diabetes include genetic factors, age and ethnicity.  However, being overweight or obese (BMI of 25kg/m2 or more) is the main modifiable risk factor.  A large number of cases of Type 2 Diabetes could be prevented or delayed by addressing being overweight or obese.  PHA invests circa £4million in a number of obesity prevention programmes that align to diabetes prevention.  Work continues with a range of other partners to promote activities that will encourage healthy lifestyles that contribute to obesity prevention.  The main programmes PHA commissioned and / or supported are grouped into three themes: healthy eating, weight management, and physical activity.  Campaigns, leaflets and resources are also produced on these themes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions have been taken by his Department following meetings with the Improving Doctor and Dentist Working Lives Group (i) during the previous mandate; and (ii) since February 2024.
answer_text: The Improving Junior Doctors and Dentists Working Lives Group last met 27/04/2021 at which a proposal for moving functions from IJDDWLG to alternative arrangements was discussed. Tripartite communications were undertaken between the BMA, Employing Organisations, and the Department to design a better way of working.  Final responses were received at the end of August 2023.  However, as responses were being reviewed and collated the BMA indicated that they would be balloting members on their willingness to take industrial action at which point in time this work was put on hold. Once industrial action has ceased, and normal industrial relations have resumed my officials will recommence this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the Business Improvement District process.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he will continue to co-fund the Active School Travel Programme for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: The current Active School Travel programme is jointly funded by the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) and the Public Health Agency (PHA) and is delivered by Sustrans. 		The current Active School Travel programme contract is held by DfI and it is due to cease on 31st July 2024. The PHA remain fully committed to the programme and have secured funding to continue to co-fund the programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many specialty doctor posts have been created since the introduction of the specialty doctor contract in 2021, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health when a response will be issued to the open letter from the British Dental Association, which was co-signed by 720 general dental practitioners and delivered to the Department of Health on 30 January 2024.
answer_text: A response to the open letter was combined with responses to other British Dental Association correspondence to the Department, and was issued on 7 March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the development of investment proposals to further support general dental services.
answer_text: I am currently considering investment proposals to further support General Dental Services into 2024/25, which will be shared with the British Dental Association in due course.While the arguments for investment into General Dental Services are strong, the fiscal situation continuing into the coming financial year remains uncertain.  It is likely that our budget will constrain the options available, and decisions must be made with this in mind.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider reinstating the Financial Support Scheme and Rebuilding Support Scheme to support general dental services.
answer_text: The Financial Support Scheme and its replacement the Rebuilding Support Scheme were put in place to sustain General Dental Services practices throughout the pandemic when capacity was severely impacted by covid related restrictions. Through these schemes, the Department provided significant and unprecedented financial support, with approximately £88.1m invested to initially maintain the financial viability of practices and subsequentially help increase levels of Health Service activity. The Rebuilding Support Scheme came to an end in July 2023 due to the absence of covid-related restrictions and the severe financial pressures arising from the Department’s 2023/24 budget settlement.Whilst there are no plans to re-establish the schemes, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on discussions to progress the recommended 2023-24 pay uplifts for dentistry.
To ask the Minister of Health what the target number of specialist nurses in motor neurone disease is by the end of the 2027-28 financial year.
answer_text: The Regional Review of Neurology Services is tasked with identifying the workforce and training needs required to underpin Neurology services going forward. In this context the Review has been considering workforce pressures in the round rather than for specific neurological conditions. In terms of the Nursing workforce the Review Team has undertaken a Nursing Workforce Review to identify the overall Nursing requirement to support Neurology services. Regarding the Consultant workforce, the Review has focused on measures to support the Neurology Training Programme to grow the overall workforce in line with a Workforce Planning Report undertaken by the Public Health Agency. It is intended that the Review, including final recommendations on workforce requirements, will be completed later in the spring.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work is ongoing to deliver improved and faster diagnosis of motor neurone disease.
answer_text: My department is leading on the Regional Review of Neurology Services which is tasked with identifying the optimum configuration of neurology services for the next 10-15 years. Several workstreams have been established in priority areas including workforce, long term and complex conditions and care coordination and are aimed at improving access. The Review is tasked with identifying the optimum configuration of neurology services for the next 10-15 years.  The work of the Review is being taken forward through workstreams in a number of priority areas some of which will have an impact on the development of MND services.It is anticipated that the Final Report of the Review will be completed in May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what learning his Department has taken from neighbouring jurisdictions regarding a clear pathway for people diagnosed with motor neurone disease.
answer_text: As part of the Regional Review of Neurology Services, work has been undertaken to develop draft condition-specific pathways for those with long term neurological conditions, including motor neurone disease.  This work has been informed by a comprehensive review of approaches taken elsewhere and by existing national guidelines, pathways and standards. It is intended that the completion of the pathways will be an initial priority during the implementation period of the Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work is underway to increase the number of consultants with specialist knowledge of motor neurone disease.
answer_text: The Regional Review of Neurology Services is tasked with identifying the workforce and training needs required to underpin Neurology services going forward. In terms of the Nursing workforce the Review Team has undertaken a Nursing Workforce Review to identify the overall Nursing requirement to support Neurology services. It is intended that the Review, including final recommendations on workforce requirements, will be completed later in the spring.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the outstanding development proposal for Belfast Boys’ Model School.
answer_text: A feasibility report in respect of the additional accommodation resulting from the approval of Development Proposal 545 has been drafted and the contractor responsible for the public private partnership has returned a high-level cost estimate. However, due to the extremely constrained capital budget, it has not been possible to commission fully costed design options for the scheme, which is currently on hold until capital funding is in place.  As I outlined in my Statement to the Assembly on Monday 12 February, I intend to bring to the Executive a significant bid for capital investment across the Schools’ Estate in 2024/25 and subsequent years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on work within his Department to review personal indemnity arrangements for GPs.
answer_text: I am aware that arrangements for clinical negligence indemnity are a matter of concern for GPs locally and I am committed to identifying solutions to address this issue for Northern Ireland GPs.  Recognising this, my Department recently reallocated funding from other areas of the GP budget to boost capacity in the service and mitigate demand and capacity pressures; this funding included support for additional costs of indemnity to enhance the capacity of the system to manage demand and was on a non recurrent basis. My Department is continuing to take forward work to look at the future model of GP indemnity provision in Northern Ireland. A number of possible options have been identified as part of this work, ranging from the current model to state intervention that includes a number of state backed model options. Departmental officials are continuing to take forward options appraisal through the business case process which will conclude with the identification of a proposed preferred way forward for indemnity arrangements in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will remove personal indemnity costs borne by GPs.
answer_text: I am aware that arrangements for clinical negligence indemnity are a matter of concern for GPs locally and I am committed to identifying solutions to address this issue for Northern Ireland GPs. Departmental officials are continuing to take forward options appraisal through the business case process which will conclude with the identification of a proposed preferred way forward for indemnity arrangements in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of the GP Access group in his Department.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of access to GP services for patients.  My Department established a GP Access Working Group, which includes  GP and service-user representation, to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients.  The working group has engaged with Department Communications Team around patient messaging and is exploring how telephony and related technologies can be best deployed to deliver improvements to the process for accessing services for patients and practices alike.Work is also ongoing to develop guidance for practices in the management of patient demand and optimising workflow within practices to maximise efficiency.   The GP Access Working group is an important part of wider work to ensure that patients across all Northern Ireland can benefit from timely access to high quality, sustainable services in their local communities both now and in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what additional analysis his Department is conducting to inform the future model of hyperacute stroke care.
answer_text: In order to support the identification of a preferred option for Hyperacute Stroke Care, a new process has been agreed by the Hyperacute Stroke Project Board set up to oversee this work.  This first stage of this process requires the commissioning of analysis which will update and extend prior analysis undertaken in 2018. This will include consideration of stroke pathways, updated stroke activity levels including projections arising from demographic changes and an assessment of improved outcomes. Work is currently underway on a communication and engagement plan to ensure stakeholder involvement in the process. The engagement will extend to people affected by stroke, the stroke workforce and the wider public. This work will be reviewed by the Project Board when it next meets in March 2024. It is intended that stakeholders will be updated following that meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department plans to engage with (i) people affected by stroke; (ii) people delivering stroke care; and (iii) the general public to co-produce a future model of stroke services.
answer_text: In order to support the identification of a preferred option for Hyperacute Stroke Care, a new process has been agreed by the Hyperacute Stroke Project Board set up to oversee this work. The engagement will extend to people affected by stroke, the stroke workforce and the wider public. This work will be reviewed by the Project Board when it next meets in March 2024. It is intended that stakeholders will be updated following that meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will consider strengthening childcare subsidies for students with childcare responsibilities.
answer_text: I recognise the rising cost of childcare and the impact this has on families who are already struggling with the cost of living. I also recognise that the high cost of childcare prevents many parents, particularly mothers, from working.My Department currently provides a suite of pathways for childcare training and qualifications through Further and Higher Education, and through vocational qualifications like apprenticeships and traineeships. I have asked my officials to carry out an audit of what we have to offer in this area. This audit will help us determine any gaps in provision or if there is a need for additional financial assistance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what engagement she has had with Treasury to ensure Northern Ireland is funded according to need.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his consideration of the scenarios presented in the Placemaking and Active Travel Review report for the York Street Interchange project.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.In October 2022 I published a Placemaking and Active Travel Review (PATR) report on the scheme and asked my officials to carry out further work on three scenarios recommended within it. This work is expected to conclude in Spring 2024. Details of the current status of the York Street Interchange project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/york-street-interchange-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the effect the withdrawal of funding from Community Foundation NI will have on other statutory services, given the level of assistance provided by the groups who use this funding.
answer_text: In March 2021, the Department launched the Mental Health Support Fund, the Cancer Charities Support Fund, and the Support for Carers Fund. These funds were established from unspent money, previously allocated to Covid-19, and was a one-off fund to last for a period 3 years up until March 2024. It is nearing the end of the fund for the Mental Health Support Fund and Cancer Charities Support Fund, however given that the final round of applications for funding for the Support for Carers Fund closed on 13 March 2023, this particular fund has been extended to March 2025 to ensure that awarded projects can utilise the funds to their full potential. It is therefore important to note that the Department has not withdrawn funding from Community Foundation NI, but rather, these funds are approaching their specified conclusion, as set out at the launch.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department carried out an Equality Impact Assessment on the proposed £30 million cuts to mental health, carers and cancer support that is managed through Community Foundation NI.
answer_text: The Mental Health, Cancer Charities and Carers Support Funds, administered and managed by the Community Foundation NI (CFNI), were established using unspent Covid-19 monies. The funds were one-off, non-recurring funds for a period of three years, from April 2021 to March 2024. The monies allocated to all three funds are now exhausted. Due to the financial situation which the Department continues to face, there is no budget to extend these funds and they will end, as planned. As a result, no Equality Impact Assessment has been undertaken. However, CFNI is to provide a robust evaluation of the outcome of the funds to  the Department before the end of March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will continue to fund the (i) Cancer Charities Support Fund; (ii) the Mental Health Support Fund; and (iii) Support for Carers Fund.
answer_text: The Mental Health, Cancer Charities and Carers Support Funds, administered and managed by the Community Foundation NI (CFNI), were established using Covid-19 funding. The funds were one-off, non-recurring for a period of three years, from April 2021 to March 2024. The monies allocated to all three funds are now exhausted. Due to the financial situation which the Department continues to face, there is no budget to extend these funds and they will end, as planned. However, CFNI will work with my officials in undertaking a robust evaluation of the funds that can support decisions around future funding provision, should additional funding become available. My Department will continue to explore ways to minimise the impact on services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the effectiveness of co-operation between the Education Authority, local schools and children's social services regarding the Operation Encompass policy.
answer_text: To date, approximately 18,000 Operation Encompass notifications have been made by the PSNI.  This sharing of relevant information to the designated staff at the pupil’s education setting allows for the setting to effectively take account of the needs of the child, and any impact that the incident may have had on them; and to provide support to that child, where appropriate.  The effectiveness of Operation Encompass has been further supported by the rollout of training to all statutory education settings.   Ongoing review of the process is important.  The EA is currently analysing responses to a survey undertaken across statutory education settings to better understand the delivery of the Operation Encompass initiative.  Initial findings confirm this has been a positive initiative in supporting children and young people affected by domestic abuse.    My officials will consider the outcome from the EA analysis of the survey and this will determine whether further action is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has reviewed, or intends to review, the policies put in place to deal with the outcomes of Operation Encompass.
answer_text: To date, approximately 18,000 Operation Encompass notifications have been made by the PSNI. Initial findings confirm this has been a positive initiative in supporting children and young people affected by domestic abuse.    My officials will consider the outcome from the EA analysis of the survey and this will determine whether further action is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to ensure the Children's Hospice can offer its full service, particularly at weekends.
answer_text: Senior officials from the Department will be meeting with the Hospice this afternoon to discuss the issues that have been raised in respect of funding and service delivery. Further update will be available before the end of the week.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will adopt a hierarchy of road users to ensure the safety of cyclists.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the timeline for work on the delivery strategy resulting from the York Street Interchange Placemaking and Active Travel Review.
answer_text: I remain committed to the delivery of the strategically important York Street Interchange scheme and the wider benefits it presents.On 11 October 2022 I published the report on the Placemaking and Active Travel Review for the scheme. This has identified a number of potential options to improve placemaking and active travel provision as part of the project.  Further work is required to develop these options and consider factors relating to their potential implementation, including how they could be delivered. Importantly, this will include further engagement with key stakeholders. This work is likely to take until early 2023 to complete and will inform the next steps for the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the report arising from the Place making and Active Travel Review into the York Street Interchange Scheme will be published.
answer_text: The Placemaking and Active Travel Review (PATR) for the York Street Interchange scheme was published on 11 October 2022. This report can be viewed at: http://www.yorkstreetinterchange.com/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will prevent GPs from charging patients to access their medical history for social security purposes.
answer_text: Under the UK General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) individuals have a right of access to their personal information.  This is known as a subject access request (SAR) and includes a patient’s request to access their medical records.  SARs must be processed free of charge and within one month.  If a large amount of information is held by the GP practice, the patient can be asked to clarify the information that would be acceptable to satisfy the SAR.  Whilst the GP practice must bear the costs of providing initial copies of medical records, further copies can be charged for.The Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO) is the regulator for data protectionA patient has the right to complain directly to the ICO if they are dissatisfied with the processing of their request – further information is available here: Make a complaint | ICO.  The ICO can be contacted by phone, email or by post.  Contact information is attached here: Northern Ireland office | ICOGuidance and further information is available on the ICO website via this weblink: Right of access | ICO

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she and the Permanent Secretary of her Department will be attending the meeting, on 14 October 2022, with representatives from programmes currently funded by the European Social Fund and officials from the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department will be represented at the meeting by a senior official.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he plans to deal with the backlog of domiciliary care packages to be provided for those living in the community.
answer_text: You will be aware that on 24 November 2021 I announced a support package of up to £23m for the independent domiciliary and wider social care sector. This additional funding has been provided with the expectation that providers will not only maintain current service levels, but increase capacity and be in a position to reach out particularly into rural areas.  £25 million has been released for 2022/2023 period.In September every year a survey is completed for one week regarding domiciliary care.  In September 2021 there was 7% more domiciliary contact hours provided compared to September 2020.  This equated to 6% increase in people receiving domiciliary care compared to September 2020.There remain ongoing challenges with providing domiciliary care packages due to difficulties with recruitment and retention of domiciliary care staff with particular challenges in rural areas due to high fuel costs. Providers operate rolling recruitment exercises in an attempt to fill workforce vacancies. The Health Trusts have processes in place to risk assess and prioritise those awaiting a package of care.  The local HSC Trusts are promoting Direct Payments as an alternative to traditional packages of care and we would encourage as many people as possible in the community to avail of this option. I will continue to support the domiciliary care sector in their attempts to meet the current demand for packages of care in the community.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when permanent pedestrian crossing will be installed at Frederick Street and Great Patrick Street, given the increase in students and pedestrian traffic required to cross at this junction.
answer_text: My officials advise that the current layout and design of the junction meets all national standards for a junction of this type. Officials have been working together with key stakeholders as part of the Junctions Working Group to determine the long-term arrangement for this junction and the intention would be to deliver this as part of the Department of Communities Belfast Streets Ahead Phase 3 Public Realm project.As a result of concerns raised by Ulster University and others my Department plans to deliver an interim scheme to further enhance the existing pedestrian crossings at the York Street/ Frederick Street/Great Patrick Street junction.  Work is scheduled to commence in October with completion expected in approximately 12-14 weeks and will include:signal controlled crossing of left slip lanes (currently uncontrolled);wider crossing points to facilitate signal controlled crossings and enhance capacity;enhancement of pedestrian footways and larger traffic islands to increase space for waiting pedestrians; andrestaging and re-signalising of the existing junction to maximise opportunities for pedestrians to cross.Officials have been careful to ensure that this work will complement the long-term plans for the junction. Officials will be monitoring the operation of the junction to ensure it meets the needs of all road users, including pedestrians.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at:  dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what engagement has taken place between his Department and Ulster University with regards to the pedestrian crossing at Frederick Street and Great Patrick Street, given the increase in students and pedestrian traffic required to cross at this junction.
answer_text: There has been ongoing engagement between my Department and Ulster University since preliminary discussions took place prior to the submission of the planning application for the new campus in the spring of 2012.The most recent engagement with the Ulster University has been through regular meetings of the Junctions Working Group, a group which is chaired by Department for Communities which brings together a number of key stakeholders, including Ulster University, to discuss development proposals for the three junctions at North/West Belfast: York Street/ Frederick Street/Great Patrick Street; Carrick Hill/ Clifton Street; and Carrick Hill/ Peter’s Hill.Officials have also been engaging with their University counterparts in meetings to discuss their Transport Operational Readiness Plan, the most recent of which took place on Tuesday 6 September 2022.Following on from our engagement with Ulster University, the Department intends to bring forward a scheme to enhance the existing pedestrian crossings at the York Street/ Frederick Street/Great Patrick Street junction with works due to commence early next month.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why a permanent pedestrian crossing has not yet been installed at Frederick Street and Great Patrick Street, given the increase in students and pedestrian traffic required to cross at this junction.
answer_text: My officials advise that the current layout and design of the junction meets all national standards for a junction of this type. Officials have been working together with key stakeholders as part of the Junctions Working Group to determine the long-term arrangement for this junction and the intention would be to deliver this as part of the Department of Communities Belfast Streets Ahead Phase 3 Public Realm project.As a result of concerns raised by Ulster University and others my Department plans to deliver an interim scheme to further enhance the existing pedestrian crossings at the York Street/ Frederick Street/Great Patrick Street junction.  Work is scheduled to commence in October with completion expected in approximately 12-14 weeks and will include:signal controlled crossing of left slip lanes (currently uncontrolled);wider crossing points to facilitate signal controlled crossings and enhance capacity;enhancement of pedestrian footways and larger traffic islands to increase space for waiting pedestrians; andrestaging and re-signalising of the existing junction to maximise opportunities for pedestrians to cross.Officials have been careful to ensure that this work will complement the long-term plans for the junction. Officials will be monitoring the operation of the junction to ensure it meets the needs of all road users, including pedestrians.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at:  dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the plans and engagement with GP surgeries regarding the roll-out of the upcoming autumn booster programme for the COVID-19 vaccine in GP surgeries.
answer_text: The COVID-19 vaccination programme in the UK is guided by the advice and recommendations of the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI), an independent expert advisory committee. JCVI advises that for the 2022 autumn booster programme, the following groups should be offered a COVID-19 booster vaccine:residents in a care home for older adults and staff working in care homes for older adultsfrontline health and social care workersall adults aged 50 years and overpersons aged 5 to 49 years in a clinical risk group,persons aged 5 to 49 years who are household contacts of people with immunosuppressionpersons aged 16 to 49 years who are carersDelivery plans are currently being prepared by the Public Health Agency, in association with the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), Health and Social Care Trusts and Community Pharmacy. SPPG is communicating with General Practice on the plans and start date.My Department is awaiting further advice from both JCVI and the Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) regarding which vaccines are approved for use and who should receive them. Once this has been agreed, final plans will be communicated to all those involved in delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of the cycle lane on the Cavehill Road, including engagement with (i) residents; (ii) local stakeholders; and (iii) local cycling campaign groups.
answer_text: I am very keen to support sustainable transport options that connect communities, tackle the impact of climate change and improve lives.Following initial objections to the construction of Phase 1 of the pop-up Cycle Lane on the Limestone Road, I have asked my officials to review the scheme and initiate a new consultation process with residents, local stakeholders and local cycling campaign groups. This will also include the proposal for Cavehill Road between Antrim Road and Westland Road.My officials advise that they hope to be in a position to commence this new consultation this Autumn.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on connecting the Cavehill Road to the city centre via a safe segregated cycle path.
answer_text: I am very keen to support sustainable transport options that connect communities, tackle the impact of climate change and improve lives.Following initial objections to the construction of Phase 1 of the pop-up Cycle Lane on the Limestone Road, I have asked my officials to review the scheme and initiate a new consultation process with residents, local stakeholders and local cycling campaign groups. This will also include the proposal for Cavehill Road between Antrim Road and Westland Road.My officials advise that they hope to be in a position to commence this new consultation this Autumn.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when will the appointments to the Board of Governors for Hazelwood Integrated Primary School be made.
answer_text: The impact of hedge removal has been considered as part of the overall A5 environmental assessment process. The main two environmental topic areas reported in the Environmental Statement that consider the removal of hedgerows are ‘Landscape and Visual’ (which is presented in Chapter 8 of the Environmental Statement Addendum 2022) and ‘Ecology and Nature Conservation’ (in Chapter 9).  From a landscape perspective, removal of hedgerows is considered in terms of the effect on landscape character and on visual receptors such as properties or those passing through the landscape. The incorporation of appropriate embedded landscape mitigation has formed an integral part of the assessment process in seeking to reduce the overall impact of the project. Such measures include the extensive planting of new boundary hedgerows throughout the scheme as part of an overall landscape strategy. From an ecological perspective, the effect on hedgerow removal was assessed both in terms of loss of vegetation and also how wildlife may be affected.The planting scheme takes into account these impacts in developing mitigation measures, for example the provision of tree, scrub, hedgerows and grassland planting helps mitigate for the loss of suitable bat foraging habitat.These mitigation measures are presented in Figure 5.2 (Environmental Mitigation and Landscaping Strategy) of the Environmental Statement Addendum 2022.
